plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: The actions we take influence our safety and wellbeing

LINES OF INQUIRY: How the human body grows and changes; Ways we can keep our body and mind healthy; Safe environments

KEY CONCEPTS: Function

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Class brainstorm and "body outline" activity?students label known parts on a blank human body template to elicit prior knowledge and curiosities about the human body. Follow-up includes developing wonder questions to guide the inquiry wall.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Daily interactive read-alouds of nonfiction texts (e.g., See Inside Your Body, The Human Body series, AI generated texts), with post-it notes for "new learning" added to the inquiry wall.
- Incursion: Mad About Science ? The Human Body, with hands-on exploration of the eye, respiratory system, and digestive system.
- Mini-lessons on nonfiction text features; students draft, illustrate, and publish their own Informative Book about the body and healthy habits, shared at Student-Led Conferences (SLCs).
- Healthy Habits Inquiry Stations: Nutrition sorting, exercise/heart rate exploration, hygiene role-play, sleep/rest charting.
- Life-Size Body Models: In pairs, students create organs and body systems, then assemble them on life-size body outlines.
- Life-Size Skeletons: Trace each child's body, introduce bones and the skeletal system, and assemble a life-size skeleton.
- Quiz Book for SLCs: Students generate quiz questions about body systems and healthy living, design a quiz book, and present at SLCs.
- Healthy Habits Presentations: Students create a presentation about their healthy habits using a multi-modal element (PowerPoint, booklet, poster, video, or model).
- Buddy Reading during Book Week: Students share their Human Body Book with Year 5s.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Informative writing (My Human Body Book) assessed for nonfiction text features and scientific understanding.
- Quiz Book for Student-Led Conferences as evidence of recall and application.
- Healthy Habits presentations (multi-modal).
- Anecdotal assessment during inquiry stations and hands-on activities.
- Sharing learning with families at SLCs and with Year 5 buddies.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with literacy (read-alouds, informative writing), science (body systems, health), and PE (physical development, safe movement choices).
- Inquiry wall and wonder wall used for student questions and new learning.
- Digital skills: Opportunity for guided use of digital resources or library texts for student-led research.
- Students encouraged to use multi-modal presentations (PowerPoint, video, poster, model).
- Transdisciplinary connections with Music (Vivaldi's Four Seasons for composition), Visual Art (not specified), and PE (movement, teamwork, wellbeing).
- Action opportunities: Making healthy choices, reflecting on routines, presenting to peers and families.
- Teacher reflection notes suggest more structured formative checkpoints and more student-led research in future iterations.

ci: Artistic appreciation and expressions inspire our imagination and creativity.
lo: Forms of expression; Expressing thoughts and ideas; Understanding and appreciating art

s.d: Students photograph their own artwork or a favourite classroom art piece, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the image and record a short voiceover explaining the inspiration, emotions, or story behind the piece. This brings visual art to life and lets students practice artistic reflection and digital storytelling in a playful, highly visual way.

Each student creates a digital 'Imagination Journal' using Book Creator, combining their own artwork, poetry, and voice recordings. They can add photos of their creative work, type or dictate short reflections, and use drawing tools to illustrate their ideas, celebrating diverse forms of expression and building a multimedia portfolio.

Students design digital posters or collages celebrating different forms of artistic expression (art, music, poetry, dance) using Canva's templates and drag-and-drop features. They can use the 'Remove Background' tool to insert themselves into famous artworks or creative scenes, fostering appreciation and playful engagement with art history.

Students use the iPad camera to document their creative process (e.g., painting, sculpting, performing), then upload photos or short videos to Seesaw. They add voice comments to explain their choices and feelings, creating a digital gallery that celebrates imagination and allows for peer and family feedback.

In small groups, students use Stop Motion Studio to create short stop-motion animations that tell a story or express an emotion using clay, paper, or classroom objects. This hands-on digital project encourages teamwork, fine motor skills, and creative storytelling through visual media.

plannerText: The unit 'How We Express Ourselves' explores artistic appreciation and expression, focusing on how imagination and creativity are inspired through diverse forms such as art, poetry, and music. Students engage in hands-on art making, poetry writing, music exploration, and digital reflection, celebrating their own creativity and learning to appreciate the creative works of others.
humanVerifiedResetReason: Suggestion edited, swapped, regenerated or changed after human verification
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Artistic appreciation and expressions inspire our imagination and creativity

LINES OF INQUIRY: Forms of expression; Expressing thoughts and ideas; Understanding and appreciating other's creativity and imagination

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, Perspective, Communication

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Bus Stop Exploration in two classrooms. In Jane's room: students explored books from the library collection and artefacts such as art pieces, photos, and instruments. In Alex's room: students discussed what creativity means to them and watched ClickView short clips linked to artistic expression. Students completed a "Unit of Inquiry Detective" template and a See, Think, Wonder sheet (though this was found to be challenging for Year 2 learners). There was also an "I am poem" activity, where students typed their poem in Book Creator and published an artwork with watercolour paint.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Poetry writing in pairs
- Learner Profile Caring Detective activities
- Essential Agreement reflections
- "Size of the problem" and "Zones of Regulation" wellbeing activities
- "Beautiful Oops" exploration
- Exploring Art and Music forms as 'data collectors' using frayer models, tables, and labelled diagrams
- Listening to different musical instruments and discussing subject-specific vocabulary
- Creating pet portraits combining knowledge of various art styles and skills learnt in Art lessons with Popi Roumeliotous
- Producing several pieces of Art and exploring composition through Music apps such as Chrome Music Lab and Blob Orchestra
- Be Balanced choice boards (guided meditations, drawing tutorials, reading about the Arts, listening to music, watching dance performances)
- Haiku chalk poems in the Music Courtyard
- Year 2 class concerts

ASSESSMENT: 
- Ongoing production of Art pieces
- Exploration of composition through Music apps
- Haiku chalk poems published in the Music Courtyard
- Year 2 class concerts
- Home learning project provides choice and agency
- Students co-constructed learning goals and success criteria, co-created word walls, and were involved in deciding how to display artworks and share learning on Seesaw

TEACHER NOTES: 
- The unit was highly transdisciplinary, with connections to Art, Music, Maths, and Writing.
- Students wrote poems in response to artworks and music, explored 2D and 3D shapes through art, and applied knowledge from Specialist classes.
- Students brought in materials and artefacts from home, including books connected to the Learner Profile and artworks made outside of school.
- The central idea was found to be hard for students to access and could benefit from simplification.
- Students found thinking routines challenging and needed teacher modelling and exposure.
- Students need support with organisational thinking skills and how to collect/record data; sketch noting was introduced as an option.
- Opportunities for greater collaboration with Specialist teachers are recommended for future iterations.
- Technology integration included Book Creator for poetry/art publishing, Chrome Music Lab, Blob Orchestra, and Seesaw for sharing learning.
- Students were highly engaged, especially with hands-on activities and sharing their work with the community.

ci: Marketplaces reflect the needs and wants of communities.
lo: Needs and wants; The changing needs of communities; The design process

s.d: Students photograph their handmade marketplace products or prototypes, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the objects and 'pitch' their product, explaining how it meets a specific need or want in the community. This brings empathy and persuasive communication into the design thinking process in a playful, visual way.

Learners design persuasive posters for their marketplace products using Canva's highly visual templates and 'Remove Background' feature. They can add photos of their prototypes, icons, and speech bubbles to clearly communicate the difference between needs and wants.

Students create a digital class book documenting the design thinking journey: identifying community needs, brainstorming, prototyping, and reflecting on feedback. Each page can feature photos, voice recordings, and drawings, making the process accessible and engaging for Year 2.

Students record short video reflections or photo journals as they test and improve their product designs. Teachers and peers can leave feedback using Seesaw's commenting tools, supporting the 'Reflective' learner profile and iterative improvement.

Students use the iPad Camera to document their product prototypes and the steps of the design process. These images can be used in digital portfolios, posters, or presentations, supporting visual storytelling and evidence of learning.

plannerText: This unit explores how marketplaces reflect the needs and wants of communities, with a strong focus on the design thinking process. Students identify and empathise with real needs in their community, brainstorm and prototype solutions, and iterate based on feedback. Activities include needs vs wants games, home connections, vocabulary building, persuasive poster creation, and a culminating project where students design and showcase a product that addresses a community need.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Marketplaces reflects the needs and wants of communities. *Please note that this unit will be more focused on needs, wants using the 'design thinking' process to meet the needs of the community rather than selling items in marketplaces in 2025.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Needs and wants; The changing needs of communities; The design process

KEY CONCEPTS: Connection; Responsibility; Function

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: 
- Stranded On An Island Provocation
- HWOO- Wants vs Needs Provocation (Year 2.pptx)
- Classroom Needs and Wants Poll: Students vote on whether items (e.g., food, computer games, water, toys) are a "Need" or a "Want" and discuss their choices.
- Learners bring two objects from home that they believe are a want or a need; use Think, Feel, Care thinking routine; discuss poverty and where it is seen in the community.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS:
- Needs vs. Wants Sorting Game: Using pictures or flashcards, students sort items into "Need" or "Want" piles and discuss.
- Home learning: Visit a market, take photos of 4 items you may need and want.
- Build a Vocabulary Wall Together: Develop shared definitions and examples for needs, wants, basic needs, and luxury wants.
- "I Want, I Need" Song or Chant: Create and sing a chant about needs and wants.
- Needs and Wants Role-Play: In pairs or groups, students role-play scenarios (e.g., camping trip) and separate items into needs and wants.
- Healthy Eating (Needs for Health): Sort foods into "Healthy" and "Unhealthy" categories and create a poster.
- Need or Want Collage: Cut out magazine pictures and sort into needs and wants on a poster.
- Venn diagram and story retell: Compare lives of different people/characters using mentor texts (Safe Same But Different, Those Shoes, Maddi's Fridge, Beatrice's Goat).
- Design Thinking Process: Prototyping, Tinker Time in the Maker Space, possible visit to a Design Class.
- Students design a product that responds to the needs of the immediate community (e.g., peers, younger students, elderly relatives).
- Excursion: Walk to Sacred Heart Mission in Glen Huntly Road to explore its purpose and take action.

ASSESSMENT:
- Evidence of learning includes participation in discussions, sorting activities, collages, role-plays, and the design and prototyping of a product to meet community needs.
- Sharing learning at a Share Fair showcasing student innovations/products.

TEACHER NOTES:
- Opportunity to connect to the SDG Zero Poverty.
- Strong focus on the design thinking process: empathising, creating, testing, and improving products to meet community needs.
- Local connection with Sacred Heart Mission and action opportunities.
- Maths integration: Addition and subtraction number stories.
- English integration: Retelling stories, using Venn diagrams, persuasive posters.
- Digital skills: Any teacher can connect digital tools to the unit.
- Tinker Time and Maker Space are key practical components.
- Possibility to connect to the text The Lost Thing by Shaun Tan.
- Transdisciplinary connections are encouraged but some specialist areas (Music, Visual Art, PE, Dance & Drama, Languages) are to be completed by respective teachers.
- Action opportunities include participation, advocacy, and social justice.
- Integration notes: Use of Seesaw for sharing, vocabulary wall, and collaborative activities.

ci: Forces and motion are all around us.
lo: Forces around us; Scientific laws of motion; Innovations in technology

s.d: Students use the iPad camera to capture sequences of objects in motion (e.g., toy cars, balls, or playground equipment) and create stop-motion animations in Stop Motion Studio. They can visually demonstrate different types of forces (push, pull, gravity, friction) and narrate what is happening in each frame, making the invisible visible in a highly creative way.

Students design digital posters or infographics illustrating different forces and simple machines found in their school playground. Using Canva's 'Remove Background' feature, they can insert themselves into action photos, label forces at play, and create vibrant, visual explanations of motion.

Students document their experiments and discoveries about forces and motion by creating interactive digital books. They can add photos, voice recordings, and drawings to explain concepts like friction, gravity, and simple machines, making their learning journey accessible and engaging for all.

Students record short video reflections or demonstrations of force experiments (e.g., rolling objects down ramps, testing friction on different surfaces) and share their thinking with peers and families. Teachers can prompt students to use key vocabulary and label forces in their videos.

Students watch curated short videos about forces, motion, and simple machines, then respond with their own video explanations or digital drawings showing what they learned. Teachers can use ClickView's interactive features to pause and annotate videos, prompting students to predict outcomes or identify forces in action.

plannerText: The Year 2 'How the World Works' unit explores forces and motion through hands-on experiments, playground investigations, and the study of simple machines. Students engage in activities such as classifying pushes and pulls, experimenting with ramps and friction, exploring magnetism and gravity, and culminating in building Rube Goldberg machines to demonstrate their understanding of forces in action.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Forces and motion are all around us

LINES OF INQUIRY: Forces around us; Scientific laws of motion; Innovations in technology

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, Function, Connection

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Year 2 will go on an excursion to Twisted Science for a provocation to the HTWW unit. In the Maker Space, students will immerse themselves with wooden simple machines, cars and ramps, and building with Lego/screw sets. They will also use illustrations from "Ada Twist Scientist" for a graffiti wall brainstorm of key words connected to being a scientist.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Excursion to Twisted Science for hands-on experiments about forces.
- Maker Space immersion with wooden simple machines, cars and ramps, Lego/screw sets.
- Forces workshops and literacy connection through a Forces Poem.
- Tinkering with simple machines and brainstorming main features using 3D models.
- Push and Pull: Tuning in video, walk around the school to classify objects as push or pull, take photos and label.
- Magnetism Workshop: Testing items for magnetism, floating magnet experiment, magnet maze creation.
- Gravity: Dripping paint art with different viscosities, object drop using slow-mo camera on iPad.
- Friction: Playground investigation, friction experiments with book/lego ramp and toy car, testing wheel and axle with weights and skateboards.
- Wedge and inclined plane: Experiments with ramps and play dough, classifying wedges.
- Screw and lever: Linked Google presentations.
- Non-fiction text features: Use mentor texts, Seesaw task, writing information reports on simple machines.
- Forces Fact Collecting: Research skills, data chart, videos, Get Epic books, body actions for friction, gravity, push, pull.
- Rube Goldberg Machines: Students construct machines as a summative task, demonstrating understanding of simple machines and forces.
- Reflection on "Ada Twist Scientist" and connection to the Learner Profile.
- Sharing learning at student-led conferences, modeling experiments and sharing journals.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Students experiment with machines (push, pull, friction, magnet, gravity) and analyze results.
- Evidence: Students use results to make conclusions about different forces.
- Rube Goldberg Machine building as a summative task, demonstrating understanding through planning, design, labeling forces, and constructing with a partner.
- Sharing journals and modeling experiments at student-led conferences.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with Maths (measurement, time, data collection), English (non-fiction text features, information reports, vocabulary building), Science (forces, energy, invention, materials), PSPE (movement, balance, coordination, physical forces), and Music (digital composition project with First Nations perspectives).
- Opportunities for digital skills: Use of iPads for slow-mo video, Seesaw for tasks, Book Creator for publishing, and digital sound resources in Music.
- Action opportunities: Students bring materials from home, model experiments for families, and connect learning to local playgrounds and community.
- Transdisciplinary connections are emphasized, with explicit links to PE, Music, and Library.
- Recommendations for future: Incorporate more visual tools (diagrams, slow-motion video) and scaffold digital sound use in Music.
- Highly engaging unit with strong student agency, hands-on learning, and language development.

ci: Taking care of our Earth starts with the choices we make.
lo: Earth's resources; How our actions affect the environment; Ways we can care for our planet

s.d: Students use Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app to interact with 3D models of Earth's resources, pollution, and conservation efforts. They can explore virtual composting, recycling, and water cycles, then create their own digital dioramas to demonstrate how everyday choices impact the planet. This hands-on AR experience makes abstract environmental concepts tangible and memorable for Year 2 learners.

Students design persuasive posters or infographics using Canva's visual templates and 'Remove Background' feature. They create campaigns encouraging their peers and families to adopt sustainable habits, such as reducing lunchbox waste or saving water, directly linking to the unit's focus on choices and environmental care.

Learners create a collaborative digital book documenting their week-long lunchbox waste audit. Each student adds a page with photos (taken using the iPad Camera), drawings, and voice recordings explaining how their choices reduced waste, reinforcing the connection between personal action and environmental impact.

The class builds a shared Padlet wall to collect and categorize examples of Earth-friendly actions at home and school. Students post photos, drawings, or short videos, then comment on each other's contributions, fostering a sense of community responsibility and idea-sharing around caring for the planet.

Teachers curate a playlist of short, age-appropriate videos on topics like recycling, composting, and water conservation. Students watch and then complete a visual reflection task, such as drawing or sequencing images, to show how the choices featured in the videos help care for Earth.

plannerText: The unit 'Sharing the Planet' explores how taking care of our Earth starts with the choices we make. Students investigate Earth's resources, how actions affect the environment, and practical ways to care for the planet, including waste audits, persuasive campaigns, and hands-on action projects like no-waste lunches and community clean-ups.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Taking care of our Earth starts with the choices we make

LINES OF INQUIRY: Earth's resources; How our actions affect the environment; Ways we can care for our Earth

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, Causation, Change

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: 
CEREs- Towards Zero Waste: Students will go on an excursion to CERES (October 14) as a provocation to the unit. Additional provocations include a picture story book provocation and a lunchbox waste collection across a week, sorting food waste for composting, recyclables, and general waste. Students will also listen to the Jack Johnson "3Rs" song.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Excursion to CERES focused on waste and sustainability.
- Picture story book provocation.
- Lunchbox waste audit and sorting (compost, recycle, general waste).
- Investigation of Earth's resources (plants, animals, water, trees, rocks, soil, sunlight, wind, air, human affection).
- Waste management learning (focus on 5Rs: reduce, reuse, recycle, rot, refuse).
- Air and water pollution investigations.
- Clean energy exploration.
- Use of Junior Landcare Learning Centre resources.
- Personalised inquiry project: students take action to care for the environment.
- Writing a persuasive speech to convince school leaders to make a positive change in the school environment.
- Walking excursion to care for the environment.
- Nude food celebration day and no waste lunch (food tech experience).
- Creation of a waste timeline showing how long different objects take to break down.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Sharing persuasive team speeches with the parent community.
- Personalised inquiry project presentations.
- Creation and sharing of a waste timeline.
- Evidence of learning through participation in excursions, speeches, and action projects.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- PSPE: While not directly connected, transdisciplinary thinking is supported during the Athletics unit by focusing on Responsibility and Connection. Students reflect on how their actions demonstrate personal responsibility and connection to others during group events.
- Opportunities for digital integration include documenting waste audits, persuasive speeches, and inquiry projects using digital tools (not specified which).
- Action opportunities are embedded through excursions, inquiry projects, and community sharing.
- Transdisciplinary connections are possible with Science (resources, pollution), English (persuasive writing), and PSPE (responsibility, fairness).
- Integration with Wellbeing curriculum is possible but not specified.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Landmarks and places of significance tell stories about our history.
lo: The significance of places to different people and cultures; The connection between landmarks and identity; How landmarks and symbols represent our history

s.d: Students use Podcast Equipment to conduct and record short interviews with family members or community elders about local landmarks and their stories. They produce a podcast episode that captures personal memories, historical facts, and cultural significance, bringing to life the voices connected to places around Elsternwick. This audio collection helps preserve and share diverse perspectives on how landmarks tell stories about history.

Students take photos of local landmarks during a campus or neighbourhood walk using the iPad Camera app, then upload them to Seesaw. They create interactive story maps by adding voice recordings and digital drawings to explain why each place is significant and what stories it tells, making connections between place and history.

Students use PicCollage to create a vibrant collage of images, drawings, and maps representing a landmark's features and surroundings. They then use ChatterPix Kids to animate a photo of the landmark, recording a short story or fun fact from the landmark's 'perspective' to bring its history to life.

Students research a local Indigenous landmark and, using Book Creator, craft a digital picture book retelling its Dreaming story or origin. They combine their own illustrations, audio narration, and simple text, directly linking to the exploration of how places are remembered and stories are preserved.

Students watch a Clickview documentary segment about a famous Victorian landmark, then use Field Guide to Victoria to investigate its flora, fauna, and cultural stories. They create a short video or illustrated fact sheet summarising what makes the place unique and why it is important to different groups.

plannerText: The unit 'Where We Are in Place and Time' explores how landmarks and places of significance tell stories about our history. Students investigate the meaning of significant places for different people and cultures, make connections between place and story, and learn about ways we preserve and remember historical sites through mapping, storytelling, and creative projects.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Landmarks and places of significance tell stories about our history.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The significance of places to different people and cultures; The connection between place and story; Ways we preserve and remember historical sites

KEY CONCEPTS: Function, Change, Connection

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Picture story book provocation with mentor text images from Amelia Lester's "Are we there yet?" and other images of famous landmarks. Students create 'graffiti' walls to share their thinking. Additional provocations include significant places photo prompts on Seesaw and a Welcome to Country (picture story book and video), as well as a smoking ceremony and campus walking tour to identify landmarks and monuments.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Holiday photo sharing about landmarks seen during holidays
- Elsternwick walking excursion to local landmarks and monuments
- Campus walking tour to identify community landmarks (MLC, Wesley, Cato, Prest Room artefacts)
- Social justice action: Students develop an Acknowledgement of Country for each class and perform at Assembly
- Mapping activities: Creating maps of Australia showing states, territories, capital cities, natural and built landmarks
- Daily virtual 'travel' to different Australian landmarks using mentor texts and Go Jetters episodes
- Researching and collecting data on significant places using texts like "Are We There Yet?" and "The Amazing Australian Road Trip"
- Dreaming stories exploration: Reading, retelling, and creating Dreamtime stories and artworks using Aboriginal symbols
- Creating a mini-dictionary of Dreaming key terms
- Exploring the connection between The Dreaming and landmarks, and writing their own Dreaming stories
- Art activity in the style of Greta Laundy depicting Australian landforms
- Reconciliation Week activities and publishing of Acknowledgement of Country
- Drama Toolbox Incursion: Tiddalik the Frog
- Use of Bee Bots and Cubetos for mapping and giving directions in Maths
- Library exploration of 'The Big' landmarks and related stories

ASSESSMENT: 
- Unit of inquiry wall display in the Year 2 corridor showcasing student learning
- Acknowledgement of Country performed at Assembly by Year 2 Purple
- Research about landmarks shared by Year 2 Gold at Assembly
- Data collection tables and research sheets on significant places
- Artistic and writing tasks (Dreamtime stories, map symbols, artworks)
- Cloudy, Misty, Sunny questions wall for ongoing formative assessment

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong transdisciplinary connections, especially with Music (soundscape creation and storytelling), Dance & Drama (Uluru movement activities), and Library (books about place and landmarks)
- Use of digital tools: Seesaw for photo prompts, mapping activities, and student reflections; ClickView for videos; Epic Books for research
- Specialist collaboration day at the start of term supported planning
- Opportunities for students to co-create learning spaces (word walls, displays), bring in artefacts from home, and engage in teamwork challenges in the Maker Space
- Recommendations for future: Strengthen research/data collection skills, connect to creation stories in Chinese, and continue modelled support for fact collecting
- The timing of the unit aligns well with Reconciliation Week and connects with Year 5 inquiry into significant Indigenous Australians
- Authentic teaching of mapping and giving directions outcomes from the Australian Curriculum through this unit

ci: Our relationships with others impact our lives.
lo: Importance of relationships; Developing and maintaining healthy relationships; The impact of relationships on wellbeing

s.d: Students take photos of themselves with friends or family and use ChatterPix Kids to animate the images, recording short messages about what makes each relationship special and how it impacts their lives. This creates a playful, visual way to reflect on the diversity and importance of their relationships.

Students collaboratively create a digital class book where each page is dedicated to a different type of relationship (friendship, family, classmates, teachers). They add photos, drawings, and voice recordings to share stories about how these relationships help them grow and feel connected.

Using Canva's visual templates, students design 'Relationship Recipe' posters that illustrate the ingredients of a healthy relationship (e.g., kindness, listening, sharing). They use the 'Remove Background' feature to add their own photos and decorate with stickers and icons.

Students document acts of kindness and positive interactions throughout the week by taking photos or drawing pictures in Seesaw. They record voice reflections explaining how these moments made them or others feel, building a digital portfolio of relationship-building actions.

In small groups, students film short skits or role-plays that show how to solve common friendship challenges or celebrate positive relationships. They edit their videos in iMovie, adding titles and music, and share them with the class to spark discussion about empathy and problem-solving.

plannerText: The unit 'Who We Are' explores how relationships with others impact students' lives. Key activities include learning about the importance of relationships, developing and maintaining healthy connections, and reflecting on how relationships can change over time. Students engage in social-emotional learning, toolbox strategies for managing emotions, and collaborative discussions about empathy, kindness, and teamwork.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Our relationships with others impact our lives.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Importance of relationships; Developing and maintaining healthy relationships; The changeable nature of relationships

KEY CONCEPTS: Connection, change, responsibility

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Read alouds such as "What if everyone did that" and "Friendship is a see-saw." Throughout the learning to learn phase, Year 2 learners reviewed Zones of Regulation, Size of the Problem, Identifying Emotions, and Growth Mindset.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Toolbox Education incursion (March 12): Students met Emma from Toolbox Education, who used the metaphor of bodies being like bottles of coke that can get shaken up, and introduced five calming tools (5 senses, move your body, body scan, think of a happy place, mindful breathing).
- Character strengths poster: Exploring what makes a great friend.
- "How to apologize" mentor text for a week-long focus on saying sorry.
- UR Strong Engagements: Friendology program focusing on friendship fires and the talk-it-out strategy.
- After recess and lunchtime, class meetings to discuss what went well, problems to solve together, and which toolbox tools could be used.
- Zones of regulation check-ins.

ASSESSMENT: Portfolio task in Physical Education and Health, where students demonstrate fundamental movement skills in games and reflect on ways they cooperate and show fairness. Health lessons highlight how good choices (like safe play and respectful friendships) affect everyday life. Teacher notes mention the portfolio task effectively captured how students applied movement skills and teamwork in real situations.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- PSPE: Focus on students' sense of identity and wellbeing, respectful relationships, teamwork, and understanding strengths. Health lessons highlight the impact of good choices on daily life.
- Music: Planned to link to the unit by exploring relationships and connections through melody and accompaniment, but only touched on briefly due to a busy term.
- Teachers suggest adding a quick self-assessment tool after each activity in the future to help students see their progress more clearly.
- The learning experiences reinforced students' sense of self and responsibility toward one another.
- No specific digital tools or technology integration mentioned, but opportunities exist for digital portfolios, self-assessment tools, and collaborative documentation of learning.

ci: Celebrations and traditions shape the culture of a community.
lo: The ways personal and cultural events are celebrated; Our appreciation of the diversity of cultures; How celebrations shape community identity

s.d: Students photograph a family or cultural artefact, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the object and record a short story about its significance in their celebration or tradition. This brings personal and cultural narratives to life, allowing students to share their heritage in a creative, voice-driven way.

Students design interactive digital posters or infographics that showcase a celebration or tradition from their own or another culture. They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to layer themselves into traditional scenes, add symbols, and include QR codes linking to audio or video explanations.

Students collaboratively create a digital class book, with each student contributing a page about a celebration or tradition important to their family. Pages can include photos, drawings, audio recordings, and text, celebrating the diversity within the classroom community.

Students work in pairs to script and record a short podcast episode interviewing each other about a family tradition or celebration, exploring its meaning and how it shapes their identity. Episodes can be shared with the school community to foster intercultural understanding.

s.t: Minecraft: Peter Is Here Ai For Cultural Heritage
s.d: Students explore how technology can be used to celebrate and protect cultural heritage by building virtual representations of significant celebrations or traditions in Minecraft. They reflect on why it is important to preserve these practices and how communities can work together to do so.

plannerText: Students investigate how celebrations and traditions shape the culture of a community by exploring personal and cultural events, appreciating diversity, and considering what it means to be a global citizen. Activities include mind mapping traditions, researching family backgrounds, comparing cultural practices, and presenting findings through creative projects and performances.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Celebrations and traditions shape the culture of a community.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The ways personal and cultural events are celebrated; Our appreciation of the diversity all around us; What it means to be a global citizen

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, Connection

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: 
- Provocation: Unpacking perspective?students are shown a LEGO creation from different perspectives and asked to draw the creation from their angle, then discuss why their drawings differ. 
- Students are asked for home learning to investigate their families' cultural background and present their learning to the group.
- Birthday Display Boards with months written in different languages and each student's photo and name on the wall outside Year 2-4 to spark wonder and curiosity.
- Tune in: Students find out what country their ancestors came from, use a world map to attach string from their name tag to places of origin, and discuss visible parts of their culture in the community.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS:
- Mind maps about traditions and cultural events.
- Sorting activity: Place celebrations and commemorations in the correct place.
- ANZAC Day activities: Think See Wonder with photos, fact hunt, making ANZAC biscuits, reading "Lest We Forget."
- Group research and poster creation on aspects of Australian culture (food, flags, celebrations, sports, flora/fauna, tourist destinations, etc.), followed by a gallery walk and presentations.
- Reading and discussing books: My Two Blankets, Same Same but Different, Advance Australia Fair, Are We There Yet?, I am Australian Too.
- LEGO perspective activity and discussion.
- Researching significant cultural events, customs, and food from students' own cultures (drawing flags, identifying symbols, traditional dances, stories, foods, etc.).
- Personal Project: Students research and present on their cultural identity, comparing with another student from a different culture.
- Music: Creating a verse for the song "I am Australian" to reflect their own story.
- Art: Creating a quilt square with a symbol or artefact from their culture.
- Assembly item: Singing their verse of "I am Australian" with slides of food, flags, dance, and a photo of the quilt.
- Student Led Conferences and digital portfolio opportunities.

ASSESSMENT:
- Student presentations on features of their cultural identity (can include artefacts, dances, songs, PowerPoint, etc.).
- Seesaw tasks: Uploading posters and reflections.
- Assembly performance of "I am Australian" with student-created verses and visual displays.
- Student Led Conferences.
- Digital portfolio entries (Seesaw).
- Self-reflection on ATL goals (communication and research skills) using "two stars and a wish."

TEACHER NOTES:
- Strong collaboration with specialists (Music, Art, Library, Chinese, PSPE) to provide transdisciplinary connections.
- Digital skills focus: Learning to use PowerPoint for presentations; future plans to use Canva for more agency.
- Library: Unpacking Australian songs from First Nations perspectives; unpacking Ned Kelly for "We are Australian."
- Chinese: Students share personal stories of family migration, language portraits, and explore migration topics.
- PSPE: Identity focus?students explore how identity evolves from cultural influences and membership in different groups.
- Emphasis on student agency: Students choose how to present their projects and set ATL goals.
- Reflection and feedback: Students reflect on their learning goals and receive peer feedback.
- Assembly and community sharing: Learning is shared with the wider school community through performances and displays.
- The unit is framed by the theme "We are one, we are many," using the song "I am Australian" as a central anchor.
- Next steps: Continue to build student agency with digital tools and deepen transdisciplinary links in future units.

ci: The rights and responsibilities of citizens are determined by the rules and laws of a community.
lo: The differences between rules and laws; People responsible for enforcing rules and laws; Rights and responsibilities of citizens; The consequences when rules, laws and rights are not met

s.d: Students design and script interactive chatbots using Wise Discussion Chatbots to simulate real-life scenarios where citizens must make decisions about rules, laws, and rights. For example, students could create a chatbot that helps a new student understand the difference between school rules and laws, or one that guides users through the consequences of breaking a rule versus a law. This approach fosters critical thinking and digital citizenship in a highly engaging, student-driven way.

Students use Canva to create persuasive digital posters or infographics advocating for a new school rule or law, clearly outlining the rights and responsibilities it would support. They can include visual elements, icons, and short persuasive texts, then present and justify their proposals to the class, connecting directly to the central idea of how rules and laws shape communities.

Students collaboratively author a digital book in Book Creator that explores the journey of a fictional citizen navigating different rules and laws at home, school, and in the wider community. Each student can contribute a page, using images, voice recordings, and text to show the consequences of following or breaking rules and laws.

Students design and distribute a Microsoft Forms survey to gather data from peers, teachers, or families about which rules or laws they believe are most important in their community. Students then analyse the results and present their findings, discussing trends and reflecting on the diversity of perspectives regarding rights and responsibilities.

Students explore the 'Active Citizen' Minecraft Education lesson (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/active-citizen), where they learn about Nobel Peace Laureates and how individuals have shaped laws and rights in their communities. Students can build virtual monuments or scenarios representing key moments of advocacy, then reflect on how these actions connect to their own rights and responsibilities.

plannerText: This unit focuses on the differences between rules and laws, the people responsible for enforcing them, and the rights and responsibilities of citizens. Students explore the consequences when rules, laws, and rights are not met, using real-world examples and collaborative activities to deepen their understanding of how communities are organised.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: The rights and responsibilities of citizens are determined by the rules and laws within that society

LINES OF INQUIRY: The differences between rules and laws; People responsible for enforcing rules and laws; Our rights and responsibilities as citizens; The consequences when rules, laws and rights are not met

KEY CONCEPTS: Function - students will understand how laws and rules work; Responsibility - students will understand who is responsible for making and enforcing rules and laws; Causation - students will understand that when rules and laws are not met there are consequences

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Students were put into partners and provided with a pack of cards. Each group created their own card game. Partners then teamed up with another set of partners and they had time to teach each other the games they had created. Students in their groups reflected on what went well and what did not work so well. As a class, record reflections and compare with other groups to see if there were similar findings. Discuss what the common element that helped or hindered the enjoyment of the games. (Slides 5?7 PowerPoint)

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Brainstorming and defining rules, laws, and rights; students provide examples and definitions (Slide 11 - PowerPoint)
- Reading and responding to texts about rules and laws (e.g., Get Epic: "Why do we need rules and laws", "Obeying laws")
- Exploring the importance of making decisions democratically and the role of local government
- Discussing the differences between rules and laws, including experiences of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples
- Maths connection: Measurement and place value ? exploring what would happen without formal systems (linking to rules for order)
- English: Writing persuasive texts on topics such as school rules or new laws; practising NAPLAN writing
- Social Studies: Investigating who makes rules, why rules are important, and consequences of not following them
- Group and class discussions about responsibility, consequences, and rights
- Student questions and wonderings about laws, rights, and consequences are collected and discussed

ASSESSMENT: Not specified.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Transdisciplinary connections include links to Maths (systems/order), English (persuasive writing), Social Studies (democracy, government, rights), and Wellbeing.
- Opportunities for student agency through co-constructed games, group reflection, and discussion.
- Digital skills and resources are mentioned but not specified for this unit.
- Wellbeing and identity links are possible through discussions of rights and responsibilities.
- No specific technology integration is documented, but opportunities exist for digital presentations, collaborative documents, or online research about laws and rights.
- Some specialist and integration sections (Music, Visual Art, Dance & Drama, Languages) are marked for completion by other teachers and are not specified in the current planner.

ci: Humans use their understanding of scientific principles to take action.
lo: The work of scientists; The process of learning; How science brings about new understandings and changes

s.d: Students design interactive 3D models of molecules or scientific principles (like states of matter or heat transfer) using Merge Cubes and the iPad. They record short video explanations, then classmates use the Merge Cube to explore each other's models in augmented reality, deepening understanding of abstract science concepts through hands-on digital manipulation.

Students create visually engaging infographics that explain the scientific method or showcase the biography of a scientist who took action based on scientific understanding. They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to add themselves as 'scientists in action' to their posters, making the learning personal and creative.

Students collaboratively author a digital book documenting their science experiments, including photos (taken with the iPad camera), diagrams, data tables, and reflections. Each student contributes a page explaining a scientific principle and how it can be used to solve a real-world problem.

Students script and record short podcasts interviewing 'famous scientists' (role-played by classmates) about how their discoveries led to real-world action. They edit and publish these podcasts, sharing them with the school community to inspire others to use science for positive change.

s.t: Minecraft: Filtering Water On The Iss
s.d: Students explore how scientific understanding is used to solve real-world problems by building and testing water filtration systems in Minecraft Education. They compare their Minecraft designs to real ISS filtration systems and reflect on how humans use science to take action. https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/filtering-water-on-the-iss

plannerText: This unit engages Year 3 students in exploring how humans use scientific principles to take action, focusing on the work of scientists, the process of learning, and how science brings about new understandings. Students conduct hands-on experiments about heat transfer and states of matter, research scientists, write biographies, create infographics, and complete open-ended science fair projects that connect learning to real-world action and sustainability.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Humans use their understanding of scientific principles to take action.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The work of scientists; The process of learning; How science brings about new understandings

KEY CONCEPTS: Causation, connection, change

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: 
Students complete a 'What is a scientist?' task: they draw a scientist, use a mind map to record what scientists do and what learner profile attributes scientists need to be successful, and list different types of sciences they know about. They discuss why we need scientists and what the world would be like without them. Students also move around 7 provocation stations (e.g., observing ice melting, food colouring in water, effervescent tablet in water, bicarb and vinegar reaction, toy with light and sound, pinwheel movement, and separating sand and water) and answer questions at each station. They are asked to draw what they think a scientist looks like, then look in a mirror to see a scientist.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Guided and shared reading experiences about scientists and their impact.
- Watching videos of scientists and discussing their discoveries.
- Writing up a variety of experiments and scientific methods.
- Writing a biography of a scientist of choice.
- Rotations of science cards to explore concepts in physical, chemical, biological, and earth sciences.
- Sorting activities to identify types of science and scientists.
- Science Fair: Students identify their passions and interests, research a topic, and create a project.
- Experiments on energy transference, states of matter, conduction, convection, and radiation (e.g., melting chocolate, pinwheel, heat transfer with spoons).
- Building models of molecules using kits and marshmallows.
- Observing and recording changes in states of matter (melting, freezing, evaporation, etc.).
- Investigating conductors and insulators of heat.
- Excursion to Twisted Science (Week 3 ? Tuesday 30th July).
- Creating infographics for scientist biographies using Canva.
- Creating graphs and tables online.
- Art: Creating a vessel from clay, exploring the process and origins of clay.
- PE: Experimenting with movement, technique, and strategy, linking to scientific thinking.
- Drama: Investigating consumerism and waste through environmental lens and Epic Theatre conventions.
- Chinese: Exploring states of matter and heat transfer via songs, texts, and experiments.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Science Fair project (physical and digital product, e.g., model, experiment, diorama, poster, PowerPoint, infographic).
- Biography of a chosen scientist presented as an infographic on Canva (Portfolio Task).
- Experiments documented in science journals.
- Presentations of science fair projects to school and parent community.
- Lunchbox data collection and analysis (maths integration).
- Student reflections using two stars and a wish.
- Ongoing formative assessment through KWL charts, diagrams, and experiment write-ups.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with Australian Curriculum (maths, English, science, history).
- Transdisciplinary connections: Art (clay and process), PE (movement and scientific thinking), Drama (environmental issues), Chinese (science vocabulary and experiments), Digital Skills (Canva, online graphs/tables).
- Action opportunities: Students encouraged to consider the impact of scientists on society and the environment, and to take action related to UN Sustainable Development Goal 12 (responsible consumption).
- Technology integration: Use of Seesaw for tasks and reflections, Canva for infographics, digital tools for data collection and presentation, iPads for research and video viewing.
- Science wall and journals used for ongoing vocabulary and concept development.
- Opportunities for student agency in choosing science fair topics and methods of presentation.
- Excursion and guest speakers (Twisted Science) to support hands-on learning.
- Emphasis on fair testing, variables, and scientific method throughout experiments.
- Collaboration with specialists (Art, PE, Drama, Chinese, Digital Skills) for a holistic approach.

ci: Adaptation and sustainability ensure the survival of living things in changing environments.
lo: Adaptations and structural features of living things; Roles of living things in an ecosystem; Actions that benefit or harm ecosystems

s.d: Students will use Minecraft Education to design and build their own 'beetopias'?habitats that support thriving bee colonies. They will apply their knowledge of adaptation and sustainability by considering what bees need to survive and how human actions can help or harm these vital pollinators. This hands-on digital ecosystem creation directly connects to the unit's focus on adaptation, ecosystems, and human responsibility.

Students create visually engaging infographics that explain the structural and behavioral adaptations of a chosen animal, highlighting how these adaptations support survival in specific ecosystems. They can also include actions humans can take to protect these animals, directly linking to the sustainability aspect of the unit.

In collaborative teams, students script, film, and edit short documentaries about endangered animals in different ecosystems. They will use iMovie to combine research, narration, and visuals, demonstrating their understanding of adaptation, threats to survival, and sustainable actions. This mirrors the actual summative task described in the planner.

Students author interactive digital books that showcase the life cycle, adaptations, and ecosystem roles of various living things. They can embed photos, diagrams, and voice recordings to explain how adaptation and sustainability are crucial for survival in changing environments.

Students collaborate on a shared Padlet board to crowdsource examples of human actions that benefit or harm ecosystems around the world. They can post images, links, and reflections, then categorize them as positive or negative impacts, fostering critical thinking about responsibility and sustainability.

plannerText: In this unit, students investigate how adaptation and sustainability ensure the survival of living things in changing environments. They explore animal adaptations (structural and behavioral), the roles of living things in ecosystems, and human actions that benefit or harm ecosystems. Key activities include researching animals, creating documentaries, designing imaginary creatures, and connecting learning to the UN Sustainable Development Goals.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Adaptation and sustainability ensure the survival of living things in changing environments

LINES OF INQUIRY: Adaptations and structural features of living things; Roles of living things in an ecosystem; Actions that benefit or harm ecosystems

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, Function, Responsibility

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: 
- Provocation for the whole unit: Students watch "The World's Largest Lesson" video and use a Think See Wonder routine to record observations and questions about sustainability (see page 74).
- Provocation for animal adaptations and ecosystems: Students observe images of unique animal features, guess the animal, its habitat, and the reason for its adaptations. In partners, they find three facts about each animal and participate in a gallery walk to share findings (page 74).
- Students also use MRS GREN (an acronym for characteristics of living things) song and activities to distinguish living and non-living things (page 75).

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS:
- Research and classify animals into categories (mammals, birds, fish, amphibians, reptiles, insects) using a jigsaw expert group activity (page 75).
- Explore and document different ecosystems through rotation activities and proformas (page 76).
- Guided reading of texts such as "Big Nosed Monkeys" and Springboard readers (Camels, Chameleons, Giant Squid, Animal Armour, Polar Bears) to identify adaptations (pages 76-77).
- Adaptation rotations: Students rotate through stations to investigate animal adaptations, including behavioral (hibernation, migration, torpor) and physical (camouflage, mimicry) features (pages 76-77).
- Food web creation and analysis of ecosystem impacts when food webs are disrupted (page 77).
- Guided reading and discussion on environmental harm and benefit, including dam construction, plastic pollution, and endangered animals (pages 78-79).
- Incorporate UN Sustainable Development Goals 14 (Life Below Water) and 15 (Life on Land) through shared reading, discussion, and student-created artwork representing goal targets (pages 74, 78-79).
- Excursion to Melbourne Aquarium (page 70).
- Visual Art: Students create artwork showing Pok?mon evolution as a metaphor for adaptation (page 71).
- Library: Research sessions, reading "Peculiar Pairs," and using green screen for documentary production (page 72).
- PSPE: Athletics unit connects adaptation in movement to biological adaptation (pages 72, 82).
- Chinese: Explore packaging choices and environmental impact, present findings using Keynote (page 72).
- Digital Skills: Learn to make and edit iMovies for documentary projects (page 72).

ASSESSMENT:
- Students work in teams to create a documentary using iMovie about an endangered animal in a specific ecosystem, incorporating research, scripts, storyboards, and a checklist/rubric based on Bloom's Taxonomy (pages 79-80).
- "Missing Link" assessment: Students design an imaginary animal with four adaptations and write an information report explaining how each feature helps survival (page 79).
- Seesaw portfolio tasks: Making a documentary, Missing Link information report, Seal Monks reading and vocabulary (pages 79-80).
- Self-reflection and peer feedback using "two stars and a wish" during documentary projects (page 81).

TEACHER NOTES:
- Strong integration of UN Sustainable Development Goals 14 and 15 throughout the unit.
- Action opportunities include gold coin donation for conservation organizations during documentary screenings (page 82).
- Transdisciplinary connections: Maths (data/graphs on animal populations), English (information reports, presentations), Science (adaptations, ecosystems, food webs), Visual Art (Pok?mon evolution artwork), PSPE (adaptation in athletics), Chinese (environmental choices and presentations), Digital Skills (iMovie, Keynote).
- Library and digital learning coach support for research, green screen, and documentary production.
- Collaboration with specialists (Art, Library, PSPE, Chinese) to deepen inquiry and provide multiple perspectives.
- Opportunities for student agency: choice of animal, ecosystem, documentary format, and presentation style.
- Reflection and feedback embedded throughout, with visible thinking routines and self-assessment.

If you need further breakdowns (e.g., specific weekly sequence or more detail on a particular learning engagement), please specify.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration leads to new discoveries that can shape and change society.
lo: The factors that motivate exploration (Causation); How people reflect on discoveries from the past to inform change (Change); How exploration brings about change and new understandings for people, places and society (Change)

s.d: Students use Minecraft Education's 'Revamp Melbourne' lesson to collaboratively design a future Melbourne that responds to the needs of explorers and new settlers. They will research historical and modern discoveries that shaped the city, then build and present their own innovative city features that reflect how exploration leads to societal change. This hands-on digital build directly connects to the unit's focus on exploration, discovery, and impact on society.

Students create dynamic infographics or digital posters in Canva to showcase the motivations, journeys, and impacts of famous explorers studied in class. They can visually map push and pull factors, timelines, and the societal changes resulting from exploration, making complex historical connections accessible and engaging.

Students author interactive digital journals or historical diaries in Book Creator, writing from the perspective of a convict, explorer, or First Nations Australian during the First Fleet era. They can embed images, voice recordings, and maps to bring their narratives to life, directly linking to the unit's focus on personal histories and the impact of exploration.

Students document their research and reflections on exploration by uploading video reflections, annotated maps, and digital artifacts to Seesaw. Teachers can prompt students to compare the perspectives of different groups affected by exploration, supporting formative assessment and peer feedback.

Students use ClickView to access curated video resources about the First Fleet, global explorers, and Indigenous perspectives. They then create short video responses or digital timelines summarizing how exploration led to change, supporting critical thinking and media literacy.

plannerText: This unit explores how exploration leads to new discoveries that can shape and change society, with a strong focus on the First Fleet, motivations for exploration, and the impact on both settlers and First Nations Australians. Students engage in historical research, perspective-taking, creative projects, and collaborative inquiry to understand the causes, effects, and ethical dimensions of exploration.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Exploration leads to new discoveries that can shape and change society.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The factors that motivate/drive exploration (Causation); How people reflect on discoveries from the past to inform change (Change); How exploration brings about change and new understandings for people, places and society (Change)

KEY CONCEPTS: Causation, Change

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Students explore a new software independently?exploration leads to discovery. Drama Toolbox incursion: students participate in role-plays of significant historical events (Burke and Wills, Gold Rush, First Fleet) to immerse them in the concept of exploration. Show an example of Captain Cook's journal entry as a model for historical perspective writing.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Watch videos such as NASA Rover completes Mars Mission, How Deep is the Ocean?, and Matthew Hensen's Historic Expedition to the North Pole.
- Use See Think Wonder thinking routine with a graphic organizer.
- Participate in an incursion (Drama Toolbox: First Fleet/gold rush case study).
- Read and analyze texts about explorers (e.g., Ten Pound Pom, Captain Cook by Rae, Meet the Flying Doctors, Race to the North Pole, Eureka Stockade, Zheng He, Burke and Wills, The Lotus Seed, Abel Tasman).
- Use T-chart graphic organizers to identify push and pull factors for exploration.
- Research convicts from the First Fleet using the First Fleet Database and create convict love tokens.
- Complete a grid visible thinking task to document changes (past, present, future) brought about by the First Fleet.
- Map the First Fleet voyage and summarize information for each location.
- Role play the arrival of the First Fleet from both European and Indigenous perspectives.
- Analyze the impact of the First Fleet on Indigenous Australians using resources like Somebody's Land by Adam Goodes and First Contact information sheets.
- Explore Australia Day and Reconciliation Day, including their significance and Indigenous perspectives.
- Create mind maps and artworks in response to Reconciliation Week themes.
- Research and present on three explorers, including their motivation, achievements, and impact on society.
- Write explorer biographies.
- Group project: Imagine setting up a new colony on Mars, incorporating IB Learner Profile traits and UN Sustainable Development Goals, and create a poster promoting the colony.
- Possible field trip: Melbourne colonial history walking tour.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Summative assessment: Write four diary entries from different perspectives (Captain Arthur Phillip, Jailer, Crew member, Convict, Free Settler, First Peoples) about the exploration of the First Fleet and the discovery of Australia.
- Seesaw tasks: Motivation for Exploration, Motivation for Exploring another Planet, Reconciliation Week mind map and reflection, Portfolio Task on explorers.
- Explorer Project: Research and present on a chosen explorer using a template (fact file, fun facts, learner profile, causation, timeline).
- Explorer Biography: Write a biography based on research.
- Group poster and presentation for Mars colony project.
- Student self-assessment and peer feedback on journal entries and projects.
- Sharing learning with the parent community during Student Led Conferences.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with Australian Curriculum (History, English, Science, Social Studies).
- Transdisciplinary connections with PE (exploration and innovation in games), Health (personal discoveries and change), Art (First Fleet ships), Music (folk music and the effect of exploration on music), and Library (research skills).
- Use of digital tools: iPads for research, Seesaw for portfolio tasks and feedback, Prezi for reflection, online databases (First Fleet Database), and trusted sources (Britannica Kids, World Book Kids, Kiddle).
- Student agency promoted through choice of explorer and project format.
- Opportunities for self and peer assessment, as well as teacher feedback.
- Indigenous perspectives and social justice are emphasized, especially regarding the impact of exploration and colonization.
- Reflection activities and visible thinking routines (See Think Wonder, Compass Points) are used throughout.
- Suggestion for future improvement: extend the unit for deeper exploration of present-day and future concepts, and further develop research skills.

ci: An inquiry into the nature of the self; beliefs and values; personal, physical, mental, social and spiritual health; human relationships.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded
plannerText: Not yet documented in PDF.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Individuals use different strategies to overcome challenges and keep themselves safe.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The ways individuals respond to challenges and setbacks; Personal strategies to manage change; Our personal responsibility to keep ourselves safe - when we feel uncomfortable (online and face-to-face); Respecting others to positively influence friendships

KEY CONCEPTS: Perspective, Change, Responsibility

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Students listen to the song "Try Everything" and discuss its message. They participate in Think/Pair/Share activities about growth and fixed mindsets, and complete getting-to-know-you activities in the first week of school.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Growth mindset sessions: Watch videos on growth vs. fixed mindset, match statements, create Y charts, read books such as "Y is for YET," "The Magic Yet," and "The Girl Who Never Made Mistakes," and complete related activities.
- Building team/group skills using Youcubed videos and activities.
- Activities to identify personal strengths, self-portraits, and positive attributes.
- Scenarios to practice changing fixed mindset statements to growth mindset.
- Challenge stations (bean bag, tangram, drawing, tongue twister, extreme dot-to-dot, writing with the opposite hand) with reflection questions at each station.
- Read and discuss "Through the Gate" by Sally Fawcett, tracking changes in mindset.
- "Grow Your Mind" program: Learn about brain parts using animal metaphors, watch videos, and complete Seesaw tasks.
- Building resilience sessions: Identify emotions, threats, challenges, and opportunities; use resilience strategy flashcards; create personal lists and possibly Kahoot games.
- Role-play scenarios to practice resilience and problem-solving.
- URStrong program: Lessons on making friends, trust, friendship fires, tricky situations, and kindness.
- Art: Create sculptures representing responses to challenges and setbacks.
- Music: Explore identity through composition.
- PE/Health: Physical challenges requiring resilience and teamwork; Health lessons on safety and trusted adults.
- Drama: Explore perspective through persuasive language and advertising.
- Library: Related lessons and resources.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Growth mindset portfolio tasks (e.g., Seesaw tasks, picture storybook reflection, problem-solving resilience activities).
- Self-reflection journals and peer feedback.
- Scenario-based activities and role-plays.
- No formal summative assessment for each line of inquiry, but evidence gathered through discussion, observation, and day-to-day teaching.
- Student-led conferences and sharing with parents via workshops and SeeSaw portfolios.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong transdisciplinary connections, especially with PE, Health, Art, Music, and Drama.
- Consistent language and strategies used throughout the year for growth mindset, resilience, and friendship.
- Cyber safety lessons and essential agreements revisited regularly.
- Friendology incursion and practical activities (e.g., bringing soft toys for roleplay) were highly engaging.
- Opportunities for student agency through choice of scenarios, self-assessment, and reflection.
- Technology integration includes SeeSaw for portfolios, Kahoot for resilience strategies, and iPad use for research and reflection.
- Teachers note the benefit of delivering the unit "as needs arise" but suggest more regular assessment and feedback in the future.
- Parents involved through workshops and encouraged to use the same language and strategies at home.

If you need a different unit, please specify.

ci: Technology transforms how we create and share ideas.
lo: How technology expands creative possibilities; The evolution of communication tools; The impact of technology on expression

s.d: Students use 'Minecraft: Shipwreck Narrative' to collaboratively design a mysterious shipwreck scene, then write and record a digital narrative from multiple perspectives (e.g., survivor, rescuer, historian). They can embed clues, hidden messages, or QR codes in the Minecraft world, transforming storytelling into an immersive, interactive digital experience that blurs the line between author and audience.

Students create interactive digital books that showcase how communication tools have evolved, embedding audio interviews with family members, video reflections, and original illustrations. They can publish and share their books with younger students, demonstrating how technology transforms both the creation and sharing of ideas.

Learners design a series of digital posters or infographics that compare historical and modern communication tools, highlighting ethical considerations and cybersafety tips. They use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to remix their own photos into creative digital collages.

Students document their learning journey by recording video reflections, uploading digital artefacts, and responding to teacher prompts about the impact of technology on creativity and communication. They can also peer-assess each other's digital projects, fostering a collaborative, reflective digital community.

Students design and distribute a digital survey to teachers and peers about technology use and creative expression. They analyse the data in Excel, then present their findings as part of a discussion on how technology shapes communication in their community.

plannerText: In this unit, students explore the central idea 'Technology transforms how we create and share ideas.' Key activities include investigating the evolution of communication tools, analysing ethical and cybersafety considerations, surveying technology use in the school, and planning digital workshops for younger students. The unit culminates in students leading hands-on technology workshops for Prep?Year 3, applying their learning to real-world service and creative expression.
humanVerifiedResetReason: Suggestion edited, swapped, regenerated or changed after human verification
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Technology transforms how we create and share ideas.

LINES OF INQUIRY: How technology expands creative possibilities; The evolution of communication tools; Ethical considerations when using digital platforms for expression

KEY CONCEPTS: Cybersafety; Ethical

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: 
Provocation #1: "Day of AI" run by Andrew. Students watch side-by-side clips comparing cave painting vs TikTok, telegram vs email, handwritten letter vs instant messaging. After each pair, students discuss what they notice and how they feel about the differences. They co-construct a classroom timeline of communication methods and reflect on why humans keep finding new ways to communicate. Exit slip: "Communication matters to me because?"

Lesson 2 (Prior Knowledge): Quick brainstorm game: "Name a tech tool you've used this week." Students share which tool they care about most and why. They create a KWL + Care chart (What do we know? What do we wonder? What do we care about?) and interview a family member about how technology/communication has changed and what they care about most when using technology.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS:
- Cybersafety Foundations: "Two Truths and a Trick" game with online behaviour scenarios; group role-play of unsafe vs principled choices; reflection on being a principled digital citizen.
- Evolution of Communication: Research carousel with stations (letters, typewriters, radios, landline phones, social media); students record strengths and limitations of each tool and add to a class chart.
- Surveying Teachers: Students co-design survey questions (Microsoft Form) about technology use in classrooms, role-play respectful surveying, and analyse results to find patterns and insights.
- App Exploration: Rotations through stations using Book Creator, Canva, PowerPoint, Camera, Stop Motion Studio; students record creative features, strengths, and limitations.
- Cybersafety Posters: Students design digital posters for Prep?3 students using Canva or Google Slides, focusing on clear, age-appropriate cybersafety messages.
- Workshop Planning: In groups, students plan and rehearse technology workshops for Prep?3 students, focusing on clear instructions, cybersafety, and creative use of technology.
- Family Tech Tips: Students create flyers with family-friendly tech tips for balanced and safe technology use at home.
- Tech Workshops: Year 4 students deliver their planned workshops to Prep?3 students, coaching them in the use of Book Creator, ScratchJr, Canva, or Stop Motion Studio.
- Reflection & Celebration: Students reflect on their learning, leadership, and the impact of their service on younger learners and the school community.

ASSESSMENT:
- Ongoing formative assessment through exit slips, reflection prompts, and peer feedback.
- Cybersafety poster for younger students.
- Analysis and presentation of teacher survey results.
- Summative assessment: Planning, delivering, and reflecting on a technology workshop for Prep?3 students ("Teach Meet").
- Reflection on technology use and leadership in UOI journals.

TEACHER NOTES:
- Strong integration with Digital Learning Program (DLP) for provocation and application sessions.
- Transdisciplinary connections: English (discussion text type), visual communication, leadership (links to WWA unit), and wellbeing (cybersafety).
- Technology integration: Microsoft Forms for surveys, Canva, Book Creator, PowerPoint, Google Slides, Stop Motion Studio, ScratchJr.
- Action opportunities: Students take meaningful action by teaching younger students and creating resources for both school and home.
- Emphasis on ethical and principled technology use, student agency in workshop design, and leadership development.
- Opportunities for student voice, choice, and ownership through co-constructed learning goals, peer feedback, and reflection.
- Final celebration includes a class showcase and certificates of service.

ci: Systems connect us.
lo: Human systems; The interconnections that exist within and across systems; The influence of systems on communities

s.d: Students use Delightex to collaboratively design and simulate a real-world human-made system (e.g., a school canteen, public transport, or recycling system). They map out the parts, connections, and possible disruptions, then test 'what if' scenarios to see how changes ripple through the system, directly experiencing the central idea that 'systems connect us.'

Students design and distribute a digital survey using Microsoft Forms to investigate which systems (transport, waste, communication, etc.) are most important to their local community. They analyse the results in Excel, identifying patterns and presenting their findings on how systems influence daily life.

Students create a collaborative Padlet where they post photos, diagrams, and short explanations of systems they observe at school, home, or in the community. They use arrows and comments to highlight interconnections between systems, building a visual map of how systems overlap and influence each other.

Students author a digital storybook in Book Creator that follows the journey of an object or person through multiple interconnected systems (e.g., a lunchbox from home to school and back, or a letter through the postal system). They use text, images, and audio to show how each system connects and what happens if one part fails.

Students explore 'The Hall of Inventions' in Minecraft Education, identifying key innovations that have shaped human systems (transport, communication, health, etc.). They then design and build their own invention in the game, explaining how it could connect or improve existing systems in their community.

plannerText: Students begin by brainstorming and mapping systems in their lives, then investigate the form, function, and connections of both natural and human-made systems through hands-on observation, digital documentation, and collaborative inquiry. They explore how systems are interconnected, what happens when systems change or fail, and how human actions can influence or improve systems, culminating in sustainability projects and real-world action.
