plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Systems connect us

LINES OF INQUIRY: Human systems; The interconnections that exist within and across systems; The influence of systems on our community and ourselves

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, change, connection

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Remove a classroom system for the morning (e.g. timetable, class jobs, lining up system), then read "Everything is Connected" by Jason Gruhl. Students discuss what a system is, what happens when a system breaks down or is removed, how it makes them feel, and how we are affected when systems fail.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Create a class mind map or graffiti wall with the word "SYSTEM" in the middle, brainstorming what systems are, what systems are used at school/home/community, and what systems have in common.
- Explore the classroom/school to identify systems (e.g. school bell, library borrowing, canteen ordering, playground rules, technology sign-out, recycling/compost bins, iPad login). Students take notes, draw diagrams, or take photos on iPads and upload to Seesaw.
- Hands-on experiment: Create a Mini Water Cycle in a sealed bag or jar, observe and record findings, label diagrams, and use See?Think?Wonder routines.
- Web of Life with String: Students each represent an organism or non-living element in an ecosystem, connect with string to show dependencies, and observe what happens when one part is removed.
- Explore local ecosystem scenarios (e.g. drought, rubbish in wetlands, frogs disappearing), map impacts, and discuss system connections.
- Make recycled paper in groups, reflect on the recycling system, and discuss sustainability.
- Composting systems: Watch videos, compare landfill vs compost with a Venn diagram, draw compost system diagrams, and discuss decomposers.
- PSPE: Team-based sports (basketball, netball, European handball) to explore roles, rules, cooperation, and strategic planning as human systems.
- Ongoing: Add to a "Types of Systems" display (home, school, nature, technology, transport, communication).

ASSESSMENT: 
- Observations and documentation of student participation in discussions, mind maps, and graffiti walls.
- Seesaw uploads of system observations, water cycle experiment results, and ecosystem diagrams.
- Group and individual reflections (written, drawn, or oral) on system connections and changes.
- Creation of recycled paper and compost system diagrams.
- Participation in the Web of Life activity and reflection on system interdependence.
- Summative tasks such as sustainability projects and presentations to real audiences (e.g. executive team).
- Reflective journaling and gallery walks of student work.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong transdisciplinary connections, especially with science (water cycle, ecosystems, recycling), literacy (expository and persuasive writing), and PSPE (team sports as systems).
- Opportunities for digital integration: Seesaw for documentation, iPads for photos and reflections, digital mind maps, and possible use of Padlet for question generation.
- Emphasis on student agency: students co-construct learning goals, participate in inquiry-based and collaborative activities, and reflect on their learning.
- Authentic action opportunities: sustainability initiatives, recycling, composting, and sharing learning with the school community.
- Collaboration among teaching staff noted as a strength, with suggestions for further integration of mathematics (data collection, analysis) in future iterations.
- Flexibility in the unit allows for evolving lines of inquiry and student-driven questions.

ci: Changes to the Earth impact environment and communities.
lo: The causes of geological change over time; How extreme environments cause natural disasters; The impact of natural events on communities

s.d: Transform your students into 'Natural Disaster News Reporters'! In this innovative project, Year 4s will research a real-world natural disaster (such as bushfires, floods, earthquakes, or volcanic eruptions) and create a dynamic multimedia news report using Adobe Express. Students will combine images, video clips (recorded with the iPad Camera app), voiceovers, and text to explain the causes of the disaster, its impact on environments and communities, and how people responded and adapted. This engaging digital storytelling approach empowers students to synthesize their learning, practice digital literacy, and creatively communicate complex scientific ideas to an authentic audience.

Have students document their hands-on STEM experiments (like the Earthquake Tower or Flood Simulation) using the iPad Camera app. They then upload annotated photos and video reflections to Seesaw, combining visual evidence with voice recordings to explain the science behind the disasters and how communities respond.

Students use Micro:bit to create animated models of volcanic eruptions, exploring geological changes and their impact on communities. They code sequences that simulate natural disasters, deepening their understanding of cause and effect in Earth's dynamic systems. (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=volcano-animations-unit-of-work)

Students will become disaster engineers by designing and building earthquake simulators and flood-resistant structures using Lego Spike Prime. They'll test how different building techniques withstand simulated natural disasters, collect data on their designs' performance, and iterate to improve resilience. To showcase their findings, students will create dynamic presentations in Canva, combining photos, diagrams, and reflections to explain how communities can adapt to Earth's changing environments.

Connect the study of floods and community response to real-world engineering by having students use Minecraft: Filtering Water On The Iss (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/filtering-water-on-the-iss). Students experiment with designing water filtration systems, linking to how communities recover from floods and ensure safe water supplies after disasters.

plannerText: This unit engages Year 4 students in exploring how changes to the Earth, such as natural disasters, impact environments and communities. Through hands-on experiments, research projects, digital presentations, and case studies, students investigate the causes of geological change, the effects of extreme environments, and how communities respond and adapt to these challenges.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Changes to the Earth impact environment and communities.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The causes of geological change over time; How extreme environment causes natural disasters; How communities respond to environmental change.

KEY CONCEPTS: Function, Causation, Change

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: 
- Students watch dramatic video footage of natural disasters ("Top 10 Most Dramatic Footage of Natural Disasters Caught on Camera").
- Jigsaw Activity: Students are grouped to become "experts" on one type of natural disaster (floods, cyclones, volcanic eruptions, earthquakes), research using curated videos, and record findings on a jigsaw piece template. They then share and assemble their pieces into a class display, discussing similarities and differences.
- Prior knowledge is activated with a ClickView video on natural disasters, followed by a class brainstorm wall or digital mind map titled "What we know about natural disasters."

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS:
- Watch videos and complete jigsaw research on floods, cyclones, volcanic eruptions, and earthquakes (ABC BTN, BBC, TED-Ed, etc.).
- Bushfire simulation using CDU's Bushfire Simulator; students investigate how conditions affect bushfire spread and create "Bushfire Action Posters."
- News broadcast project: In groups, students script and film a BTN-style news report on an imaginary bushfire, explaining causes, processes, and effects, and upload to Seesaw.
- Indigenous fire practices: Students learn about cultural burning and compare with modern firefighting.
- Case studies: Research major bushfires and earthquakes (e.g., Black Saturday, Lombok 2018, Japan 2011), create visual timelines, and compare community responses.
- Earthquake Tower Experiment: Build and test model structures for earthquake resistance, record results, and reflect.
- Flood mapping and simulation: Visual source analysis of global floods, optional hands-on flood simulation, and mapping flood-prone areas.
- Group research and presentations on real flood events (Spain 2024, QLD/NSW 2022, Pakistan 2022), focusing on community response.
- Independent research project: Students select a natural disaster event, use a research log (Seesaw), and create a poster or digital presentation addressing causes, effects, and community response.
- Maths integration: Mapping disasters, measuring bushfire area, flood runoff experiments, climate data analysis, and disaster relief budgeting (see pages 106?110).

ASSESSMENT:
- Formative: Natural Disaster Quiz, reflection questions after each session, Venn diagrams comparing disasters, thinking routines (Claim-Support-Question), and exit tickets.
- Summative: Independent research project (poster or digital presentation) demonstrating understanding of the central idea and lines of inquiry, with self-management and research goals set and reflected upon in Seesaw.
- Group news broadcast videos uploaded to Seesaw.
- Peer and teacher feedback on presentations and research logs.
- Presentations to younger students (Year 2/1), using a presenting rubric.

TEACHER NOTES:
- Strong transdisciplinary links: English (explanation texts, cause/effect), Maths (measurement, data, mapping), Science (experiments, scientific method), Music (composing in response to environmental issues), Visual Art (to be completed), PSPE (identity/interactions, PE), Digital Skills (LibGuide, Seesaw, digital mind maps), Library (mentor texts).
- Action opportunities: Raising awareness, emergency kit tips, protecting animals, advocating for preparedness.
- Integration of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives through Indigenous fire practices and cultural burning.
- Use of digital tools: Seesaw for research logs, video reflections, and uploading group projects; digital mind mapping; curated online resources (LibGuide, Britannica, World Book, Kiddle).
- Student agency: Choice of disaster/event for research, co-constructed learning goals, flexible grouping, and self-assessment.
- Emphasis on reflection and metacognition: Students set and review self-management and research goals, reflect on learning after each session, and share findings with peers and the school community.
- Opportunities for further technology integration: Digital presentations (Canva, PowerPoint, Google Slides), video creation, and online collaborative tools.

(References: Pages 1?29, 106?110, including embedded images and diagrams for maths integration and learning engagement visuals.)

ci: Country, language and community are significant to the lives of First Nations people.
lo: The diversity of First Nations people; The importance of community, family and kinship; The importance of Country to First Nations people

s.d: Students use Merge Cubes and the Field Guide to Victoria app to create interactive 3D representations of significant sites, animals, and plants connected to local First Nations Country. They record audio explanations in Woiwurrung or English, sharing the cultural and ecological significance of each element. These cubes become a digital 'living museum' that can be explored by peers and shared with the community.

Students design digital posters or infographics in Canva that visually represent the connections between Country, language, and community for First Nations people. They incorporate Aboriginal language words, maps, and symbols, using Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to layer student artwork or photos of local Country.

Students collaboratively author a digital book in Book Creator that tells the story of local First Nations Country, language, and community. Each student contributes a page with text, images, and audio, including interviews with Elders (if available), personal reflections, and research on local language groups.

Students build a collaborative Padlet wall where they post and comment on digital artefacts (photos, audio, video, text) that represent their learning about the significance of Country, language, and community. They can include Aboriginal place names, language words, and reflections on how these elements shape identity and belonging.

Students script, record, and edit short podcasts in Adobe Express Podcasting, sharing stories about the importance of Country, language, and community for First Nations people. Episodes might feature interviews, retellings of Dreaming stories (with permission), or personal reflections on connection to place.

plannerText: This unit explores how Country, language, and community are significant to the lives of First Nations people. Students investigate the diversity of First Nations groups, the importance of traditions, language, and connection to place, and reflect on how these elements shape identity and belonging. Activities include mapping, language exploration, storytelling, and creative projects that deepen understanding of Indigenous perspectives.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Country, language and community are significant to the lives of First Nations people.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The diversity of First Nations people; The importance of community, family and traditions to First Nations people; The importance of Country to First Nations people

KEY CONCEPTS: Connection, Perspective

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: The main provocation is the Year 4 camp, used as an immersive experience to reflect on the significance of Country, community, and language. Before camp, students brainstorm what Country, community, and language mean to them and First Nations people, and complete a Seesaw prior knowledge task. After camp, students revisit and document their thinking in Seesaw using a different colour to show growth in understanding.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Split screening and brainstorming on the significance of Country, community, and language.
- Research and note-taking on Indigenous groups around the world using slides and a world map (Native Americans, Adivasi, Berber People, Baka, Maori, Mongols, Mayans, Inuits, Nenet, Quechuan).
- Padlet reflections on facts learned about each group.
- Central Idea and Lines of Inquiry jigsaw activity.
- Listening to Baker Boy's "Maryunna" and exploring Aboriginal languages, creating a visual dictionary of Woiwurrung words.
- Reading and discussing 'Back on Country' by Adam Goodes and 'My Culture and Me' by Gregg Drerise.
- Seesaw task: significance of community to children of the Koorie Language Group; drawing and writing about being a Wesley Kid.
- Researching Indigenous contributions to ANZAC Day, including case studies of Reg Saunders, Leonard Waters, and Douglas Grant.
- Reading 'Welcome to Country' by Aunty Joy Murphy, discussing and writing personal and class Acknowledgements of Country.
- Artistic expressions: research and graphic organiser on painting (rock art), music (didgeridoo/rhythm sticks), and dance.
- Dreaming stories: reading, summarising, and performing the Bundjil and Waa story.
- Songlines: video analysis, exploration stations (artworks, digital resources, books), and anchor chart creation.
- Bush tucker: BTN video, research on bush foods, tasting session.
- Profile of Adam Goodes: video, hand template with five facts, Seesaw reflection.
- 3-2-1 Bridge reflection on learning.
- Listening to Little Yarns podcasts about Country and community.
- Creating totem designs in Art for display, possibly planting First Nation plants as action.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Seesaw prior knowledge and reflection tasks.
- Indigenous Groups Table and Padlet reflections.
- Visual dictionary of Aboriginal language.
- Artistic expression graphic organisers.
- Dreaming story summaries and performances.
- Songlines exploration station responses and anchor chart.
- Bush tucker research and tasting reflection.
- Adam Goodes profile (hand template and Seesaw reflection).
- 3-2-1 Bridge reflection.
- Totem designs for display.
- Personal and class Acknowledgement of Country writing.
- Group and individual research tasks on Indigenous veterans.
- Participation in discussions and visible thinking routines.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong transdisciplinary connections with Art (totem design), Library (research and presentations), and Maths (mapping, songlines, location).
- Camp is used as a key provocation and reference point for understanding Country.
- Integration with Reconciliation Week and authentic links to Wellbeing and Social Studies.
- Use of digital tools: Seesaw for reflections and tasks, Padlet for sharing facts, online language resources, and digital slides for research.
- Opportunities for student agency: question matrix, choice of research focus, creative responses, and action projects.
- Action opportunities include creating displays, sharing learning with younger students, and possible environmental action (planting).
- Emphasis on learner profile attributes (Caring, Open-minded) and approaches to learning (thinking, communication, social skills).
- Teacher reflections highlight the unit's success in fostering deep, meaningful learning and strong student engagement.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Human migration is a response to challenges and opportunities
lo: The reasons people migrate; Migration throughout history; Effects of migration on communities, cultures and individuals
plannerText: Not yet documented in PDF.

ci: Leaders shape communities.
lo: Roles and responsibilities of effective leadership; The purpose of leadership in communities; Qualities of influential leaders

s.d: Students design and script an AI chatbot that simulates a famous leader (e.g., Malala Yousafzai, Greta Thunberg, Adam Goodes). Using Wise Discussion Chatbots, students program the bot to answer questions about leadership qualities, responsibilities, and community impact, allowing peers to 'interview' the leader and reflect on how leaders shape communities.

Students create interactive digital leadership portfolios using Canva, showcasing their own leadership journey, strengths, and reflections. Each portfolio includes multimedia elements (photos, videos, quotes) and is shared with the school community to inspire others and celebrate diverse leadership styles.

Students collaboratively author a digital book profiling local, national, and global leaders. Each student contributes a page highlighting a leader's roles, responsibilities, and impact on their community, integrating text, images, and audio narration to bring the stories to life.

Students design and distribute a survey using Microsoft Forms to gather data on leadership qualities valued by their peers and teachers. They analyse the results in class, discuss trends, and present findings on what makes an effective leader in their school community.

Students explore the 'Active Citizen' Minecraft Education world, where they learn about Nobel Peace Laureates and how their leadership shaped communities. Students reflect on the qualities of these leaders and build a virtual monument or community project inspired by their learning.

plannerText: The unit 'Who We Are' explores the central idea that 'Leaders shape communities.' Students investigate the roles, responsibilities, and qualities of effective leaders through case studies, guided reading, and self-reflection. Activities include researching famous leaders, analysing leadership in different contexts, and applying their understanding by developing their own leadership skills and presenting applications for school leadership roles.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Leaders shape communities.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Roles and responsibilities of effective leadership; The purpose of leadership; The strengths and qualities of a leader

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, Causation, Responsibility

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: 
- Students watch a clip from Toy Story Crossing the Road and discuss why it was shown, how characters respond, which roles are played, and how leadership is demonstrated.
- Central Idea is revealed: "Leaders Shape Communities." Elements of the unit are introduced from a classroom display.
- Students break down the central idea and discuss what it means to be a leader, what questions they have about leadership, and leadership opportunities at Wesley College Junior School.
- Students watch the short speech 'Fall Forward' and reflect in trios on how they will try to 'Fall Forward' this year.
- Students create a leadership quote to drive their leadership in 2025, written on strips of paper and displayed in the learning environment.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS:
- Read "The world needs who you were made to be" by Joanna Gaines and discuss the message and learner profiles.
- Compass points learning task: students identify their personality type and label themselves as a colour.
- Read a series of texts throughout Week 1 to expose students to leadership (e.g., Dear Mr. President, I love my purse, The Darkest Dark, The Thing Lou Couldn't Do, What's my superpower, The Little Hummingbird).
- Roles and responsibility sort: students use online dictionaries to define roles and responsibilities, then sort examples for different professions.
- Brainstorm and role-play the difference between a leader and a boss.
- Use the 'Learner profile and learner attitudes' reflection page to identify which elements a great leader would use all the time, some of the time, or never.
- Brain Pop Jr. and YouTube videos about Martin Luther King Jr., followed by a Claim-Support-Question thinking routine.
- Case studies of famous leaders (e.g., Adam Goodes, Albert Einstein) with comprehension and reflection tasks.
- Students identify and research a famous leader to write an information report about.
- Leadership workshops led by Nathan Basaraba, focusing on qualities of a leader and application to students' own lives.
- Students self-assess their leadership qualities and write applications for various leadership roles (e.g., Culture and Traditions Leader, Ambassador, STEM, Sports, SRC, Social Justice, Library, Environment, Arts, Publication).
- Visual Art: students paint a portrait of their chosen leader on canvas.
- Music: discussions and modelling of leadership and responsibility in music and choir lessons.
- PSPE: "Leadership in Action" portfolio task, reflecting on leadership moments in PE and health lessons.

ASSESSMENT:
- Portfolio task on Seesaw: Roles and responsibilities of leaders.
- Leadership application Seesaw tasks for various roles.
- Information report writing on a chosen leader.
- Self-assessment rubrics for leadership qualities.
- Leadership in Action portfolio task in PE.
- Visual Art portrait of a leader.
- Guided Reading comprehension and note-taking on famous leaders.

TEACHER NOTES:
- Leadership workshops are a highlight, with ongoing sessions throughout the year focusing on different aspects (e.g., SMART goals, legacy).
- Strong transdisciplinary connections with Art and Library; opportunities to expand into Music and PE for future iterations.
- Possible integration of statistics (e.g., collecting data on leadership qualities and measuring growth over the year).
- Wellbeing connections through goal setting and self-reflection.
- Students are encouraged to have voice, choice, and ownership through co-constructed learning goals, self-assessment, and leadership applications.
- The unit is the foundation for the Year 4 leadership journey, with the Leadership Assembly early in Term 1 necessitating an early start to the unit.
- In 2025, the unit became a year-long focus, which was considered a successful decision.
- Opportunities for digital learning include Seesaw for reflections, applications, and portfolio tasks; potential for further technology integration in research and presentations.

ci: People can create or manipulate messages to target specific audiences.
lo: The devices used to target audiences; Critical evaluation of messages; How we know we have been influenced

s.d: Students design and script their own AI-powered chatbot that simulates a persuasive advertiser or influencer. The chatbot interacts with classmates, adapting its language and tactics to target different audiences (e.g., children, parents, teens), allowing students to critically analyse and reflect on how digital messages are manipulated in real time.

Students create a series of contrasting digital advertisements for the same product, each tailored to a different audience (e.g., kids, adults, eco-conscious consumers). They use Canva's design tools, including the 'Remove Background' feature, to manipulate imagery and layout, then present and justify their design choices.

Students script, record, and edit a podcast episode where they deconstruct a popular media message (song, ad, or news story), identifying persuasive devices and discussing how the message is shaped for its intended audience. They publish their podcast for peer review and feedback.

Students collaboratively curate a digital wall of real-world media examples (ads, news clips, social posts), tagging each with the intended audience and persuasive techniques used. They then comment on each other's posts, debating the effectiveness and ethical implications of the messaging.

Students become school reporters in a Minecraft world, investigating three unique stories and evaluating the credibility and bias of different sources. They decode clues, uncover the truth, and reflect on how information can be manipulated to influence audiences. URL: https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/the-investigators

plannerText: This unit explores how people create or manipulate messages to target specific audiences. Students critically analyse media, advertisements, and stories, identifying persuasive devices and evaluating their impact. Activities include deconstructing texts, creating targeted messages, and reflecting on how they know they have been influenced.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: People can create or manipulate messages to target specific audiences.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The devices used to target audiences; Critical evaluation of messages; How we know we have been influenced

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, responsibility, perspective, change

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Visit to the LUME to see the Vincent van Gogh exhibition, followed by a treasure hunt at the Melbourne Museum to spark curiosity. In class, students unjumble words to create the central idea and discuss their meanings.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Analysis of rich mentor texts such as "Click, Clack, Moo: Cows That Type," "The True Story of the Three Little Pigs," "The Sneetches," "Fox," "The Day the Crayons Quit," "I Want My Hat Back," "Tuesday," "If I Built a Car," and "Voices in the Park."
- Song lyric analysis: "Beds Are Burning" by Midnight Oil, "From Little Things Big Things Grow" by Paul Kelly and Kev Carmody, "My Island Home" by Christine Anu, "I Was Only 19" by Redgum, "Down Under" by Men at Work.
- Viewing and analysis of Australian advertising campaigns and media: "Australian Made" commercials, GetUp! activism videos, ABC BTN reports, "Dumb Ways to Die" campaign, "The Gruen Transfer" advertising analysis, "The Final Quarter" documentary, Australian Government social campaigns (e.g., Slip, Slop, Slap), and Kevin Rudd's 2008 "Sorry Speech."
- Literacy block lessons using "Click, Clack, Moo: Cows That Type" for comprehension, vocabulary, sentence structure, evaluative comprehension, narrative story graphing, and writing alternative endings.
- Persuasive writing activities: "Can You Convince Me?" game, analysis and planning of persuasive texts, argument mapping, and writing on topics such as "Dogs Are Better Than Cats."
- Visual Art: Research and exploration of van Gogh's techniques, followed by students creating their own artwork inspired by van Gogh.
- Text response activities for "My Island Home" and "Dumb Ways to Die" campaign.
- Viewing and discussion of "The Final Quarter" documentary, focusing on media influence and public perception.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Seesaw Reading Fluency Assessment
- Student responses to comprehension and evaluative questions
- Written alternative story endings
- Persuasive writing samples and argument maps
- Visualisation activities and artwork in the style of van Gogh
- Text responses and discussions
- Portfolio of learning engagements

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration of digital resources: Seesaw for assessment, YouTube for video texts, online campaign materials, and digital anchor charts for persuasive writing.
- Transdisciplinary connections with Visual Art (LUME excursion, van Gogh study), English (narrative and persuasive writing, text analysis), and Social Studies (media influence, national identity, Indigenous perspectives).
- Opportunities for students to use technology for research, communication, and presentation (ICT skills).
- Emphasis on critical thinking, ethical use of media, and understanding of how messages are constructed and received.
- Action opportunities include analysing real-world campaigns and reflecting on personal influence and responsibility as media consumers and creators.
- Note: PE and Dance & Drama did not connect to this unit; Music and Language specialists to complete their integration.

ci: Entrepreneurs have a responsibility to develop, market and sell sustainable products and services.
lo: The power of giving back; How our environment is impacted by the products we create; Sustainable business practices

s.d: Students work in teams to design and build a sustainable business solution for Melbourne's future in Minecraft Education. They must consider the needs of the community, environmental impact, and how their entrepreneurial idea can give back. After building, students present their solution and explain how it addresses sustainability and social responsibility.

Students create a digital marketing campaign for their sustainable product or service, including posters, infographics, and social media posts. They use Canva's design tools to visually communicate their product's environmental benefits and responsible business practices, then present their campaign to peers for feedback.

Students design and distribute a digital survey to gather data from the school community about consumer preferences for sustainable products. They analyse the results to inform their product development and marketing strategies, making data-driven decisions as real entrepreneurs.

Students script, record, and edit a podcast episode where they interview local entrepreneurs or sustainability experts (real or role-played by classmates). The podcast explores the challenges and responsibilities of running a sustainable business, and is published to the school community.

Students collaboratively brainstorm and document their entrepreneurial journey on a shared Padlet board. They post ideas, research, product sketches, marketing drafts, and reflections, allowing for peer feedback and iterative improvement throughout the unit.

plannerText: Students explore the responsibilities of entrepreneurs in developing, marketing, and selling sustainable products and services. They use the design thinking process to research, ideate, prototype, and test their ideas, culminating in a market stall event where they pitch and sell their creations to the school community.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Entrepreneurs have a responsibility to develop, market and sell sustainable products and services using sustainable methods.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The power of giving back; How our environment is impacted by the products we create; The environment and sustainability of consumer choice

KEY CONCEPTS: Function, causation, responsibility

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Watch Down To Earth, Season 2 Episode 8 Eco-Innovators on Netflix. Unpack the central idea. Reflect, Act, Share. Thinking Routine ? What is the carbon cycle? Shared readings about the carbon cycle, vocabulary exploration, reading comprehension activities, and creating a flow diagram to explain the carbon cycle. Case studies on innovations to reduce atmospheric carbon, including videos and articles about carbon builder, Sam Elsom, regenerative farming, and Great Wrap. Survey Junior School students about potential products to develop and sell at the market.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Shared readings and comprehension activities about the carbon cycle and sustainability innovations.
- Case studies on companies practicing corporate social responsibility, with group roles for reading, highlighting, writing, and reporting.
- Matching activity on learner profile attributes of entrepreneurs.
- Lessons on what it means to be an entrepreneur, including videos and articles.
- Design thinking process: students work through each stage (empathize, define, ideate, prototype, test, make in bulk) using ChatGPT and other resources.
- Conducting market research with Junior School students, including writing formal emails to teachers.
- Data analysis and visualization from market research.
- Prototyping and testing product ideas.
- Advertising and setting up a market stall to sell products.
- Budgeting for expenses and income.
- Home learning: make an advertisement.
- SRC and guest speakers (Courtney, week 6) on types of action.
- Iceberg action activity (Week 6, Angelee).

ASSESSMENT: Team presentations on corporate social responsibility case studies; Reading comprehension questions; Learning journals documenting the design thinking process; Prototypes and products for the market stall; Budgeting tasks; Advertisement creation; Market stall event as a culminating performance task.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with English (resume writing, spelling, reading comprehension, grammar), Maths (financial maths, fractions, data collection and interpretation), and PE (goal setting, responsibility, risk-taking).
- Digital skills: students use ChatGPT to support the design thinking process, conduct research, and generate questions; formal email writing modeled with slides and videos.
- Action opportunities include participation in the market stall, advocacy, and social entrepreneurship.
- Transdisciplinary connections to Music (instrument research project linked to systems and function), Dance & Drama (myth creation and performance), and Visual Art (not specified).
- Opportunities for student agency through choice of product, design process, and advertising.
- Wellbeing connections through responsible decision-making and teamwork.
- Technology suggestions: use of digital surveys, data visualization tools, email, and ChatGPT for ideation and research.

ci: Design processes enable people to respond creatively to challenges in the natural world.
lo: The nature and impact of natural hazards (Causation); How design processes are used to generate and test solutions (Function); The ethical and practical considerations in human responses to natural challenges (Responsibility)

s.d: Students program CoDrone EDU drones to simulate emergency response scenarios for natural hazards such as bushfires, floods, or earthquakes. They design and code flight paths to deliver 'aid packages' to mapped zones, collect environmental data, or survey affected areas, directly linking to the unit's focus on design processes and creative responses to real-world challenges.

s.t: Minecraft: Filtering Water On The Iss
s.d: Students explore how water is filtered on the International Space Station, then design and build their own water filtration systems in Minecraft Education. This hands-on digital activity connects to the unit's emphasis on innovative design solutions to natural challenges.

Students use Micro:bits to monitor classroom energy usage and design simple alert systems that encourage sustainable energy practices. This activity supports the unit's focus on ethical and practical considerations in human responses to environmental challenges.

Students create interactive digital presentations that document their design thinking process for a chosen natural hazard challenge. They record voiceovers, annotate diagrams, and visually explain how their prototypes address real-world problems.

Students design persuasive infographics or campaign posters advocating for innovative solutions to local natural hazards, using Canva's design tools and 'Remove Background' feature to create professional-quality visuals for community advocacy.

plannerText: This unit engages Year 5 students in investigating natural hazards and the impact on people and environments. Students explore design thinking cycles, analyse real-world challenges, and develop, prototype, and pitch innovative solutions to natural disasters. Activities include mapping, data analysis, stakeholder empathy, prototyping, and community advocacy.

The experience culminates in a virtual showcase where students explain their creative solutions and scientific thinking.","url":""},{"t":"Wise Discussion Chatbots","d":"Learners engage with a teacher-created Wise Discussion Chatbot using the Socratic Tutor scenario, where the chatbot takes on the role of 'Dr Maya Chen, a disaster resilience engineer'. The project culminates in a showcase where teams pitch their homes, demonstrating both engineering and coding solutions.","url":""}]
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Design processes enable people to respond creatively to challenges in the natural world.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The nature and impact of natural hazards (causation); How design processes are used to generate and test solutions (function); The ethical and practical considerations in human responses to natural challenges (responsibility)

KEY CONCEPTS: Function, Causation, Responsibility

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Provocation: "Nature doesn't need people. People need nature" ? analysis of interdependence. Students engage in workshops with UNICEF, read Jackie French's Fire picture book to explore metaphorical thinking and human responses, and watch the "Nature is Speaking" Julia Roberts video to provoke inquiry into the power of nature and humans' place in the system. Students create a Wonder Wall around the question: How can design help people live with nature's power?

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Jigsaw on different types of natural hazards (bushfires, floods, droughts, earthquakes, etc.)
- Bushfires and data literacy using infographics and comprehension tasks
- Earthquakes and tectonic plates (science understanding, mapping, cut-out models)
- Floods and flood mitigation/preparedness
- Traditional Indigenous land management (cool burning)
- Concept mapping linking natural hazards to human and environmental impacts
- Risk Triangle activity distinguishing hazards from disasters
- Venn diagram comparing modern and traditional land management
- Mapping fire risk, infrastructure, and vulnerable locations
- Comparing Newcastle and Meckering earthquakes
- Systems thinking model to sort causes, impacts, and responses
- UNICEF Workshop Part 2: Design Thinking Introduction
- Earthquake-resistant structure design challenge
- Ideation and stakeholder analysis using stakeholder cards
- Drafting "How might we?" questions
- Prototyping week: students build and test tangible solutions
- Use of persuasive pitch structure to explain process and design decisions
- School Showcase and Pitch Presentations
- Poster/timeline creation for community advocacy (e.g., flood protection in Lismore)
- Optional: Write to real-world stakeholders (council, emergency services) sharing ideas

ASSESSMENT: Prototypes and pitch presentations; feedback from peers and teachers; reflection using UNICEF marking criteria; public exhibition or community showcase; creation of information materials for younger students or community; optional submission to UNICEF semi-finals and finals.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with Maths (data analysis, measurement, mapping, budgeting), Science (natural processes, design thinking, scientific inquiry), and Social Studies (geography, impacts of hazards, Indigenous perspectives).
- Opportunities for digital skills: data visualization, mapping tools, using Padlet or digital Wonder Walls, prototyping with digital tools, and creating persuasive presentations.
- Action opportunities include participation, advocacy, and social entrepreneurship, with explicit links to the UN Sustainable Development Goals.
- Transdisciplinary connections are emphasized, with collaborative workshops (e.g., UNICEF), and authentic links to local and global challenges.
- Teachers are encouraged to use thinking routines, flexible grouping, and universally designed lessons to support agency and inclusion.
- Reflection and feedback are built into the process, with opportunities for student voice and choice throughout the inquiry and design cycle.

ci: Finding peaceful solutions to conflict can make a difference in the world.
lo: Types of conflict; Causes and impacts of conflict; Conflict resolution

s.d: Students design and train AI chatbots to simulate real-world conflict scenarios (e.g., playground disputes, community disagreements, historical conflicts). They script dialogue for both sides, program the chatbot to respond with peaceful resolution strategies, and test each other's bots to see if they can reach a positive outcome. This pushes students to deeply analyse causes, perspectives, and peaceful solutions to conflict.

Students collaboratively create digital infographics or interactive posters that break down different types of conflict (personal, local, global) and showcase step-by-step peaceful resolution strategies. They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to add themselves into historical or hypothetical conflict scenes, visually demonstrating their role as peacebuilders.

Set up a digital 'Peace Wall' where students post real-life or historical examples of conflict and brainstorm possible peaceful solutions. They can comment on each other's posts, vote on the most effective strategies, and reflect on how different perspectives shape conflict and resolution.

Students author and publish interactive digital storybooks that follow characters through a conflict and resolution journey. They can embed audio reflections, video dramatizations, and hyperlinks to real-world peace initiatives, making their books a resource for younger students.

Using the 'Peace Builders' lesson (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/peace-builders), students explore stories of Nobel Peace Laureates and collaboratively build virtual monuments or environments that represent peaceful solutions to conflict. They reflect on how their builds symbolize real-world actions and present their creations to the class.

plannerText: This unit explores the theme 'Sharing the Planet' with a central idea focused on finding peaceful solutions to conflict. Students investigate types, causes, and impacts of conflict, analyse real-world and historical examples, and engage in activities such as literature analysis, music, drama, and digital collaboration. The unit emphasizes perspective-taking, resolution strategies, and student agency in advocating for peace at personal, local, and global levels.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Learners create 3D conflict scenario dioramas on Merge Cube, then use Delightex to animate peaceful resolutions and dialogue between characters. This app smash empowers students to visualise and role-play conflict resolution strategies, making abstract concepts tangible as they explore causes, impacts, and solutions to conflict in local and global contexts. The interactive models spark rich discussion and reflection on how peaceful actions can change the world.","url":""},{"t":"Microsoft Forms","d":"Students design and distribute a class survey using Microsoft Forms to collect peers' opinions on different types of conflict and effective peaceful resolutions. They analyse the collected data to identify common causes and impacts of conflict, then collaboratively create a summary report that highlights key findings and suggests strategies for conflict resolution aligned with the unit?s central idea.","url":""},{"t":"Adobe Express Podcasting","d":"Students create a 'Peace Talks' podcast, analysing protest songs, historical events, and personal stories of conflict resolution. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), they collaborate to script and record episodes that explore peaceful solutions to conflict, amplifying diverse perspectives and fostering empathy.","url":""},{"t":"Canva","d":"Students will work in teams to create an interactive digital 'Peace Pathways' infographic in Canva. Students will incorporate protest song lyrics, quotes from historical peacemakers, and their own reflections. This empowers students to synthesise their learning and share actionable pathways to peace with the wider school community.","url":"https://www.canva.com/"},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students collaboratively explore the 'Peace Builders' Minecraft Education world (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/peace-builders), engaging with stories of Nobel Peace Laureates and designing their own virtual spaces that promote dialogue, negotiation, and peaceful solutions to conflict. They reflect on how these digital environments can inspire real-world action.","url":""},{"t":"Peace Pathways: Interactive Conflict Resolution Board","d":"Students collaboratively design and build a large, physical board game that simulates real-world conflict scenarios and peaceful resolution strategies. Using cardboard, recycled materials, clay, and art supplies, they craft the game board, event cards, and player pieces representing diverse cultures and perspectives. Integrating Sphero Indi robots or similar, students program these robots to act as 'peacemakers' that move along the board based on players' decisions and outcomes. As part of the Design Cycle, students empathise by researching global and local conflicts, define key issues, ideate peaceful solutions, prototype their board and tech integrations, test play with classmates, and refine both physical and digital elements. This hands-on, hybrid project fosters creativity, critical thinking, and empathy by blending tangible making with interactive technology.","url":""}]
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Finding peaceful solutions to conflict can make a difference in the world.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Types of conflict; Causes and impacts of conflict; Conflict resolution

KEY CONCEPTS: Connection, perspective

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Read the picture book "Old Turtle" with students, followed by a 'making connections' reading comprehension activity called 'Double Entry Journal'. Model the process for students. Complete a Colour ? Symbol ? Image thinking routine about "Conflict". Watch a video of John Lennon singing "Imagine", hand out lyrics, and ask students how John Lennon imagines peace can be achieved through the lyrics. Students then complete another Colour Symbol Image chart about what peace means to them.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Analyse and discuss songs such as "Imagine" by John Lennon, "Blowin' in the Wind" by Bob Dylan, "We are the World" by Michael Jackson, and "From Little Things Big Things Grow" by Paul Kelly and Kev Carmody, focusing on themes of peace, protest, and reconciliation.
- Read and discuss picture books including "Sorry Day" by Coral Vass, "Say Yes" by Jennifer Castles, "The Rabbits" by John Marsden and Shaun Tan, and "My Country" by Ezekiel Kwaymullina and Sally Morgan.
- Use videos and documentaries such as "ABC Education: Indigenous Australia ? Fighting for Equality", "Paul Kelly ? From Little Things Big Things Grow" (music video), and "Australian Human Rights Commission: Bringing Them Home Report".
- Engage with articles and short non-fiction texts about reconciliation and peacebuilding, including "Reconciliation Australia: Stories of Reconciliation" and "UNICEF Australia: Children as Peacebuilders".
- Read novels and chapter books such as "The Burnt Stick" by Anthony Hill, "Nanberry: Black Brother White" by Jackie French, "Two Wolves" by Tristan Bancks, and "Rabbit-Proof Fence" by Doris Pilkington.
- Complete a Colour ? Symbol ? Image thinking routine about "Conflict" and "Peace".
- Science Fair Project: Generate a list of 10 high-interest topics for exploration, create inquiry questions, and use Padlet for sharing.
- Model question generation using an avocado seed, sort questions into key concept categories, and use research records to guide inquiry.
- PSPE: Participate in team-based games and cooperative activities in PE to explore conflict and resolution in real-time situations.
- Dance & Drama: Study improvisation, focusing on perspective, connection, and collaborative skills.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Double Entry Journal for reading comprehension.
- Colour ? Symbol ? Image charts for conflict and peace.
- Reading and discussion responses to songs, picture books, and novels.
- Science Fair Project: Inquiry question generation and research record.
- PE reflections on conflict resolution and teamwork.
- Drama appraisals and constructive criticism.
- Ongoing formative assessment through class discussions, group work, and individual reflections.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Integration with PSPE (Physical Education) for real-time conflict and resolution practice.
- Use of Padlet and ChatGPT for digital question generation and concept sorting.
- Strong focus on Australian context, Indigenous perspectives, and reconciliation.
- Opportunities for transdisciplinary connections with English (literature and comprehension), Social Studies (history and reconciliation), and the Arts (music, drama, and visual art).
- Action opportunities include participation, advocacy, and social justice, with links to the UN Sustainable Development Goals.
- Library and Wellbeing integration through resources on conflict resolution and "I statements".
- Teacher reflections highlight the importance of exploring conflict in PE and the growth in students' ability to use respectful language and consider multiple perspectives.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Colonisation has shaped society.
lo: The impact of colonisation; Colonisation stories; The effects of colonisation ??? past, present and future

s.d: Students research key inventions and innovations that resulted from or were impacted by colonisation in Australia and globally. They then build their own 'missing' inventions in Minecraft, justifying how these inventions could have changed the course of colonisation or society. This allows for deep exploration of historical 'what ifs' and creative thinking about technology, culture, and power.

Students investigate the impacts of colonisation by creating layered, annotated maps that compare Indigenous lands before and after European settlement, highlighting changes to geography, place names, and land use. They analyse multiple perspectives by plotting historical events and cultural sites, then present their findings as interactive map stories that reveal how colonisation has shaped society across time and place.

Students create interactive timelines or digital storyboards that map out the stories of colonisation from multiple perspectives?Indigenous, colonisers, and contemporary voices. They can embed images, quotes, and primary sources to visually represent the complexity and ongoing effects of colonisation.

Students collaboratively curate a digital wall of colonisation stories, artefacts, and primary sources. Each student contributes a post from a different perspective (e.g., First Nations, settlers, convicts, or modern descendants), fostering empathy and critical analysis of historical narratives.

Students author and publish digital books that retell colonisation stories?real or imagined?from the viewpoint of people, places, or even non-human entities (like a river or landscape). They can include audio narration, historical images, and reflective questions to deepen understanding.

plannerText: Students explore how colonisation has shaped society by investigating its impacts, comparing stories from multiple perspectives, and analysing effects on people and places past, present, and future. Activities include mapping, creative writing, comparative analysis, and hands-on projects inspired by resources such as the Australian Museum's 'Unsettled' exhibition and texts like 'The Rabbits' by Shaun Tan.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Colonisation has shaped society.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The impact of colonisation; Colonisation stories; The effects of colonisation - past, present and future (people and places)

KEY CONCEPTS: Function, form, connection, perspective

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: 
Learn today from yesterday for a better tomorrow. (2021) Bugia naway gabun buridja ? Uncle Noel Butler, Budawang, Yuin. Carved spotted gum from bushfire ravaged Country. Students engage with resources from the Australian Museum exhibition "Unsettled," including a podcast and visual artefacts. Students define what the term 'unsettled' means to them, identify their understanding of 'Australia's foundation story', and examine history from the perspective of First Nations people. They engage with three objects from the exhibition and visualise the length of time that First Nations peoples have been managing and sustaining Country. Each student plaits three one-metre lengths of fabric (red, black, and yellow), sews them together, and weaves them through the school fence to represent First Nations presence, then weaves red, white, and blue to represent colonisation, with signs explaining the timeline.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Lessons from the "Unsettled" Australian Museum unit, including:
  - Defining and reflecting on the term 'unsettled' and Australia's foundation story.
  - Investigating objects from the exhibition and visualising timelines.
  - Exploring different means of communication, signal fires, and honouring ancestors through artwork.
  - Examining historical sources and artworks for evidence about the past and the impact of terra nullius.
  - Mapping of massacres using the Colonial Frontier Massacres map.
  - Comparing and contrasting colonisation stories from around the world using Venn diagrams and T-charts.
  - Creative writing task about the colonisation of Mars.
  - Mentor text study: "The Rabbits" by Shaun Tan.
  - Poetry related to "Unsettled."
  - Cartography and mapping of the East coast of Australia.
  - Measurement activities from "Unsettled" (length, conversion).
  - Science links: Earth Science (colonisation of weeds), colonisation of Mars.
  - Chinese: Learning geography-related words, iMovie/Keynote projects.
  - Home learning: Create an artwork to honour someone important; compare and contrast texts/images.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Formative assessment tool (Year 5 WWAIPAT Trigraph) at the beginning, middle, and end of the unit.
- Reflections worksheet, investigating objects worksheet, numbers challenge worksheet, signal fire warning worksheet, examining historical sources worksheet, cross-hatching worksheet.
- Note-taking on Venn diagrams and T-charts.
- Written paragraphs comparing and contrasting images/texts.
- Creative writing and visual art pieces.
- Ongoing reflection and discussion.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with Australian Museum's "Unsettled" resources and digital content (podcasts, online exhibitions, interactive maps).
- Opportunities for transdisciplinary connections: English (poetry, creative writing, mentor texts), Maths (measurement, mapping), Science (earth science, colonisation of species), Social Studies (cartography, history), Chinese (geography vocabulary, digital presentations).
- Digital skills: iMovie, Keynote, online research, use of digital maps and timelines.
- Action opportunities: weaving project as a public art installation, home learning projects, and critical reflection on historical perspectives.
- Emphasis on multiple perspectives, especially First Nations voices.
- Suggestion for digital learning coach: support with digital storytelling, interactive mapping, and multimedia presentations.

ci: People's choices are influenced by their beliefs and values and can impact others.
lo: How values and beliefs impact choices; How people's choices and actions impact others; Responsible decision-making

s.d: Students design and script interactive chatbots that simulate real-life ethical dilemmas or value-based scenarios. Using Wise Discussion Chatbots, they can create branching conversations where users must make choices based on different beliefs and values, then reflect on the impact of those choices. This allows students to deeply explore perspective-taking and responsible decision-making in a digital, interactive format.

Students collaboratively build a digital 'Beliefs & Values Wall' using Padlet, where they anonymously post examples of how beliefs and values influence choices in their own lives or in global contexts. They can comment, like, and discuss posts, fostering a safe space for dialogue and reflection on diverse perspectives.

Students use Canva to design persuasive digital posters or infographics that advocate for a value or belief they feel passionate about. They must consider how visual choices (color, imagery, layout) and language can influence others, directly connecting to the unit's focus on the impact of choices and responsible communication.

Students create interactive digital storybooks in Book Creator, narrating a personal or fictional story where a character's beliefs and values influence their decisions and impact others. They can embed audio reflections, images, and hyperlinks to deepen the exploration of consequences and responsibility.

Students explore the 'Active Citizen' Minecraft Education world, learning about Nobel Peace Laureates and how their beliefs and values led to impactful choices. Students then build their own digital monuments or scenarios in Minecraft to represent how values-driven actions can shape communities.

plannerText: The unit 'Who We Are' explores how people's choices are influenced by their beliefs and values and how those choices can impact others. Students engage in reflection, discussion, and creative activities to examine perspective, causation, and responsibility, with explicit connections to social and self-management skills. Learning experiences include portfolio reflections, collaborative discussions, and creative expression in both digital and physical formats.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Canva","d":"Students will create an interactive digital poster or infographic in Canva that explores how personal beliefs and values shape decision-making and the ripple effects of those choices on others. They will interview classmates or family members about a value-driven choice, then visually map out the perspectives, motivations, and impacts using Canva's design tools. plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: People's choices are influenced by their beliefs and values and can impact others.

LINES OF INQUIRY: How values and beliefs impact choices; How people's choices/actions impact others; Responsible decision-making

KEY CONCEPTS: Perspective, causation, responsibility

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: What is self-efficacy and how does it help us become better learners? (Self-Efficacy.pptx). What is positive self-talk? Students explore these concepts as provocations to spark inquiry into how beliefs and values shape choices.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Reflection in music lessons about how personal actions (home practice, behaviour, responsibilities) affect relationships within small instrumental groups.
- In Visual Art, students reflect on how their thoughts about themselves and others affect their choices in creating artwork, focusing on staying on task and building positive work habits.
- In PE, students explore perspective and responsible decision-making through team-based activities and small-sided games, followed by a portfolio reflection task analysing a decision made during a game from another's point of view.
- Inquiry into self-efficacy and positive self-talk.
- Learning engagements are designed to promote agency, with thinking routines, learning outcomes, and success criteria for approaches to learning.
- Students may use a wonderwall or Padlet for question generation.

ASSESSMENT: Portfolio reflection task in PE where students analyse a decision made during a game, reflect on its impact, and consider it from another person's point of view. Ongoing documentation of learning, with opportunities for teacher and peer feedback.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Transdisciplinary connections to Music (reflection on group dynamics), Visual Art (self-perception and work habits), and PE (decision-making, perspective, responsibility).
- Opportunities for digital skills integration (Padlet, Seesaw, or other digital tools for question generation and reflection).
- Social and emotional learning is explicitly referenced in the Wellbeing curriculum.
- Teacher reflection notes highlight the effectiveness of small-sided games and portfolio tasks in PE, and suggest building in more peer feedback opportunities in future iterations.
- Not all subject areas (e.g., Science, Social Studies, Dance & Drama, Languages) have documented connections for this unit.
- The planner encourages flexible grouping, student agency, and universally designed lessons.

ci: An inquiry into the ways in which we discover and express ideas, feelings, nature, culture, beliefs and values.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded
plannerText: Not yet documented in PDF.
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Various student led

LINES OF INQUIRY: Various student led

KEY CONCEPTS: Function, Causation, Change and Responsibility

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Provocation ? PYPEX Launch Day, including a Launch Day Agenda, Launch Day Slides, and a Goose Chase activity. Students identify their passions and interests, complete VIA character strengths survey, and begin inquiry by modeling with teacher examples (e.g., Fast Fashion).

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Students identify personal passions and interests using slide decks and journals.
- Brainstorm positive and negative elements around interests and assign groups.
- Decide on a shared issue for inquiry and create an Essential Agreement.
- Unpack vocabulary and key concepts related to their issue.
- Compile inquiry questions and sort them by key concepts.
- Create lines of inquiry and a central idea for their group inquiry.
- Develop a transdisciplinary theme, Approaches to Learning, and Learner Profile Attributes for their inquiry.
- Create a unit of inquiry wall display (using templates, Canva, or other digital tools).
- Prepare for mentor meetings and complete 360? assessments.
- Research lines of inquiry, analyse elements of a media campaign, and create a media campaign (30+ items per group).
- Brainstorm and plan types of media to create, culminating in a media campaign.
- Engage in ongoing reflection, mentor meetings, and peer feedback.
- Music: Option to choose a music-related topic, compose and perform.
- French: Poetry types linked to individual passion, showcase, and assembly song.
- Chinese: Venn diagram comparing research topics between Australia and China, graduation performances.
- Library: Research and referencing skills, file management, presentation skills.
- Digital platforms: Use of Canva, PowerPoint, and online workspaces for presentations and displays.

ASSESSMENT: 
- 360? assessments (self, peer, teacher feedback)
- Reflection journals
- Media campaign (30+ items per group)
- Exhibition displays and presentations to the community
- Mentor interactions and expert feedback
- Use of digital platforms (Canva, online workspace) to document and share learning

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Student agency is central: students choose topics based on personal passion, design their own inquiries, and set learning goals.
- Strong emphasis on transdisciplinary connections: integration with music, French, Chinese, library, and digital skills.
- Collaboration among Year 6 team and specialists to support inquiry and presentation skills.
- Technology integration: Canva, PowerPoint, online journals, digital workspace for exhibition displays, and communication with mentors.
- Action opportunities: students encouraged to connect inquiry to real-world issues and take action (e.g., advocacy, community engagement).
- Reflection and feedback are ongoing, with regular opportunities for self- and peer-assessment.
- Teacher reflections highlight the importance of inquiry-based learning, student choice, and collaborative projects for deep engagement and understanding.
- Music and arts integration is possible for students who choose those pathways.
- Note: The unit is highly student-driven, so specific content and lines of inquiry will vary by group.

ci: Student-led exhibition (PYPEX) ??? Students develop and present their own units of inquiry.
lo: Student-led transdisciplinary themes; Approaches to learning; Research and presentation skills

s.d: Students use 'Minecraft: Revamp Melbourne' to collaboratively design a future city that models a new system of government, including voting booths, parliamentary chambers, and public spaces. They must explain how their digital city reflects the values, structures, and decision-making processes explored in the unit, and present their solutions to real-world challenges in governance.

Students create persuasive campaign materials (posters, infographics, and social media posts) for a fictional political party or government initiative, using Canva's design tools to visually communicate key values, policies, and the structure of their proposed system.

Students collaboratively build a digital wall on Padlet to map and compare different global political systems, adding research, videos, and debate points. They can comment on each other's posts to simulate parliamentary debate and negotiation.

Students author interactive digital books that explain the process of law-making in Australia, embedding videos, diagrams, and student-recorded interviews with local representatives or experts. The books can be shared with younger students or published for the school community.

Students script, record, and edit a podcast series where they role-play as members of parliament, debating current bills, explaining the preferential voting system, and interviewing 'experts' (classmates) about different government structures and values.

plannerText: This unit explores how values and structures determine how a system of government operates. Students investigate Australia's political system, the law-making process, and compare global political systems through activities such as mock elections, debates, research projects, and hands-on simulations. The unit emphasizes critical thinking, communication, and student agency in understanding governance and civic participation.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA:  
Values and structures determine how a system of government operates.

LINES OF INQUIRY:  
the function of Australia's political system and government structure; the processes involved in creating laws in Australia; different political systems and the values that underpin them

KEY CONCEPTS:  
Change, Connection, Perspective

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN:  
As students enter the classroom, teachers designate them with either a 'pink' or 'white' marshmallow. Each colour represents a different political system. During the lessons, different rules apply for each, depending on the values of each system.  
Lesson 1: Collectively unpack the lines of inquiry and central idea displays. Students complete the Tri-Chart Pre-Assessment in black ink.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS:  
- Watch The Westminster System (ClickView), discuss, and define democracy.  
- Research topics (Monarch in a democracy, Upper/Lower House, Prime Minister, Judiciary, Governor-General, Cabinet) using Parliament and its people - Parliamentary Education Office and record in Dot Jot notes.  
- Watch videos and use mind maps to explore the three levels of government; research and present findings; play a government responsibilities game.  
- Create a triarama detailing the 3 levels of government using provided PPT and resources.  
- Learn about the House of Representatives and Senate through videos and discussion.  
- Watch BTN: Passing a bill in parliament, complete a flow chart, and make a training video for new MPs about law-making.  
- Research roles in parliament and create campaign posters; run through interactive slides.  
- Guided research on preferential voting, create campaign posters for political parties, run a mock election using preferential voting booths and tools.  
- House of Representatives role play and public speaking activities.  
- Participate in a Parliamentary Insiders PEO Digital Incursion (50-minute program on law-making, responsibilities, government formation, accountability).  
- Excursion to Victorian Parliament for a parliamentary role play (students act out roles and pass a law on a selected topic).  
- Research and present on different political systems (democracy, communism, dictatorship, monarchy) using jigsaw format and provided resources.  
- Monarchy lesson and personal inquiry; debate constitutional monarchy vs. republic.  
- Investigate principled and corrupt leaders.  
- Values brainstorm (personal, family, school, national).

ASSESSMENT:  
- Tri-Chart conceptual pre- and post-assessment.  
- Triarama model of government levels.  
- Training video for new MPs on law-making.  
- Campaign posters for political parties.  
- Mock election and preferential voting activity.  
- Student-led research projects on government topics, presented in various formats (persuasive writing, presentations, political cartoons).  
- Peer and self-assessment after conceptual assessments and projects.  
- Public display of projects (triaramas, posters), videos from mock elections and law-making shared at assemblies or digitally, Seesaw uploads, newsletter/WiSE posts.

TEACHER NOTES:  
- Strong integration with maths (preferential voting, fractions/percentages), literacy (debating, persuasive writing), and research skills.  
- Collaboration with drama and ICT specialists for presentations and media.  
- PE links: students explore how rules, roles, and values shape systems in sport and society, reflecting on fairness, collaboration, and decision-making.  
- Student agency supported through choice of research topics, formats for presenting understanding, and participation in mock elections and debates.  
- Feedback is specific and actionable; students engage in self- and peer-assessment.  
- Opportunities for digital learning include creating videos, using online research tools, and sharing work on Seesaw.  
- Reflection notes suggest future focus on deeper global perspectives and more time for group debriefs in PE.  
- Music integration was limited due to scheduling; future units may include more cross-disciplinary links.

ci: Energy may be converted, transformed, and used to support human progress.
lo: Different forms of energy and how they are used; The impact of different energy sources on the planet; Sustainable energy practices

s.d: Students use Micro:bits to design and code interactive models that demonstrate how energy can be monitored and conserved in real-world scenarios. For example, they could create a digital energy meter that tracks 'usage' in a classroom simulation, helping them visualize energy transformation and efficiency. This hands-on coding task directly links to the unit's focus on energy conversion and sustainable practices.

Students explore how solar energy powers the International Space Station and understand how robotic tools help astronauts fix items in space. They can build and test solar panel systems in Minecraft, connecting directly to the unit's exploration of renewable energy technologies and the transformation of energy for human progress.

Students create animated explanations of energy transformations using Explain Everything. They can illustrate and narrate the journey of energy from source (e.g., wind, coal, solar) to end use (e.g., powering a light bulb), making abstract concepts visible and supporting the unit's inquiry into different forms of energy and their applications.

Students script, record, and edit a podcast episode where they debate the pros and cons of renewable versus non-renewable energy sources. This activity encourages research, critical thinking, and communication, while connecting directly to the unit's lines of inquiry about the impact of energy sources on the planet.

Students use Merge Cubes to create and explore 3D models of energy systems (such as wind turbines, solar panels, or electrical circuits). By manipulating these models in augmented reality, students gain a deeper understanding of how energy is converted and transformed, supporting the unit's focus on function and responsibility.

plannerText: The Year 6 'How the World Works' unit explores how energy may be converted, transformed, and used to support human progress. Students investigate different forms of energy, analyze the impact of energy sources on the planet, and examine sustainable energy practices through hands-on experiments, research, and collaborative projects.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Energy may be converted, transformed, and used to support human progress.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Different Forms of Energy and how they are used; The impact of different energy sources on the planet; Sustainable Energy Practices.

KEY CONCEPTS: Perspective, Function, Responsibility

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Students are shown a stimulus video of roller coaster footage to spark curiosity about energy. They discuss how a roller coaster achieves high speeds without an engine, then watch a video titled "What Is Energy?" and complete related worksheets. Students use a 'Zoom in' Thinking Routine with an image to unpack the central idea (see Provocation HTWW.pptx).

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS:
- Introduction to energy types using videos, mind mapping, and daily life examples.
- Investigating energy transformations via Rube Goldberg Machine video and hands-on analysis of toys/devices.
- Exploring the importance of electricity through "The Boy Who Harnessed the Wind" (book, video, and mind mapping).
- Group discussions and scenario analysis about the impact of blackouts.
- Exploring electrical circuits with videos and interactive slides.
- Drawing and explaining William's Dynamo using thinking routines and videos.
- Analysing coal power stations, renewable vs. non-renewable energy sources, and their impacts through videos, diagrams, and PMI charts.
- Comparing state energy sources using data analysis and Venn diagrams.
- Building Solar Buddy lights using the Solar Buddy kit and instructional videos.
- Researching renewable and non-renewable energy, completing PMI charts, and conducting a library inquiry on clean energy.
- Watching the documentary "2040" and taking notes on environmental impacts and sustainable practices.

ASSESSMENT: Summative assessment via SAT HTWW Energy.pptx. Evidence includes student work samples (mind maps, diagrams, experiment reports), group projects (e.g., Solar Buddy lights), presentations, and portfolio reflections. Formative assessments include initial reflections, scenario discussions, and ongoing feedback during activities.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Action opportunities include building on prior learning and encouraging student-initiated action, especially around sustainability.
- Strong transdisciplinary connections: science (energy, electricity), literacy (experiment reports, procedures), HASS (historical, geographical, cultural aspects of energy), maths (data analysis), PSPE (energy transfer in movement), and visual art.
- Integration with digital tools: Use of ClickView, online videos, digital mind maps, Seesaw for sharing, and digital workspaces for project documentation.
- Excursions and guest speakers are not specifically mentioned, but hands-on kits (Solar Buddy), documentaries, and interactive digital resources are central.
- Teacher reflection notes suggest ordering DC motors, batteries, and wires for future hands-on construction tasks, and highlight the value of practical, inquiry-driven, and collaborative learning experiences.

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways.
lo: How community values shape the management of the natural environment; The need for sustainable practices; Taking action for environmental stewardship

s.d: Students use Minecraft Education to explore the complex issues of habitat destruction and land-use conflict between humans and elephants. They will analyse real-world case studies, design and trial solutions in Minecraft, and assess their effectiveness, directly connecting to the unit's focus on how communities interact with and impact the environment.

Students collaboratively build a digital wall documenting examples of local and global environmental stewardship, including Indigenous land management, conservation efforts, and community action. Each student posts research, images, and reflections, creating a living resource that demonstrates diverse community-environment interactions.

Students design persuasive digital posters or infographics advocating for specific conservation actions or environmental stewardship, using evidence from their case studies and camp experiences. These can be shared with the school community to inspire real-world action.

In small groups, students script and record short podcasts interviewing 'experts' (role-played by peers or teachers) about successful community-environment partnerships, Indigenous practices, or conservation challenges. Episodes are published for the school community, building communication and research skills.

Students create interactive 3D models of local ecosystems or conservation projects using Merge Cubes. They annotate their models with audio or text explaining how different communities interact with these environments, highlighting both positive and negative impacts.

plannerText: The Year 6 'Sharing the Planet' unit centres on how communities interact with the environment in different ways, with a strong focus on environmental stewardship, Indigenous practices, and conservation. Key activities include immersive experiences at Camp Mallana, case study analysis of community-environment relationships, reflective journaling, and student-led action projects promoting sustainability and advocacy.
