(https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=musical-microbit-unit-overview)","url":""},{"t":"Emotion Mirrors: Interactive Self-Portraits","d":"Students explore self-expression by designing and building interactive self-portraits that respond to touch or movement. Using Makey Makey kits (or micro:bit touch sensors), students craft physical portraits from recycled cardboard, fabric, yarn, and mixed media. They embed conductive materials (aluminum foil, copper tape) into their art, connecting facial features or accessories. When touched, these trigger digital soundscapes, short poems, or light patterns (programmed on Scratch or micro:bit) that express different emotions or moods. Students follow the Design Cycle: empathise (explore feelings and artistic styles), define (choose emotions to express), ideate (sketch interactive features), prototype (build and wire up the portraits), test (share and refine), and reflect. The final gallery lets visitors interact with each portrait, experiencing the creator's unique artistic and emotional expression through both physical art and digital response.","url":""}]

ci: Communities create rules, laws and services to help people live together fairly and safely.
lo: Effective and influential leadership; Strengths that help leaders guide others; How leadership impacts communities

s.d: Students will use Minecraft Education's 'Revamp Melbourne' lesson to collaboratively redesign a part of Melbourne, focusing on how rules, laws, and services shape fair and safe communities. They will justify their design choices and reflect on how their virtual city meets the needs of its citizens, directly linking to the central idea of community organisation.

Students create a digital 'Community Noticeboard' using Padlet, posting examples of local rules, laws, and services they research or observe in their neighbourhoods. They can comment on each other's posts, discuss fairness and safety, and propose improvements, fostering real-world connections to the unit.

Students script and record short podcasts interviewing 'community leaders' (role-played by peers or actual guests) about the importance of rules and services. They edit and publish episodes, building communication skills and deepening understanding of how leadership and laws impact daily life.

Students design and distribute a digital survey using Microsoft Forms to gather data from peers or family about which community services are most valued and why. They analyse the results in class, discussing how community needs drive the creation of rules and services.

Students use Canva to design infographics or posters explaining a specific community rule, law, or service, highlighting its purpose and impact. They can use the 'Remove Background' feature to add photos of their local area, making the project visually engaging and relevant.

plannerText: This unit explores how communities create rules, laws, and services to help people live together fairly and safely. Students investigate effective leadership, the function of organisations, and the impact of societal decision-making through scenarios, guest speakers, team-building activities, and persuasive speeches for leadership roles.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students design interactive 3D models of community spaces (like a council chamber or emergency services hub) using Merge Cubes, then create an augmented reality 'choose your own adventure' story in Book Creator where users make decisions as community leaders, exploring the impact of different rules and services on fairness and safety.","url":""},{"t":"Microsoft Forms","d":"Students collaboratively design and distribute a digital survey to gather data from peers and families about which community rules and services are most valued, then analyse the results to inform a class debate on effective community organisation.","url":""},{"t":"Sphero BOLT","d":"Students program Sphero BOLT robots to simulate emergency response missions, navigating obstacle courses that represent community challenges. They design routes that reflect how rules and services help keep people safe, collaborating to demonstrate leadership and problem-solving in real-world scenarios. This hands-on activity brings the unit?s focus on community organisation and safety to life through engaging robotics.","url":""},{"t":"Canva","d":"Students work in teams to design persuasive digital posters or infographics advocating for a new community service or law, using Canva's design tools and 'Remove Background' feature to create visually compelling arguments.","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students explore the stories of Nobel Peace Laureates and build their own virtual community in Minecraft Education, making decisions about rules, laws, and services to ensure fairness and safety for all citizens.","url":"https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/active-citizen"},{"t":"Justice in Action: Community Courtroom Makerspace","d":"Students work in teams to design, build, and role-play a miniature courtroom and town square using cardboard, recycled materials, and craft supplies. Each team creates physical models of key community buildings (court, council, police station, etc.), then codes a Sphero Indi or Bee-Bot as the 'citizen' navigating real-life rule or law scenarios (e.g., resolving a dispute, reporting a problem). Teams use iPads to document their process and record mock 'court cases' where they debate and enact fair solutions, integrating digital storytelling with hands-on construction and collaborative problem-solving.","url":""}]

ci: The design and use of products can have an impact on the natural world.
lo: How material properties determine their use; Material solutions for a sustainable future; How research solves problems in the natural world

s.d: Students use Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app to investigate the properties of different materials in 3D, then create an interactive AR showcase comparing sustainable and non-sustainable materials. Students record their findings in short video explanations using the iPad camera and upload to Seesaw for peer review.

Students document their science experiments and research on material properties, decomposition, and sustainable solutions by creating a multimedia science journal in Book Creator. They include photos, videos, diagrams, and reflections to track their learning journey throughout the unit.

Students design infographics in Canva to communicate the environmental impact of different materials used in everyday products. They use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to create product cutaways and visually highlight sustainable alternatives.

s.t: Minecraft: Its Good To Be Green
s.d: Students explore a sustainable building in Minecraft Education, analyzing how design choices impact the environment. They reflect on how these principles could be applied to their own enrichment toy prototypes and document their ideas in a digital portfolio.

Students conduct a survey using Microsoft Forms to gather data on classmates' and families' choices around product materials and recycling habits. They analyze the results in Excel to identify trends and present their findings to the class.

plannerText: Students investigate how material properties determine their use, explore sustainable solutions, and research how products impact the natural world. They conduct hands-on science experiments, document findings in digital journals, and design, build, and test enrichment toys for animals using sustainable materials.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students design interactive 3D models of their animal enrichment prototypes using Merge Cubes, then document the design process, material choices, and environmental impact in a digital science journal on Book Creator. This app smash allows students to virtually 'hold' and explore their creations while reflecting on sustainable design choices.","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students explore a sustainable building in Minecraft Education, analyzing how material choices and design decisions impact the environment. They can then reflect on how these principles could be applied to their own enrichment toy prototypes.","url":"https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/its-good-to-be-green"},{"t":"Micro:bit","d":"Students investigate sustainable product design by building and testing prototypes with Micro:bit, using the 'Electrical Conductors' lesson (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=electrical-conductors-unit-of-work). They explore how material properties affect conductivity and product function, linking science, design, and environmental impact. This hands-on challenge makes STEM learning relevant and exciting.","url":""},{"t":"Sphero BOLT","d":"Students program Sphero BOLT robots to simulate animal movement and interaction with different enrichment toy prototypes. This hands-on coding activity helps them test and iterate their designs for functionality and animal engagement.","url":""},{"t":"Lego Spike Prime","d":"Students design and build animal enrichment devices using Lego Spike Prime, exploring how different material properties affect their creations? functionality and sustainability. They program their models to simulate animal interactions, testing how their designs respond to movement and behaviour patterns. This hands-on activity deepens understanding of product design impact on the natural world while fostering creativity and computational thinking.","url":""},{"t":"Eco Enrichment Arcade","d":"Students use the Design Cycle to invent and build interactive animal enrichment games inspired by arcade machines, focusing on sustainability and animal wellbeing. Working in teams, students brainstorm animal needs, sketch ideas, and prototype enrichment devices using recycled cardboard, plastic bottles, fabric, string, and natural materials. They integrate programmable Bee-Bots or Sphero Indi robots as 'moving parts'?for example, a Bee-Bot could trigger a treat dispenser or reveal a hidden toy when an animal interacts with the device. Teams test and refine their creations, documenting iterations in their digital science journals, and reflect on how their material choices impact both function and environmental footprint.","url":""}]

ci: Country, language and community are significant to the lives of First Nations people.
lo: Diversity of First Nations people; Importance of community, family and traditions; Importance of Country to First Nations people

s.d: Students use Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app to create interactive 3D representations of significant First Nations sites, languages, or cultural artifacts. They can record audio explanations in local Indigenous languages or English, and classmates can explore these virtual objects by rotating the cubes, deepening understanding of Country and community.

Students write and illustrate their own Dreamtime-inspired stories, incorporating research about First Nations traditions, Country, and language. They can embed audio recordings of themselves reading in English and, where possible, in a local Indigenous language, then publish and share these digital books with younger students.

Using Canva, students design visual infographics or posters that highlight the diversity of First Nations communities, languages, and connections to Country. They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to incorporate their own photos of artwork or excursions, and add QR codes linking to further resources or student-created videos.

Students collaboratively build a digital map or timeline on Padlet, pinning stories, artworks, and facts about different First Nations groups across Australia. Each post can include images, student reflections, and links to Dreamtime stories or interviews with elders, fostering a sense of shared inquiry.

Students use 'Minecraft: Revamp Melbourne' (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/revamp-melbourne) to design a future Melbourne that respects and integrates First Nations perspectives on Country, language, and community. They must explain how their builds reflect Indigenous values and sustainable practices.

plannerText: Students explore the significance of Country, language, and community to First Nations people through Dreamtime stories, art analysis, excursions (e.g., CERES, Yiramalay elders), and hands-on activities. They create narratives, artworks, and dioramas, engage with elders, and reflect on physical, cultural, and spiritual connections to Country.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students create interactive Dreamtime stories or virtual dioramas using Merge Cubes, overlaying 3D symbols, animals, and landscapes significant to First Nations cultures. They then app smash by documenting their creative process and cultural reflections in Book Creator, combining AR visuals, text, and audio explanations to deepen understanding of Country, language, and community.","url":""},{"t":"Canva","d":"Design a collaborative digital mural in Canva where each student contributes a panel representing an aspect of First Nations identity, such as language, Country, or community. Use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to incorporate student photos or artwork, and layer in authentic Indigenous symbols and patterns for a visually rich, respectful mural.","url":""},{"t":"Padlet","d":"Build a living digital map on Padlet where students pin locations of different First Nations groups, add audio snippets of local languages, and share research on traditions, family structures, and connections to Country. Encourage students to comment and ask questions to foster inquiry and respectful dialogue.","url":""},{"t":"Seesaw","d":"Students record video reflections or interviews with family members about their own connections to place, language, and community, then compare and contrast with First Nations perspectives. Use Seesaw to curate a class gallery of these stories, highlighting diversity and shared values.","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students explore how First Nations peoples' knowledge of Country supports biodiversity by building 'beetopias' in Minecraft Education. They apply Indigenous perspectives on land stewardship and sustainable habitats, then reflect on the importance of community and tradition in environmental care.","url":"https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/build-with-bees-habitat"},{"t":"Dreamtime Waterways: Interactive River Journey","d":"Students work in teams to design and build a large-scale, winding river diorama using recycled materials, clay, cardboard, natural objects, and paint, representing the importance of waterways in First Nations cultures. Along the river, students craft significant landmarks, flora, and fauna, inspired by Dreamtime stories and Indigenous art. Integrating Sphero Indi robots, students code cultural storytelling 'journeys'?the robots follow hand-crafted, painted paths along the river, stopping at key points where QR codes or NFC tags (embedded in crafted rocks or trees) trigger audio narrations, student-created digital artworks, or Dreamtime story segments on iPads. The project blends physical making, coding, and digital storytelling, deepening understanding of Indigenous connections to Country and water.","url":""}]

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration leads to discoveries, opportunities and new understandings.
lo: Reasons for exploration; How people have explored over time; Consequences of exploration

s.d: Students research a significant historical explorer or invention that changed the course of exploration. Using Minecraft: The Hall of Inventions, they design and build a 3D model of their chosen invention or a scene from a key exploration, then present their build to the class, explaining its historical impact and how it led to new understandings.

Students plot the journeys of famous explorers on Google Maps, annotating key stops with text, images, or links. They compare historical routes with modern geography, discussing how technology has changed our ability to explore and understand the world.

Students create a digital journal in Book Creator, documenting their own 'exploration' of a chosen topic, place, or historical period. They include multimedia elements such as photos, voice recordings, and maps to reflect on discoveries and new understandings.

Using Canva, students design an interactive timeline or infographic that illustrates the evolution of exploration methods and technologies over time, highlighting key discoveries and their consequences.

Students collaborate on a Padlet wall to share research about different explorers from various cultures and eras. Each student posts a summary, image, and a reflection on how that explorer's journey led to new opportunities or understandings.

plannerText: This unit engages students in investigating the reasons for exploration, how people have explored across history, and the consequences of exploration. Activities include mapping journeys, researching explorers, writing historical narratives, and hands-on projects that connect exploration to discovery and new understandings.
humanVerifiedResetReason: Suggestion edited, swapped, regenerated or changed after human verification

ci: People's beliefs contribute to the diversity of human life.
lo: Spiritual or religious beliefs; Similarities and differences of spiritual beliefs; Contributions of different religions to human diversity

s.d: Students design and script interactive chatbots that simulate respectful interfaith dialogues. Using Wise Discussion Chatbots, students can program scenarios where users ask questions about different spiritual or religious beliefs, and the chatbot responds with accurate, inclusive information. This fosters empathy, digital citizenship, and a nuanced understanding of diversity in beliefs.

Students create visually engaging infographics or digital posters comparing symbols, places of worship, and practices from different world religions. Canva's 'Remove Background' feature can be used to highlight key religious artifacts or figures, supporting visual literacy and cross-cultural understanding.

Students author a digital 'Beliefs Around the World' book, compiling research, reflections, and interviews with family or community members about their beliefs. They can include audio recordings, images, and text, making the book accessible and multimodal.

Students curate a playlist of short documentary clips from Clickview that showcase rituals, festivals, and places of worship from various religions. They annotate each clip with their own reflections and questions, then share the playlist with peers for discussion.

Students collaboratively build a digital 'Belief Wall' on Padlet, posting images, facts, and personal stories about spiritual or religious diversity in their class and community. They can comment on each other's posts, fostering respectful dialogue and inquiry.

plannerText: This unit explores how people's beliefs contribute to the diversity of human life, focusing on spiritual or religious beliefs, similarities and differences among religions, and the contributions of these beliefs to human diversity. Key activities include gallery walks of religious imagery, question walls for different religions, research and information reports on world religions, excursions to places of worship, and the creation of cultural posters and information reports.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students create interactive 3D Merge Cube experiences representing different places of worship or symbols from world religions. They then app smash by embedding videos, audio reflections, and AR screenshots into a digital Book Creator 'Belief Museum', allowing students to virtually 'hold' and explore diverse beliefs in their hands.","url":""},{"t":"Adobe Express Podcasting","d":"Students create a series of short podcasts where they interview family members or classmates about their spiritual or religious beliefs and traditions, then reflect on how these contribute to human diversity. Learners script questions, record audio, and edit their episodes to share respectful stories that deepen intercultural understanding and empathy.","url":"https://podcast.adobe.com"},{"t":"Canva","d":"Students design visually stunning infographics comparing the core beliefs, symbols, and practices of major world religions. Use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to create digital collages of places of worship, and present findings in a class 'Belief Gallery Walk'.","url":""},{"t":"Clickview","d":"Curate a playlist of short documentaries and virtual tours of places of worship from Clickview. Students use the platform's interactive video features to annotate, ask questions, and reflect on similarities and differences between spiritual practices.","url":""},{"t":"Seesaw","d":"Students record video interviews with family members or community leaders about their beliefs and traditions using the iPad camera. They upload and annotate these interviews in Seesaw, tagging key vocabulary and reflecting on how beliefs shape identity.","url":""},{"t":"Beliefs Bridges: Connecting Cultures Construction","d":"Students work in teams to research different world religions and spiritual beliefs, identifying key symbols, artefacts, and values. Using recycled materials (cardboard, fabric, string, clay, etc.), each group physically builds a symbolic 'island' representing a belief system?incorporating meaningful structures, colours, and objects. Next, teams collaboratively design and construct 'bridges' (using craft sticks, LEGO, or connectors) that link the islands, symbolizing shared values or respectful connections between beliefs. The bridges feature simple programmable elements: students embed Sphero Indi cars or similar robots, coding them with colour tiles to travel across the bridges and stop at key points where students record short explanations (using iPads to trigger audio via QR codes or NFC tags attached to the bridges). This hands-on project blends physical creativity, digital storytelling, and robotics, helping students visualize and celebrate the diversity and interconnectedness of human beliefs.","url":""}]

ci: People design for purpose.
lo: How design thinking creates in various ways; Design thinking meets community needs; Effective ways to engage an audience

s.d: Students use Minecraft: Education Edition to collaboratively design and build innovative solutions for Melbourne's future needs, directly connecting to the unit's focus on purposeful design and engaging with real-world community challenges. They must justify their design choices and reflect on how their creations meet current and future needs, mirroring the design thinking process.

Students script, record, and edit a podcast episode where they pitch their design solution for a chosen natural disaster scenario, using persuasive language and sound effects to engage an audience. This task supports the unit's emphasis on effective communication and pitching ideas.

Students create digital infographics or persuasive posters to visually communicate the impact of their design solution, integrating data, empathy maps, and stakeholder perspectives. Canva's 'Remove Background' feature can be used to enhance visuals without a green screen.

Students storyboard and animate the journey of their design from ideation to prototype, narrating the design thinking process and highlighting how their solution addresses community needs. This supports reflection and clear communication of process and purpose.

Students collaboratively document their research, empathy maps, and feedback cycles on a shared Padlet wall, enabling real-time peer feedback and iterative improvement of their design solutions. This creates a transparent record of the design thinking journey.

plannerText: Year 5 students at Glen Waverley explore 'How We Express Ourselves' through the central idea 'People design for purpose.' The unit immerses students in the design thinking process as they investigate natural disasters, research community needs, and collaboratively prototype innovative solutions. Activities include mapping, data analysis, empathy mapping, ideation, prototyping, pitching, and reflection, with strong links to UNICEF's child rights and real-world problem solving.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students design and prototype interactive 3D models of disaster-resistant shelters or emergency tools using Tinkercad, then bring their creations to life with Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app. They can record AR video walkthroughs using the iPad camera, narrating their design thinking and how their solution meets a specific community need. This immersive, hands-on workflow makes abstract design concepts tangible and engaging.","url":""},{"t":"Canva","d":"Students create persuasive digital pitch decks for their UNICEF-inspired design solutions, using Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to showcase their prototypes and integrate infographics, maps, and empathy maps. This supports the pitch-writing and presentation phase, allowing students to visually communicate their process and impact.","url":""},{"t":"Micro:bit","d":"Students become hazard heroes by designing and coding disaster response devices with Micro:bit, inspired by the 'Night Safety' lesson (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=night-safety). They create wearable alerts or safety systems, connecting their design thinking to real-world community needs and resilience. This project brings STEM and social responsibility together in a dynamic, student-led way.","url":""},{"t":"Book Creator","d":"Teams publish interactive digital journals in Book Creator, combining photos, videos, voice recordings, and diagrams to document each stage of the design thinking process. This supports reflection, self-assessment, and sharing with the wider school community.","url":""},{"t":"Lego Spike Prime","d":"Students collaborate in teams to design and program Lego Spike Prime robots that simulate emergency response tasks related to natural hazards. They create functional prototypes that demonstrate purposeful design and problem-solving, applying the design thinking process to meet community needs. This hands-on activity brings the unit?s central idea to life by combining creativity, coding, and teamwork in authentic scenarios.","url":""},{"t":"Hazard Heroes: Rescue Robotics Challenge","d":"Students work in teams to identify a natural hazard affecting a community (e.g., flood, earthquake, bushfire) and design a hands-on rescue mission scenario. Using Sphero Indi robots as 'rescue vehicles,' students physically build a detailed hazard landscape with recycled materials?such as rivers, collapsed bridges, blocked roads, and shelters?on large cardboard bases. They craft movable barriers, construct safe zones, and create obstacles that mirror real-world challenges. Teams then program their Sphero Indi to autonomously navigate the custom-built terrain, deliver 'emergency supplies' (represented by small crafted tokens), and reach affected areas. Throughout, students document their design thinking process, test and iterate their physical and digital prototypes, and present their final rescue solutions to an audience, explaining how their design addresses both the hazard and community needs.","url":""}]

ci: Economic systems are an integral part of human society.
lo: Wealth is more than money; Responsibilities when managing income; Income is earned and managed in many ways

s.d: Students collaborate in Minecraft Education to redesign Melbourne for the future, focusing on how economic systems shape city infrastructure, trade, and community services. They must justify their design choices based on economic principles, simulate trade, and reflect on how their solutions meet both current and future needs of a diverse society.

Students create digital infographics or animated explainers that break down complex economic concepts such as taxation, budgeting, or the difference between needs and wants. These can be shared with the school community to promote financial literacy and spark discussion about economic responsibility.

Students use Excel to model personal and community budgets, track classroom 'income' and 'expenses' from simulated jobs or side hustles, and visualize data with graphs. This hands-on approach helps them understand financial decision-making and the impact of economic choices.

Students collaboratively build a digital 'idea wall' to share and debate what constitutes wealth, how income is managed, and the responsibilities of citizens in economic systems. They can post reflections, questions, and multimedia responses to real-world economic scenarios.

Students author interactive digital books that tell the story of a fictional character navigating different economic systems, making choices about earning, spending, and saving. They can embed audio reflections, videos, and images to bring economic concepts to life.

plannerText: This unit explores how economic systems underpin human society, with students investigating concepts of wealth, income, and financial responsibility. Activities include provocations about what makes someone 'wealthy', debates on tax, simulated classroom economies, budgeting exercises, and the collaborative design of ideal communities within set financial constraints.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students design and build interactive 3D models of their 'ideal community' or economic system using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app. They then use the iPad camera to record a virtual guided tour, narrating how wealth, resources, and responsibilities are distributed in their imagined society. This merges tactile, spatial, and digital storytelling for a futuristic approach to economic systems.","url":""},{"t":"Freeform","d":"Students collaborate on a dynamic Freeform board to visually map and categorise diverse forms of wealth beyond money, using images, text, and drawings to represent their ideas about economic systems. They organise their thinking around the unit?s central idea by connecting personal, social, and cultural examples of wealth, creating a rich, interactive digital mural that evolves with their inquiry.","url":""},{"t":"Adobe Express Podcasting","d":"Students produce an 'Economic Voices' podcast, simulating interviews with historical and contemporary figures about economic systems, wealth, and responsibilities. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), they script, record, and edit episodes that bring economic concepts to life through storytelling and debate.","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students explore the evolution of economic systems by visiting the virtual museum in Minecraft: The Hall of Inventions. They research a key economic innovation (e.g., currency, banking, trade networks), then build their own invention in the museum, explaining its impact on society and connecting it to their classroom inquiry. This fosters historical perspective and creative application.","url":"https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/challenges/the-hall-of-inventions"},{"t":"Microsoft Excel","d":"Students use Excel to model personal or community budgets, experimenting with different income sources, expenses, and savings strategies. They create interactive spreadsheets that visually demonstrate the consequences of financial decisions, supporting critical thinking about economic responsibility and resource allocation.","url":""},{"t":"Wealth Web: Community Resource Exchange Game","d":"Students use the Design Cycle to collaboratively invent and build a hands-on board game that models how communities organise, share, and grow different forms of wealth (not just money). In teams, they design a 3D 'community map' using cardboard, clay, and recycled materials to represent neighbourhoods, resources (like skills, time, food, knowledge), and opportunities. They then integrate programmable Sphero Indi robots as 'community couriers'?students code them to follow colour-coded tracks between locations, simulating the flow of resources and jobs. Game mechanics, designed and prototyped by students, include physical tokens, custom dice, and event cards, all crafted by hand. The final game is play-tested by peers, requiring players to cooperate, trade, and solve challenges to build a thriving, equitable community?making abstract economic concepts tangible and dynamic through creative making and digital-physical integration.","url":""}]

ci: The interactions between living things, materials, and light enable us to make decisions about the world
lo: How structural features and adaptations help living things survive; How weathering and erosion cause change to Earth's surface; How light is used in science and technology

s.d: Students will use Minecraft Education to explore the concepts of magnetism and gravity by designing a magnetic structure that could protect the International Space Station or assist its astronauts. This hands-on digital simulation directly connects to the unit's focus on the interactions between materials and forces, allowing students to experiment with invisible forces in a creative, collaborative virtual environment.

Students use Merge Cubes to create and explore interactive 3D models demonstrating how light interacts with different materials (transparent, translucent, opaque) and how these interactions affect living things. They can record their observations and share digital walkthroughs of their models, making abstract science concepts tangible and engaging.

Students collaboratively create interactive whiteboard presentations to model scenarios such as how light enables plants to grow (photosynthesis), or how materials are used in disaster-resistant building designs. They can annotate, animate, and narrate their explanations, supporting both science understanding and communication skills.

Students program Sphero BOLT robots to navigate obstacle courses that simulate natural hazards (e.g., bushfires, floods, earthquakes). They collect data on speed, distance, and light sensor readings, connecting robotics to real-world problem-solving and the unit's focus on decision-making in response to environmental challenges.

Students design persuasive infographics or digital posters using Canva to communicate the importance of making informed decisions about materials and light in disaster preparedness (e.g., bushfire-resistant materials, reflective surfaces for heat management). They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to enhance visuals with their own iPad photos.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 5 students in understanding natural hazards (bushfires, earthquakes, floods) and how the interactions between living things, materials, and light inform decision-making. Students engage in collaborative inquiry, data analysis, mapping, prototyping, and persuasive pitching, culminating in designing and presenting solutions to real-world challenges with a strong focus on empathy, scientific thinking, and the design process.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students create interactive 3D models of natural disasters (e.g., bushfires, earthquakes, floods) using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app, then record AR video explanations using the iPad camera. They can app smash with Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to place themselves inside their AR scenes, narrating the science and human impact for a truly immersive digital artifact.","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students use Minecraft Education's 'Invisible Forces At Work' lesson to explore magnetism and gravity, then design a magnetic structure that could protect the International Space Station or assist astronauts. This directly connects to the unit's focus on the interactions between materials and forces in the real world.","url":"https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/invisible-forces-at-work"},{"t":"Micro:bit","d":"Students engineer early warning wearables for natural disasters using Micro:bit, guided by the 'Night Safety' lesson (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=night-safety). They code devices that simulate alerts for bushfires, floods, or earthquakes, linking science, technology, and community safety. This hands-on project empowers students to turn empathy into action through innovation.","url":""},{"t":"Padlet","d":"Students collaboratively create a digital 'Wonder Wall' on Padlet by posting questions, research findings, and Clickview video clips about natural hazards such as bushfires, earthquakes, and floods. They use Padlet?s mapping feature to build a labelled visual map that tracks the locations and impacts of these disasters across Australia, connecting directly to the unit?s central idea about interactions between living things, materials, and light. This activity supports inquiry into how natural forces cause change to Earth's surface and helps students analyse data to make informed decisions about protecting communities.","url":""},{"t":"Microsoft Excel","d":"Students collect and organise data on natural disasters such as bushfires, floods, and earthquakes using Microsoft Excel. They create dynamic charts and graphs to visualise patterns and trends, helping them analyse risks and make informed decisions about community safety. This hands-on activity connects the central idea by exploring how data-driven insights support disaster preparedness and resilience.","url":""},{"t":"Disaster Early Warning Wearables","d":"Students use the Design Cycle to invent and prototype wearable early warning devices that help communities respond to bushfires, earthquakes, or floods. First, they research real-world warning systems and brainstorm features that could alert and protect children during disasters. In teams, students physically craft wearable prototypes (e.g., wristbands, badges, or headbands) using materials like felt, recycled plastics, Velcro, LEDs, conductive thread, and cardboard. They integrate a simple programmable sensor (such as a Makey Makey or Circuit Playground Express) to simulate detecting a hazard?triggering lights, sounds, or vibrations. Students code their device (e.g., using block-based coding) to respond to 'danger signals' (like shaking for earthquakes or a heat source for bushfires). The project culminates in a showcase where students pitch their wearable's design, demo its warning features, and reflect on how it could uphold children's rights and safety in disaster-prone areas.","url":""}]

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways.
lo: The need for conservation; Community values inform management of the natural environment; Ways environmental stewardship can drive change

s.d: Students use Minecraft Education to design and build a future-focused, sustainable version of Melbourne, integrating real-world environmental stewardship and community values. They must justify their design choices based on local council initiatives and conservation needs, then present their virtual city to the class as a persuasive pitch for change.

Students create persuasive digital campaigns (posters, infographics, or short videos) advocating for a local environmental initiative inspired by Monash Council programs. They use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to integrate photos from their nature walk and overlay key conservation messages.

Students collaboratively build a digital mind map of local council sustainability initiatives, linking each to its potential impact on the community and environment. They add research, photos, and reflections from their council investigation and nature walk.

Students author a digital book documenting their inquiry into First Nations stewardship, local flora and fauna, and their own conservation proposals. They combine text, images from the iPad camera, and audio reflections to demonstrate understanding of community-environment interactions.

Students curate and annotate a playlist of short videos on conservation, local council actions, and environmental stewardship. They add their own video reflections explaining how each resource connects to the central idea and propose actions for their school community.

plannerText: Students explore how communities interact with the environment through conservation, local values, and stewardship. They investigate First Nations perspectives, research local council sustainability initiatives, conduct a nature walk to observe local flora and fauna, and develop persuasive pitches for environmental action within their school community.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students create interactive 3D models of local flora and fauna using the Merge Cube and iPads, then use the Merge Explorer app to overlay digital information about conservation needs and stewardship actions. They can record video tours of their models using the iPad camera, narrating how their community can interact with and protect these species.","url":""},{"t":"Padlet","d":"Students collaboratively research and document local council sustainability initiatives on Padlet, then design persuasive digital posters in Canva to promote their chosen initiative to the school community, integrating real data and student-generated visuals.","url":""},{"t":"Book Creator","d":"After a nature walk around the school, students use Book Creator to compile a multimedia field guide of native plants and animals, including photos, audio reflections, and conservation tips tailored to their local environment.","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students explore how bees interact with their environment by applying their knowledge of stewardship and conservation to design and build digital 'beetopias' in Minecraft, supporting thriving bee colonies and reflecting on the importance of biodiversity.","url":"https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/build-with-bees-habitat"},{"t":"Micro:bit","d":"Students program Micro:bit devices to collect and display real-time environmental data, such as temperature or moisture levels, simulating how communities monitor local ecosystems. They create interactive models that demonstrate the impact of conservation efforts on their local environment, linking directly to the unit?s focus on stewardship and community values. This hands-on coding activity encourages scientific inquiry and environmental responsibility.","url":""},{"t":"Living Waterways: Interactive River Ecosystem","d":"Students work in teams to design and construct a large, modular river ecosystem model using recycled cardboard, clay, fabric, and natural materials. They research local waterways and First Nations perspectives to inform the ecosystem's features?such as wetlands, native plant zones, and animal habitats. Integrating Sphero Indi robots, students program the robots to represent 'water flow' or animal migration, navigating through their hand-built landscape. As part of the Design Cycle, students iteratively test and refine their physical models to ensure the 'water' can move through the system without blockages, simulating the impact of pollution, conservation efforts, and community interventions. The final ecosystem includes interactive elements (e.g., removable pollution sources, movable animals, or plant growth markers) and is presented to the school community with student-led explanations of conservation choices and stewardship strategies.","url":""}]

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: The discovery of resources has impacted the growth and development of nations.
lo: Significance of resource discovery in development of societies; Impact of migration on cultural, social and economic growth; Exploration and settlement effects on Indigenous and colonial populations

s.d: Students research a key resource discovery (e.g., gold, wool, wheat, or minerals) that shaped Australia's growth, then use Minecraft: The Hall of Inventions to design and build a 3D model of the invention or technology that enabled its extraction or use. Students present their build as a virtual museum exhibit, explaining its historical impact on Australia's development.

Students create interactive digital timelines using Canva, mapping out major resource discoveries in Australia and their ripple effects on migration, Indigenous communities, and economic growth. Each event can include images, short videos, and student-written summaries to deepen understanding of cause and effect.

Students collaboratively build a Padlet 'Migration Wall' where they post digital artefacts (photos, maps, quotes, short audio clips) representing different migration waves to Australia, linking each to the discovery of resources and the resulting cultural, social, and economic changes.

Students use Explain Everything to create animated explainer videos that unpack the push and pull factors of migration during the Gold Rush and other resource booms, integrating maps, voiceovers, and annotated diagrams to show how resource discovery transformed Australian society.

Students curate and annotate a playlist of historical video clips from Clickview, focusing on the Gold Rush, Eureka Stockade, and other pivotal moments. They add their own commentary and discussion prompts to highlight how these events shaped the nation's growth and identity.

plannerText: Students explore how the discovery of resources such as gold, wool, and wheat impacted the growth and development of Australia, with a focus on migration, economic change, and effects on Indigenous and colonial populations. Activities include historical research, case studies, timelines, perspective-taking, and hands-on prototyping to understand the significance of resource discovery in shaping societies.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students create interactive 3D artefacts representing key resources discovered in Australia (e.g., gold, wheat, wool) using Merge Cube. They then app smash by embedding their 3D models and explanatory videos into a digital Book Creator journal, allowing for immersive storytelling about how each resource shaped the nation's growth.","url":""},{"t":"Padlet","d":"Launch a collaborative migration map: students research and post digital 'story cards' on a Padlet world map, each card representing a migrant group or individual who contributed to Australia's development, including their reasons for migration and the impact on society.","url":""},{"t":"Adobe Express Podcasting","d":"Learners create a Gold Rush podcast series, narrating stories from the perspectives of migrants, Indigenous Australians, and colonial settlers. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), students blend research, interviews, and dramatic storytelling to explore how resource discovery shaped nations and changed lives.","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students explore the Minecraft: The Hall of Inventions world, then select a historical Australian invention or resource. They research its significance and build a new exhibit in Minecraft, presenting how this resource or invention contributed to national growth.","url":"https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/challenges/the-hall-of-inventions"},{"t":"Micro:bit","d":"Students program Micro:bit devices to simulate communication methods used during the Gold Rush, creating interactive messages that represent how prospectors and settlers shared information about resources and migration. They design simple code to send alerts or directions, connecting technological innovation to the social and economic growth of nations. This hands-on activity brings history and coding together, deepening understanding of the unit?s central idea.","url":""},{"t":"Gold Rush Innovation Trail: Interactive Prospecting Adventure","d":"Students work in teams to design and build a large, physical 'prospecting trail' inspired by Australia's goldfields, using recycled materials, cardboard, clay, and fabric to create realistic terrain, dig sites, rivers, and historical landmarks. Integrating Ozobot robots, students program them to represent gold-seekers navigating the trail, encountering challenges such as Indigenous land boundaries, environmental obstacles, and encounters with other migrants. Each stop features student-made, interactive artifacts (e.g., pop-up tents, sluice boxes, mine entrances) that trigger physical mechanisms (levers, pulleys) or QR codes for digital mini-narratives. The project follows the Design Cycle: students research gold rush history, ideate trail features and challenges, prototype structures and mechanisms, test Ozobot navigation, and refine both physical and digital elements for an immersive, hands-on exploration of how resource discovery shaped migration, innovation, and impact on communities.","url":""}]

ci: Managing change requires acceptance, knowledge and strategies.
lo: Establishing, managing and maintaining relationships; Managing behaviours in digital situations; Managing changes associated with puberty

s.d: Students create digital self-portraits using iPads, then use Merge Cubes to transform their artwork into interactive 3D AR experiences. Each cube face can represent a different aspect of their identity or a strategy for managing change, allowing peers to explore and 'unlock' stories about acceptance, growth, and resilience in an immersive gallery walk.

Students create interactive digital wellbeing journals using Explain Everything, combining drawings, text, and voice recordings to reflect on managing change and emotional responses. They animate self-portraits or symbols to express personal insights about acceptance and strategies for navigating puberty and digital behaviours. This dynamic multimedia project deepens their understanding of the central idea by making their reflections engaging and shareable.

Students collaboratively design empathy map posters in Canva, visually representing the feelings, needs, and strategies of someone experiencing change (e.g., starting at a new school, going through puberty, or facing online challenges). These can be shared digitally or printed for classroom discussion.

Students create and publish digital sticky notes on a shared Padlet board, each detailing a strategy, resource, or personal story related to managing change in relationships, digital behaviours, or puberty. They actively comment on and 'like' peers? posts, collaboratively building a dynamic resource bank that reflects their growing knowledge and acceptance of change. This interactive wall serves as a visual and evolving product that connects directly to the unit?s central idea of managing change through acceptance, knowledge, and strategies.

Each student authors a multimedia eBook in Book Creator, combining text, images, audio reflections, and video clips to document their learning about managing change, acceptance, and growth. The final product serves as a personal guide and can be shared with younger students or families.

plannerText: This unit explores how managing change requires acceptance, knowledge, and strategies, with a focus on relationships, digital behaviours, and personal growth. Students engage in reflective journaling, digital art, empathy mapping, and collaborative discussions, culminating in creative projects that support wellbeing and positive transitions.

Each cube face can represent a different aspect of their identity or a strategy for managing change, allowing peers to explore and 'unlock' stories about acceptance, growth, and resilience in an immersive gallery walk.","url":""},{"t":"Explain Everything","d":"Students create interactive digital wellbeing journals using Explain Everything, combining drawings, text, and voice recordings to reflect on managing change and emotional responses. This dynamic multimedia project deepens their understanding of the central idea by making their reflections engaging and shareable.","url":""},{"t":"Canva","d":"Students collaboratively design empathy map posters in Canva, visually representing the feelings, needs, and strategies of someone experiencing change (e.g., starting at a new school, going through puberty, or facing online challenges). They actively comment on and 'like' peers? posts, collaboratively building a dynamic resource bank that reflects their growing knowledge and acceptance of change. This interactive wall serves as a visual and evolving product that connects directly to the unit?s central idea of managing change through acceptance, knowledge, and strategies.","url":""},{"t":"Book Creator","d":"Each student authors a multimedia eBook in Book Creator, combining text, images, audio reflections, and video clips to document their learning about managing change, acceptance, and growth. The final product serves as a personal guide and can be shared with younger students or families.","url":""},{"t":"Transitions Time Capsule: Interactive Memory Vaults","d":"Students use the Design Cycle to collaboratively design and build physical 'time capsules' from recycled materials, cardboard, and craft supplies, each representing a key transition or change in their lives (e.g., moving schools, making new friends, learning new skills). Each capsule features a secret compartment and a digital element: students embed programmable micro:bit devices (or similar) to add interactive features such as sound messages, LED patterns, or simple digital counters that activate when the capsule is opened. Students prototype, test, and iterate both the mechanical and digital components, reflecting on the emotions and strategies associated with change. The final interactive vaults are shared in a class 'Transitions Gallery,' where peers unlock, explore, and discuss the stories and wellbeing strategies hidden inside each capsule.","url":""}]

ci: The media engages its audience and invites a response
lo: How media targets audiences; Techniques used to engage and persuade; Critical evaluation of media messages
plannerText: Not yet documented in PDF.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Delightex","d":"Students design immersive 3D virtual 'advertising worlds' in Delightex, then embed a teacher-created Wise Discussion Chatbot (Socratic Tutor scenario) as 'Alexis Kim, an ethical marketing consultant' to challenge visitors with dilemmas about persuasive techniques and sustainability. After exploring the world and chatting with Alexis, students summarise their new insights in a reflection, deepening their understanding of how media shapes consumer choices and ethical decision-making.","url":""},{"t":"Padlet","d":"Students use Padlet to collaboratively brainstorm and map out the influences on their chosen product, then design and distribute a Microsoft Forms survey to gather real data on peer influences and advertising effectiveness. This workflow mirrors the unit's research and analysis tasks, making student inquiry visible and actionable as they interpret survey results and update their Padlet with evidence-based conclusions.","url":""},{"t":"Adobe Express Podcasting","d":"Learners script and record persuasive audio advertisements or slam poems critiquing media influence and ethical consumerism using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com). Their podcasts are published as part of a class 'Media Gallery', sparking discussion about the power of audio storytelling to shape opinions and drive action.","url":""},{"t":"Canva","d":"Students create dynamic, data-driven infographics and digital posters that visually communicate the impact of advertising strategies, peer influence, and environmental consequences of consumer choices. Canva's 'Remove Background' feature can be used to remix product images, allowing students to reimagine familiar ads and highlight ethical alternatives.","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students use the verified lesson 'Media Literacy: News and Bias' (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/media-literacy-news-and-bias) to investigate how media sources use persuasive techniques and bias to influence audiences. They present their findings by building in-game newsrooms and posting screenshots with critical reflections on advertising claims.","url":""},{"t":"Makey Makey","d":"Persuasion Arcade: Build & Code an Interactive Ethical Shopping Game?students work in teams to design and construct a physical 'arcade booth' using recycled materials, LED lights, and simple circuits. Using Makey Makey or Micro:bit, they program interactive buttons that trigger digital media (audio jingles, persuasive slogans, or ethical dilemma videos) on an iPad, following the Design Cycle to prototype, test, and refine their booth, then reflect on how their arcade influences thinking about ethical consumerism.","url":""}]

ci: An inquiry into the interconnectedness of human-made systems and communities; the structure and function of organisations.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

s.d: Students use Minecraft Education to collaboratively design a future-focused, sustainable version of Melbourne that addresses the interconnectedness of human-made systems (transport, energy, waste, housing) and community needs. They must justify their design choices based on research into real-world organisational structures and present their city as a model for efficient, equitable urban organisation.

Students design and distribute digital surveys to collect data on consumer choices, advertising influences, and ethical considerations within their school community. They analyse the results in Excel to identify patterns and trends, directly connecting to the unit's focus on how organisations and systems influence decision-making.

Students create persuasive digital advertisements for alternative, sustainable products or organisational models. They use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to enhance visuals and apply design principles learned in Visual Art, linking to the unit's exploration of advertising strategies and influence.

Students collaboratively build a digital wall mapping the structure and function of various organisations (e.g., companies, NGOs, government bodies). They post research findings, infographics, and reflections, visually demonstrating the interconnectedness of systems and communities.

Students compose and record a multimodal song, rap, or slam poem that critiques or reimagines consumerism and organisational influence. They integrate literary devices and data from their research, directly supporting the unit's action-oriented creative assessment.

plannerText: Students collaboratively investigate the influences on consumer choices for a chosen product, focusing on advertising, peer influence, and environmental impact. They conduct research, analyse data, and present findings through multimodal presentations, including creative action pieces such as songs, advertisements, or slam poems that propose alternative, sustainable models of consumerism.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students create 3D models of interconnected human-made systems?like supply chains or transportation networks?on Merge Cube, then use Delightex to animate the flow of goods, information, and influence. This app smash brings abstract systems thinking to life, allowing learners to manipulate variables and observe ripple effects in real time. The activity supports inquiry into how advertising, peer influence, and ethical choices shape communities and consumer behaviour.","url":""},{"t":"Microsoft Forms","d":"Groups create and distribute digital surveys to the school community to gather authentic data on peer influences, advertising effectiveness, and ethical considerations in consumer choices. Students then analyse the results in Excel to identify trends and present their findings as part of their inquiry into how organisations shape consumer behaviour.","url":""},{"t":"Micro:bit","d":"Students design and code ethical pop-up market systems using Micro:bit, inspired by the 'Data Handling' lesson (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=data-handling-unit-summary). They gather and analyse data on consumer choices, simulate transactions, and reflect on the impact of their decisions. This project fuses economics, ethics, and digital innovation.","url":""},{"t":"Padlet","d":"Classes use Padlet to crowdsource and visually map the production, marketing, and disposal journeys of selected products. Each group posts images, research, and ethical/environmental findings, allowing for a collaborative, interactive exploration of the structure and function of organisations within global systems.","url":""},{"t":"Google Maps","d":"Students use Google Maps to trace and visualise the global journey of a product, identifying key locations such as production sites, shipping routes, and retail outlets. They create interactive maps that highlight ethical and sustainable choices within supply chains, deepening their understanding of interconnected human-made systems and the impact of consumer decisions. Students use Street View to observe ground-level evidence, compare places and capture notes that connect directly to the unit.","url":""},{"t":"Ethical Pop-Up Market: Build & Code Smart Stalls","d":"Students work in teams to design, build, and code a physical 'pop-up market' stall that showcases a product with ethical and sustainable features. Using recycled materials, cardboard, and craft supplies, each group constructs a stall with interactive elements (e.g., spinning wheels, product displays, moving signs). They embed simple programmable electronics (such as micro:bit or Makey Makey) to add digital features like sound effects, scrolling LED messages, or touch-activated info panels that communicate the ethical story behind their product. The design cycle guides students through empathising with ethical consumers, ideating fair trade or eco-friendly product concepts, prototyping their stall, testing interactive features, and refining their presentation. The final market is set up in the Makerspace, where peers and visitors can 'shop', interact with the stalls, and reflect on how advertising, peer influence, and ethical considerations shape consumer choices.","url":""}]

ci: Scientists undertake experimentation to confirm, contest and create new theories.
lo: The scientific method; How experiments lead to new understandings; The relationship between planets and the Sun; Energy transfer and transformation in circuits

s.d: Students use 'Minecraft: The Scientific Method' (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/the-scientific-method) to collaboratively design, conduct, and document their own digital experiments. They can manipulate variables, collect data, and present findings in a virtual lab, directly mirroring the inquiry cycle and scientific experimentation focus of the unit.

Students record and annotate their science experiments step-by-step, using Explain Everything to capture hypotheses, procedures, results, and reflections. This creates a dynamic digital science journal that documents their inquiry process and supports metacognitive thinking.

Students use Merge Cubes to explore 3D models of molecules, circuits, and planetary systems, allowing them to visualize and manipulate scientific phenomena related to energy, matter, and planetary movement, deepening their conceptual understanding.

Students design and distribute digital surveys to collect data on classmates' experiment results, then analyse the aggregated data for trends and patterns. This reinforces the importance of repeatability, data collection, and evidence in scientific inquiry.

Students create audio reports or science podcasts in GarageBand, explaining their experimental process, findings, and how their results confirm, contest, or create new scientific theories. This supports communication skills and creative synthesis of learning.

plannerText: This unit engages students in hands-on and digital scientific inquiry, focusing on experimentation to confirm, contest, and create new theories. Students investigate energy in circuits, chemical changes, planetary movement, and the scientific method, culminating in designing and presenting their own experiments.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Micro:bit","d":"Students use Micro:bit to collect and analyse data from scientific experiments, bringing the scientific method to life as they investigate planetary motion or energy transfer. They visualise their findings and draw conclusions, deepening their understanding of how experimentation leads to new theories. (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=data-handling-unit-summary)","url":""},{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students create augmented reality models of electrical circuits, reversible/irreversible changes, or planetary systems using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app. They can record short video explanations overlaying their AR models, demonstrating their understanding of scientific concepts and inquiry methods.","url":""},{"t":"Sphero BOLT","d":"Students program and control Sphero BOLT robots to simulate planetary orbits and energy transfer in circuits, applying the scientific method to test hypotheses about motion and forces. They collect data from their robot?s sensors to analyse variables such as speed and distance, creating visual representations of their findings. This hands-on exploration deepens understanding of how experiments confirm and contest scientific theories in physical science and earth science.","url":""},{"t":"Microsoft Forms","d":"Students design and distribute digital surveys using Microsoft Forms to collect data on classmates' hypotheses, experiment results, or scientific misconceptions. They analyze the data in Excel, drawing evidence-based conclusions and reflecting on the importance of data in scientific inquiry.","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students engage with the 'Minecraft: The Scientific Method' lesson (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/the-scientific-method), where they plan and conduct experiments in a virtual world, practicing variables, fair testing, and data collection, directly linking to the unit's focus on how scientists experiment to build knowledge.","url":"https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/the-scientific-method"},{"t":"Planetary Motion Marble Run: Code & Construct","d":"Students work in teams to design and build a large-scale, multi-level marble run that models planetary movement and energy transfer. Using recycled materials (cardboard, tubes, tape, popsticks), they physically construct tracks that represent orbits, inclines for gravitational slingshots, and obstacles for celestial events. Teams integrate micro:bit sensors (or similar age-appropriate microcontrollers) to digitally track marbles (planets) as they travel, displaying data (speed, distance, 'orbital period') on a connected tablet. Students program the micro:bit to trigger lights or sounds at key events (e.g., 'eclipse' zones, energy transfers). The project culminates in a science fair demonstration where students explain how their hybrid creation models scientific concepts of motion, energy, and planetary systems, and reflect on how scientists use both physical models and digital tools to test and refine theories.","url":""}]

ci: The influences on our consumer choices reveal the interdependence between human and natural systems and call for responsible action toward just and sustainable futures.
lo: Influences on consumer choices (function); How consumer decisions impact living things and their habitats (causation); Alternative models of consumerism that honour a fair and sustainable future (perspective)

s.d: Students use 'Minecraft: Revamp Melbourne' to collaboratively design and build a future-focused, sustainable version of Melbourne. They must integrate real-world research on ethical consumerism, sustainable materials, and urban systems, then justify their design choices in terms of environmental and social impact. This immersive simulation challenges students to apply their understanding of consumer choices and interdependence between human and natural systems in a highly creative, systems-thinking context.

Students create persuasive multimedia campaigns using Canva to advocate for more ethical and sustainable consumer choices. They can design infographics, social media posts, or digital posters that highlight the environmental and social impacts of popular products, using data from their research to influence real-world audiences.

Students design and distribute digital surveys using Microsoft Forms to collect data on peer and family consumer habits. They analyse the results to identify trends, influences, and opportunities for more responsible action, then present their findings using visual data representations.

Students collaboratively build a digital wall on Padlet to document and reflect on case studies of companies (local and global) that have shifted toward more sustainable and just business models. They can post articles, videos, and their own reflections, fostering a shared knowledge base and critical discussion.

Students script, record, and publish a podcast episode using Adobe Express Podcasting, where they interview local business owners, sustainability experts, or peers about the challenges and opportunities of making ethical consumer choices. This real-world connection deepens their inquiry and communication skills.

plannerText: Students investigate the influences on consumer choices, analyse advertising and peer influence, and research the ethical and environmental impacts of products. They collaborate to present findings, create persuasive multimodal texts, and take action by proposing or advocating for more just and sustainable consumer models.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Delightex","d":"Students collaboratively design immersive 3D virtual worlds in Delightex to visualize the entire lifecycle of a consumer product, from resource extraction to disposal. They then embed Padlet boards within their worlds for peers to leave critical feedback, questions, and alternative sustainable ideas, creating a dynamic, interactive exhibition that brings the complexities of consumer choices and sustainability to life.","url":""},{"t":"Microsoft Forms","d":"Students create and distribute digital surveys using Microsoft Forms to gather real data from their peers and families about consumer habits, influences, and attitudes toward sustainability. They analyze the results to identify trends and present their findings as evidence in their group investigations.","url":""},{"t":"Micro:bit","d":"Students use Micro:bit to collect and visualise data about their own consumer choices, exploring the interdependence between humans and natural systems. They analyse patterns and propose actions for more sustainable living, making data-driven decisions for real-world impact. (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=data-handling-unit-summary)","url":""},{"t":"GarageBand","d":"Students compose and record original slam poems, raps, or jingles in GarageBand, using literary devices and sound design to creatively persuade listeners to reflect on their consumer habits and consider alternative, sustainable models.","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students explore the concept of recycling and sustainability in the 'Radical Recycling' Minecraft Education lesson, gaining hands-on experience with recycling systems and then reflecting on how these models could be adapted or improved in their own communities.","url":"https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/radical-recycling"},{"t":"Sustainable Product Pitch: Smart Packaging Prototypes","d":"Students work in teams to design, build, and prototype an innovative, eco-friendly product package that addresses an environmental issue caused by consumer choices (e.g., single-use plastics, food waste, over-packaging). Using recycled materials (cardboard, bottles, fabric, etc.), they construct a physical prototype of their packaging solution, integrating a micro:bit or Makey Makey to add an interactive digital feature (such as a touch sensor that plays a persuasive audio message, or a display showing the environmental impact of their design). Students use the Design Cycle: researching existing packaging, brainstorming and sketching solutions, building prototypes, testing with peers, and refining based on feedback. Each team creates a short video pitch using iPads, showcasing their prototype's features and sustainability benefits to a mock 'eco-investor' panel.","url":""}]

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Human needs drive the development of nations.
lo: How human needs drive national development; Role of migration in nation building; Impacts of development on Indigenous and colonial populations

s.d: Students build 3D migration journeys or nation-building timelines on Merge Cube, then use Delightex to animate key events, push-pull factors, and personal stories. This app smash helps learners visualise the forces that drive the development of nations and the lived experiences of migration, deepening empathy and historical understanding. The interactive models serve as powerful tools for sharing research and sparking discussion.

Create a digital timeline in Canva that visually compares pre- and post-colonised Australia, layering key events, migration waves, and changes in citizenship rights. Students can app smash by incorporating iPad camera photos from their Melbourne Museum excursion and use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature for artefact images.

Facilitate a collaborative Padlet where students post and respond to migration stories, personal family histories, and reflections on citizenship. They can link to research, videos, and maps, fostering a living digital archive that connects classroom learning to real-world experiences.

Students author a multimedia eBook in Book Creator, weaving together first-person migration narratives, maps, and primary source analysis. They can include voice recordings, scanned artefacts, and interactive quizzes about the influence of systems on citizens.

Design and distribute a Microsoft Forms survey to the school community, gathering data on migration backgrounds, reasons for moving, and perceptions of Australian identity. Students analyse the results in Excel, then present their findings on how needs and systems influence migration and citizenship.

plannerText: This unit explores how human needs drive the development of nations by comparing pre- and post-colonised Australia, examining the relationship between migration and needs, and analysing the influence of systems on citizens. Students engage in historical inquiry, personal narrative, museum excursions, and creative projects to understand migration, citizenship, and the shaping of Australian identity.
