The interactive models serve as powerful tools for sharing research and sparking discussion.","url":""},{"t":"Canva","d":"Create a digital timeline in Canva that visually compares pre- and post-colonised Australia, layering key events, migration waves, and changes in citizenship rights. Students can app smash by incorporating iPad camera photos from their Melbourne Museum excursion and use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature for artefact images.","url":""},{"t":"Padlet","d":"Facilitate a collaborative Padlet where students post and respond to migration stories, personal family histories, and reflections on citizenship. They can link to research, videos, and maps, fostering a living digital archive that connects classroom learning to real-world experiences.","url":""},{"t":"Book Creator","d":"Students author a multimedia eBook in Book Creator, weaving together first-person migration narratives, maps, and primary source analysis. They can include voice recordings, scanned artefacts, and interactive quizzes about the influence of systems on citizens.","url":""},{"t":"Microsoft Forms","d":"Design and distribute a Microsoft Forms survey to the school community, gathering data on migration backgrounds, reasons for moving, and perceptions of Australian identity. Students analyse the results in Excel, then present their findings on how needs and systems influence migration and citizenship.","url":""},{"t":"Migration Memory Machines","d":"Students will research the journeys and experiences of diverse migrants to Australia, both pre- and post-colonisation. Using the Design Cycle, they will select a real or imagined migration story, then design and build a tangible, interactive 'memory machine'?a physical artifact that tells the story through movement, sound, and light. Students will craft the base using recycled materials, cardboard, and clay, constructing elements like turning wheels, pop-up scenes, or sliding panels. They will integrate simple programmable electronics (e.g., BBC micro:bit or Makey Makey) to trigger recorded oral histories, ambient sounds, or LED lights when parts are moved or touched. Each machine must physically embody key moments or challenges from the migration narrative, allowing viewers to 'travel' through the story by interacting with the device. This hands-on project encourages empathy, creativity, and a deep understanding of how migration shapes identity and nationhood.","url":""}]

ci: An inquiry into the nature of the self; beliefs and values; personal, physical, mental, social and spiritual health; human relationships.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

s.d: Students design and train AI-powered chatbots to simulate conversations about wellbeing, mindset, and neurodiversity. Each group codes their chatbot to respond to scenarios about brain function, growth mindset, and strategies for managing wellbeing, allowing peers to interact and reflect on different perspectives of 'Who We Are'.

Students script and record a podcast series exploring the physiology of the brain, personal stories of mindset shifts, and wellbeing strategies. They can interview classmates or staff, edit with music and sound effects, and publish episodes to share insights on how identity and wellbeing shape learning.

Students create interactive digital books documenting their personal wellbeing journeys. Each page can include diagrams of the brain, reflections on mindset, photos of their artefact space, and strategies for self-regulation, combining text, audio, and video for a multimodal exploration of identity.

Students collaboratively build a digital wall where they post and comment on strategies for health, safety, and wellbeing. They can share resources, personal reflections, and questions about neurodiversity, creating a living resource that grows as their understanding deepens throughout the unit.

Students design infographics or posters that visually explain the parts of the brain, the difference between growth and fixed mindsets, or wellbeing strategies. Using Canva's 'Remove Background' feature, they can incorporate their own photos or artefacts, making the visuals highly personal and relevant.

plannerText: This unit explores identities as individuals and as part of collectives, focusing on physical, emotional, social, and spiritual health and wellbeing. Key activities include learning about the brain's physiology, reflecting on mindset and experiences, and developing strategies to support health, safety, and wellbeing through hands-on, reflective, and collaborative tasks.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Learners create interactive 3D brain models or wellbeing maps on Merge Cube, then use Delightex to animate neural pathways, zones of regulation, or growth mindset strategies. This app smash makes neurodiversity and self-regulation visible, empowering students to explore how their brains work and how experiences shape learning and wellbeing. The activity supports reflection and collaborative discussion about personal growth and health.","url":""},{"t":"Padlet","d":"Students create a collaborative digital wellbeing journal on Padlet, where they post daily reflections, strategies, and creative responses related to their personal health and mindset. This interactive space encourages peer feedback and shared learning about emotional regulation and neurodiversity, deepening their understanding of the central idea through ongoing dialogue and visual storytelling.","url":""},{"t":"Micro:bit","d":"Students program their Micro:bit to build a step counter that tracks their daily physical activity by coding the accelerometer sensor to detect movement. They test and refine their code to ensure accurate step counts, then analyse their data to reflect on how physical activity supports their wellbeing, linking to the central idea of personal health and self-awareness.","url":"https://microbit.org/projects/make-it-code-it/step-counter/"},{"t":"Book Creator","d":"Students create a digital 'Neurodiversity Storybook' in Book Creator, illustrating how different brains learn and thrive. Each page can feature a unique learning profile, myth-busting facts, and student-generated wellbeing tips, integrating research, creativity, and empathy.","url":""},{"t":"Canva","d":"Using Canva, students design vibrant infographics or posters that visually map the parts of the brain, their functions, and links to wellbeing strategies. They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to personalize their posters with their own photos, making neuroscience relevant to their own identities.","url":""},{"t":"Neurodiversity Quest: Build & Code Your Brain Adventure","d":"Students work in teams to design and build a large, interactive 3D 'brain map' maze using cardboard, recycled materials, and craft supplies. Each section of the maze physically represents a different brain region or function (e.g., memory, emotion, creativity, self-regulation), decorated with symbols and textures. Students embed simple electronics (e.g., Makey Makey, LED circuits) to trigger sounds, lights, or digital messages when touched. Using Sphero Indi robots or similar, students code a 'neurodiverse explorer' to navigate the maze, encountering hands-on challenges and reflection prompts at each station (e.g., a puzzle about growth mindset, a sensory task for emotional regulation). The project culminates in a showcase where teams guide others through their custom brain adventure, explaining the links between physical structures, mental health, and personal identity.","url":""}]

ci: People recognise important events through celebrations and traditions.
lo: The traditions of our families and communities; How and why people celebrate; The similarities and differences between celebrations

s.d: Students use Sphero Indi to create a 'celebration parade,' programming the car to travel through stations representing different cultural events and traditions. As Indi visits each stop, students share stories, music, or artefacts, making global celebrations interactive and memorable.

Students photograph symbols or artefacts from celebrations important to their families or communities, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate these images and record a short explanation about the tradition or event. This activity helps students express their understanding of how people recognise important events through celebrations, connecting directly to the unit?s central idea and lines of inquiry about family and community traditions. The final product is a lively, recorded animation that showcases students? oral language skills and their reflections on similarities and differences between celebrations.

Students create and perform simple puppet shows using Puppet Pals to share stories about their favourite family or cultural celebrations. They select characters and backgrounds to represent key traditions, then record their voices narrating the significance of each celebration, bringing their personal and community traditions to life through digital storytelling.

Students create a digital celebration journal in Seesaw by drawing pictures, recording their voices sharing family traditions, and uploading photos of celebrations they experience. They explore the central idea by comparing their own celebrations with those of classmates, building a vibrant, multimodal collection that can be shared with families to showcase diverse traditions and important events.

Students create vibrant digital collages using photos, drawings, and text to showcase their family and community celebrations. They combine images of traditional foods, costumes, and decorations with short captions explaining the significance of each element, expressing their understanding of how and why people celebrate important events.

plannerText: This Prep HWEO unit explores how people recognise important events through celebrations and traditions, with students engaging in cultural dances, storytelling, art (such as circle art), music, and hands-on activities like making traditional foods. The unit emphasises sharing personal and family traditions, comparing global celebrations, and expressing understanding through creative, multimodal experiences.

ci: We all have roles and responsibilities in the community
lo: Roles people play; Responsibilities in the community; How working together helps everyone

s.d: Learners use Sphero Indi to design a community map, programming the car to visit different helpers and locations within the school. As Indi travels, students explain each role and responsibility, reinforcing their understanding of how everyone contributes to a caring community.

Students take photos of themselves or classroom helpers, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the images and record short messages about their responsibilities, creating a playful digital 'talking roles' gallery.

Students create individual digital journal entries in Seesaw where they draw or photograph their classroom jobs or community helpers, then add simple voice recordings to explain their roles and responsibilities. The teacher compiles these entries into a shared class portfolio, allowing students to explore each other's contributions and reflect on how working together supports the community.

Learners film and edit short skits in iMovie, acting out different community roles and responsibilities. They collaborate to script, perform, and record scenes that show how helpers contribute to the school and neighbourhood, then share their movies with peers to spark discussion about teamwork and responsibility.

Use PicCollage on iPads for students to visually sort and group images of different community roles (e.g., teacher, nurse, firefighter, gardener), then present their collages to the class, explaining the importance of each role using simple voice recordings.

plannerText: In this unit, Prep students explore the central idea that everyone has roles and responsibilities in the community. Through stories, role play, visual timetables, and hands-on activities, they learn about different helpers in the school and wider community, develop essential agreements, and reflect on their own responsibilities as learners and friends.

ci: An inquiry into the natural world and its laws; the interaction between the natural world and human societies.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded
plannerText: Not yet documented in PDF.

ci: Human actions change the environment.
lo: How people interact with different environments; The effect people have on the environment; Ways in which people can take action and care for the environment

s.d: Students use the iPad camera to photograph examples of human impact on the school grounds (e.g., rubbish, garden care, recycling bins). They then create a visual 'before and after' story in Adobe Express, adding stickers, voice recordings, and simple animations to show how small actions can change the environment.

Students record themselves explaining how they care for the environment at home or school, using drawings and photos to illustrate their actions. Teachers can prompt with sentence starters and students can respond with voice, drawing, or simple labeling.

Students collaboratively make a digital class book about different environments (desert, marine, forest, playground) and how humans can help or harm them. Each student adds a page with their drawing, a photo, and a short audio description.

Students collect and photograph natural and human-made objects from the playground, then use PicCollage to sort and label them into 'helpful for the environment' and 'harmful for the environment' categories, adding emojis and stickers for visual support.

Using Canva's 'Remove Background' feature, students create posters showing a sea creature or plant in a healthy environment and then in a polluted one. They use simple icons, photos, and their own drawings to highlight the impact of human actions.

plannerText: Prep students explore different environments (desert, marine, playground), identify plants and animals, and discuss human impact such as pollution and recycling. Activities include drawing, storytelling, excursions (e.g., aquarium), and hands-on projects like building models and creating class books to reflect on how human actions change the environment.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: An inquiry into orientation in place and time; personal histories; the relationship between and the interconnectedness of individuals and civilisations.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded
plannerText: Not yet documented in PDF.

ci: To be healthy, people???s basic needs must be met.
lo: How our brain is growing and changing; How our emotions, behaviours and actions promote relationships; How we care for our bodies

s.d: Students take photos of their healthy lunchboxes or favourite fruits and vegetables, then use ChatterPix Kids to make the foods 'talk' about why they are healthy and how they help our bodies. This creates a fun, memorable way for Preps to explain nutrition concepts in their own words.

Students photograph their lunchboxes and use the drawing and voice recording tools to sort and explain which foods are 'everyday' (healthy) and which are 'sometimes' foods. They can also record themselves role-playing healthy habits like handwashing or exercise, sharing these with families.

Using Canva's visual templates and 'Remove Background' feature, students create a colourful collage of healthy habits (e.g., sleep, exercise, fruit, handwashing). They can drag and drop images, add stickers, and record short audio clips describing each healthy choice.

Students build a digital 'Healthy Me' book, adding photos, drawings, and voice recordings about their favourite healthy foods, exercises, and ways to stay calm. Each page can focus on a different aspect of wellbeing, supporting oral language and visual literacy.

Students use the iPad Camera to document healthy choices throughout the school day?snack time, active play, mindfulness moments. These photos can be sequenced into a visual diary or slideshow, then shared with the class or families to reinforce healthy routines.

plannerText: The Prep 'Who We Are' unit focuses on understanding what it means to be healthy, with students exploring nutrition, exercise, sleep, hygiene, and emotional wellbeing. Activities include sorting foods, learning about the Zones of Regulation, role-playing healthy habits, and creating visual and oral reflections on making healthy choices at school and home.

ci: People express their emotions through the way they communicate.
lo: Different ways to communicate emotions; How art, music and movement express feelings; Understanding others' expressions through body language and facial cues

s.d: Students take photos of their own facial expressions or body language, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the images and record their voices describing the emotion and how they are expressing it. This creates a playful, visual portfolio of emotional expression that directly connects to the unit's focus on non-verbal communication.

Students design digital 'emotion posters' using Canva's templates and 'Remove Background' feature to insert their own photos. They add emojis, colours, and simple words to visually represent different emotions and ways people express them, reinforcing vocabulary and visual literacy.

Students record short video reflections or role-plays showing how they express different feelings through body language, facial expressions, or movement. These are shared in a class Seesaw journal for peer feedback and discussion, supporting self-awareness and empathy.

Students create a collaborative digital class book where each page features a student's drawing or photo of an emotion, paired with a simple audio recording explaining how they express that feeling. This supports multimodal communication and builds a resource for emotional vocabulary.

Students use the iPad Camera to capture classmates demonstrating different emotions through facial expressions and body language. These images are then sorted or matched to emotion words in a classroom activity, deepening understanding of non-verbal cues.

plannerText: This unit explores how people express their emotions through communication, with a strong focus on body language, facial expressions, and non-verbal cues. Students engage in visual arts, drama, and music activities to explore and represent emotions, create team identities, and reflect on how feelings are shown and interpreted in different contexts.

ci: Systems are involved in preparing products for consumers.
lo: Where products come from; The systems products go through; How people choose their products

s.d: Students take photos of different products (like apples, milk, or clothing) at various stages of their journey from farm to consumer. They record their voices as the products, explaining each step of the process in a fun, animated way, making the 'system' visible and memorable for young learners.

Students create a digital flowchart or sequence using photos and drawings to show the journey of a product (e.g., apple, milk, or clothing) from its origin to the consumer. They can add voice recordings to explain each stage, supporting multimodal learning and assessment.

Using Canva's visual templates, students collaboratively design posters that illustrate the stages a product goes through before reaching consumers. They can use the 'Remove Background' feature to create collages of products and systems, making the invisible visible.

Students watch curated videos about food and clothing production systems (such as 'Farm to Table' or 'War on Waste'), then use iPads to record short video reflections or create their own mini-documentaries about what they learned regarding systems and processes.

Students build a digital book documenting the journey of a chosen product. Each page represents a stage in the system (e.g., farm, factory, transport, shop), with photos, drawings, and audio narration to bring the process to life for early readers.

plannerText: This unit explores the systems involved in preparing products for consumers, focusing on the journey of items like food and clothing from their origins to the end user. Students investigate, sequence, and represent the stages products go through, using videos, flowcharts, and hands-on making to deepen their understanding of processing, transportation, and consumer choice.

ci: Weather affects our lives in different ways.
lo: Types of weather conditions; How weather impacts our lives (clothing, activities, food, feelings); Why weather changes (seasons, patterns)

s.d: Students become 'weather reporters' by capturing daily weather changes using the iPad Camera, then app smash into Delightex to create interactive, animated weather scenes. They can drag and drop digital clothing, foods, and activities onto avatars to show how weather impacts daily life, then record their voices explaining their choices.

Students take photos of their own weather-related drawings or classroom weather charts, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate them with talking mouths. Each student records a short weather report or explains how the weather affects what they wear or do that day, building confidence and oral language skills.

Students create vibrant digital collages that showcase different weather types and how they affect daily life, combining photos, drawings, and text to represent clothing, activities, and foods for each season. This hands-on activity encourages them to visually organize their observations and express their understanding of weather?s impact in a fun, age-appropriate format.

Students use Seesaw to document their daily weather observations by uploading photos, sorting digital clothing and activity cards, and recording their reflections. Teachers can set up interactive activities where students match weather types to appropriate clothing or activities, supporting formative assessment.

Students create simple animated stories in ScratchJr showing how weather changes affect their day. For example, they can program a character to choose an umbrella when it rains or put on a hat when it is sunny, reinforcing cause and effect through coding and storytelling.

plannerText: The unit engages Year 1 students in exploring how weather affects daily life, including clothing, activities, and food choices. Students investigate weather patterns, collect and graph data, compare seasonal changes, and express their understanding through creative activities such as weather reports, tableaux, and music. The unit emphasizes student agency, hands-on inquiry, and visual documentation of learning.

ci: Living things have structures that allow them to survive in different environments.
lo: The physical structure of plants and bees; How features support survival in different environments; Human impact on living things

s.d: Students take photos of their own bean plants or clay bee sculptures, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the plant or bee explaining its unique adaptations for survival. This brings scientific drawing and observation to life, making plant and animal structures memorable and personal.

Students create a highly visual infographic poster in Canva showing the life cycle of a plant or bee, using icons, arrows, and minimal text. They can use the 'Remove Background' feature to add their own photos of classroom plants or bee models, making the learning authentic and hands-on.

Students document their bean diary or plant/animal adaptation research as a digital book in Book Creator, combining photos, drawings, voice recordings, and short videos. Each page can focus on a different adaptation or survival strategy, supporting multimodal learning.

Students record time-lapse videos or photo journals of their plant growth experiments, then annotate and narrate their observations in Seesaw. They can compare how different conditions (light, water) affect plant structure and survival, supporting scientific thinking and reflection.

Students create a collage of different plant and animal adaptations using classroom photos, magazine cutouts, and digital stickers. They label each adaptation visually, then present their collage to the class, explaining how each feature helps survival in a specific environment.

plannerText: Students explored the structures and adaptations of plants and bees through hands-on experiments (bean diaries, plant growth in different conditions), scientific drawing, clay sculpture, and research. They investigated how living things survive in various environments, with a focus on plant and animal adaptations, pollination, and human impact on ecosystems.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: History and culture connect the past, present and future.
lo: How technology has changed over time; The purpose and significance of artefacts; How the past connects to the present and future

s.d: Students design a 'Future Technology Timeline' poster, blending photos of artefacts from home with their own imaginative drawings of future devices. They use Canva's drag-and-drop features and 'Remove Background' tool to visually compare past, present, and predicted future technologies, directly connecting to the unit's focus on change over time.

Students record oral family history interviews with parents or grandparents using the iPad Camera app, then upload and annotate these videos in Seesaw. They can add voice comments and simple drawings to highlight changes in school life, technology, or family traditions across generations.

Each student creates a digital 'My Family Artefact Book' by photographing artefacts (using the iPad Camera app) and sequencing them in Book Creator. They add voice recordings explaining the significance of each item and how it connects their family's past, present, and future.

The class collaborates on a visual Padlet wall, uploading photos or drawings of family artefacts, old and new technologies, and dance styles from different eras. Students use emojis and voice notes to compare similarities and differences, building a shared digital timeline of cultural change.

Students watch curated short videos on Clickview about the evolution of technology and daily life. They then use the iPad Camera app to record their own 'then and now' video reflections, describing what has changed in their lives or families, and upload these to Seesaw for class discussion.

plannerText: Students explore how history and culture connect the past, present, and future by investigating family artefacts, creating timelines, comparing old and new technologies, and predicting future changes. Activities include sorting photos, interviewing family members, exploring artefacts, and designing future inventions, with a strong focus on visual, hands-on, and oral storytelling.

ci: We learn about ourselves through the games we play.
lo: How to identify and manage emotions; How players' choices affect relationships; Our responsibilities when playing games

s.d: Students collaboratively design a digital board game about playground fairness using Delightex, then bring their characters to life by recording voice-acted game scenarios in Puppet Pals. This app smash allows students to explore multiple perspectives and practice resolving conflict through creative storytelling.

Students document and reflect on their own game creations and playground experiences by capturing photos, drawing, and recording voice-overs in Seesaw. They can share strategies for fair play and problem-solving, building a digital portfolio of social learning moments.

Students take photos of their favorite playground games or team mascots, then animate them with their own voices using ChatterPix Kids to explain the rules, share tips for teamwork, or describe how to handle disagreements during play.

Students create visual collages of 'What Makes a Game Fair?' using photos from the iPad camera, stickers, and drawings. They can visually sort examples of positive and negative playground behaviors, then present their collages to the class for discussion.

Students create short digital movies where they act out and narrate the essential agreements for playing games fairly and respectfully. They use iMovie to record their voices and videos, then add simple titles and transitions to make their agreements come alive and feel personal. This hands-on storytelling helps them reflect on how their choices affect relationships and responsibilities during play.

plannerText: This unit explores how students learn about themselves through the games they play, focusing on fairness, rules, teamwork, and resolving conflict. Activities include creating essential agreements, reflecting on emotions and behavior during play, building team identities, and using digital tools to document and share learning about positive social interactions.

ci: Artefacts provide a window into the beliefs and values of cultures.
lo: Beliefs and values contribute to a culture; The importance of rituals and traditions; How artefacts capture history and cultural expression

s.d: Students photograph a personal or family artefact using the iPad camera, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the artefact, recording a short voiceover as if the artefact is 'speaking' about its cultural significance, beliefs, or traditions. This brings artefacts to life and allows students to express understanding in a creative, highly visual way.

Students create a digital collage or poster showcasing artefacts from their own culture and others, using Canva's drag-and-drop interface and 'Remove Background' feature to layer images, symbols, and text. They can visually compare and contrast beliefs and values represented by different artefacts.

Students build a multimedia digital book about their family's traditions and artefacts, combining photos, drawings, audio recordings, and simple text. Each page can focus on a different artefact or tradition, supporting oral storytelling and visual expression.

Students document their learning journey by uploading photos, voice recordings, and short videos explaining the meaning of artefacts and rituals in their culture. Teachers can prompt students to respond to classmates' posts, fostering intercultural dialogue and reflection.

Students work in small groups to script and film a short documentary or 'news report' about a chosen artefact or tradition, using the iPad camera and iMovie to edit clips, add voiceovers, and insert titles. This allows them to practice oral communication and digital storytelling.

plannerText: This unit explores how artefacts provide a window into the beliefs and values of cultures. Students investigate their own and others' traditions, rituals, and artefacts through activities such as drawing, research, dance, music, and storytelling. They share personal artefacts, learn about cultural celebrations, and create projects that express and compare cultural perspectives.

ci: Technology can influence the way ecosystems can survive.
lo: An ecosystem has animals, plants and microorganisms; Technology can cause ecosystems to adapt and evolve; Technology can help or hinder ecosystems

s.d: Students collaboratively create an interactive digital ecosystem map, layering drawings, voice recordings, and animations to show how different technologies (like satellites, sensors, or robots) impact the survival of plants and animals. They can simulate changes (e.g., introducing a robot pollinator) and narrate the effects on the ecosystem, making abstract systems visible and dynamic.

Students design a 'Tech Timeline for Ecosystems' using Canva's visual templates, illustrating how different technologies have changed the way humans interact with and protect natural habitats. They can use the 'Remove Background' feature to integrate their own photos (taken with iPad cameras) into their posters, showing themselves as ecosystem scientists or inventors.

Students document a week-long 'Ecosystem Watch' by taking daily photos of a school garden or local habitat with iPads, then upload and annotate these in Seesaw. They record voice reflections about how technology (e.g., sprinklers, weather sensors, apps) supports or disrupts the ecosystem, making learning visible for families and peers.

Students author a multimedia eBook titled 'If I Invented a Robot for Nature...' using Book Creator. They combine hand-drawn illustrations, photos, and audio to imagine and explain a new technology that could help an ecosystem survive, considering both positive and negative impacts.

Students take photos of objects in the schoolyard (e.g., a tree, a bird, a solar panel) and use ChatterPix Kids to animate them. Each object 'talks' about how technology affects its life in the ecosystem, encouraging empathy and systems thinking through playful digital storytelling.

plannerText: This unit explores how technology can influence the survival and adaptation of ecosystems. Students investigate the roles of robotics and other technologies in society, engage with coding and digital storytelling, and reflect on ethical questions about technology's impact on the environment. Activities include coding, persuasive writing, and hands-on projects that connect technology to real-world ecosystems.

ci: People and the planet are reliant on different energy sources.
lo: Energy types (electricity, fossil fuels, solar); Energy helps us in daily life; Energy transformation; Responsibility to conserve energy and find sustainable sources

s.d: Students create talking energy source characters using ChatterPix Kids, giving each energy type (solar, fossil fuel, wind, etc.) a voice to explain how it works and its impact on the planet. These animated characters are then uploaded to Seesaw for a class-wide interactive energy debate, allowing students to respond to each other's posts with their own energy character's perspective.

Students design and code simple animations showing the journey of energy from source to use (e.g., sun to solar panel to light bulb), visually demonstrating energy transformation and sustainability concepts in a hands-on, creative way.

Students collaborate to create a class podcast series where each episode explores a different energy source from the unit, such as solar, fossil fuels, or electricity. They record their own voice explanations, share findings from experiments, and discuss why conserving energy is important, producing a multimodal audio project that deepens understanding of energy transformation and sustainability.

Students use the iPad camera to capture images of energy use around the school (e.g., lights, computers, playground equipment) and assemble them in PicCollage, labeling each with icons to show whether the energy is renewable or non-renewable, sparking discussion about sustainability in their immediate environment.

Students create vibrant word clouds using key vocabulary from the unit?such as solar, fossil fuels, electricity, and sustainability?to visually represent the different energy sources and their importance. They experiment with colors and shapes to highlight words that show how energy helps us daily and why conserving it matters, producing a colorful digital poster that sparks discussion about responsible energy use.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 2 students in exploring different energy sources?such as solar, fossil fuels, and electricity?through hands-on experiments, digital circuit building, and collaborative inquiry. Students investigate energy transformation, sustainability, and the impact of energy choices on people and the planet, culminating in reflective activities and student-driven advocacy for cleaner energy.

ci: To sustain life, people and environments depend on one another.
lo: Interdependence of people and environments; How people's wants and needs affect the land; Responsibility to use sustainable resources; Connection between fossil fuels and environmental impact

s.d: Students collaboratively create an interactive 'Resource Detective' digital board in Delightex, where they upload photos (taken with iPads) of everyday objects and environments, then use Seesaw to record audio reflections about whether each item uses finite or infinite resources, and how it connects to sustaining life. This app smash encourages visual thinking, voice, and agency while making abstract sustainability concepts tangible.

Students take photos of objects or drawings representing sustainable and non-sustainable resources, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate them with voices explaining their role in sustaining life and the importance of sharing resources. This brings student learning to life in a playful, memorable way.

Students collaborate in small groups to record short podcast episodes where they share their 'Tug of War' debates about fossil fuels, explaining how people and environments depend on each other and discussing sustainable choices to protect the planet. Using Adobe Express Podcasting, they add voice recordings, sound effects, and simple music to make their environmental messages engaging and clear.

Learners design vibrant visual collages comparing seasonal patterns and resource use in the Northern and Southern Hemispheres, using stickers, text labels, and their own photos to illustrate how people adapt to and depend on their environments.

Students program simple animated stories in ScratchJR that show how people and environments depend on each other to sustain life. They create characters representing plants, animals, and people, then design scenes illustrating sustainable resource use and the impact of fossil fuels. This hands-on coding activity deepens understanding of interdependence through storytelling and sequencing.

plannerText: This unit engages Year 2 students in exploring finite and infinite resources, the impact of fossil fuels, and the importance of sustainable practices for sharing the planet. Activities include debates, visual comparisons of seasons, and investigations into how people and environments depend on one another to sustain life.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Journeys can lead to challenges and new understandings.
lo: Types of journeys (physical, learning, cultural); Challenges encountered on journeys; New understandings gained through journeys

s.d: Students create an interactive digital 'Journey Map' using Delightex, visually mapping out a family or fictional journey with images, icons, and voice recordings. They then upload their map to Seesaw, narrating the challenges and opportunities faced along the way, allowing for peer feedback and reflection on how journeys lead to growth.

Students take photos of themselves or family members and use ChatterPix Kids to animate the images, giving voice to a character who shares a story about a significant journey, highlighting challenges and new understandings gained.

Students record short podcasts sharing stories about different types of journeys they have experienced or imagined, describing the challenges faced and new understandings gained. Using Adobe Express Podcasting, they add voice reflections and simple sound effects to bring their journey narratives to life, developing oral communication and storytelling skills connected to the unit's central idea.

Children create a visual collage representing the stages of a journey (physical, learning, or cultural), using photos, stickers, and drawings to show how each stage brought new challenges and understandings.

Students create and perform their own puppet show retelling a journey story, using Puppet Pals to design characters and settings that represent different types of journeys and challenges. They narrate how the characters overcome obstacles and gain new understandings, bringing the central idea to life through engaging digital storytelling.

plannerText: This unit explores the concept of journeys, focusing on how they present challenges and opportunities for growth. Students investigate different types of journeys through storybooks, interviews, and personal reflections, culminating in creative tasks that highlight the changes and understandings gained from these experiences.

ci: The way we interact with others is influenced by who we are
lo: Personal identity and strengths; How self-regulation supports learning; Building resilience through knowledge of ourselves

s.d: Students create an interactive 'Strengths Adventure' by designing a branching scenario in Delightex where they make choices based on their personal strengths and self-regulation strategies, then act out and record their own avatars navigating these scenarios in Puppet Pals. This app smash brings self-awareness and decision-making to life in a playful, visual way.

Students use Seesaw to document and reflect on their self-regulation strategies by capturing photos or short videos of their 'Calm Palm' activities, sharing with peers, and giving feedback on what helps them get calm independently.

Students record short personal podcasts where they share their strengths, describe strategies they use to stay calm, and explain how their choices affect their learning and friendships. Using Adobe Express Podcasting, they combine their voice recordings with simple sound effects to create engaging audio stories that reflect their self-awareness and resilience.

Students take photos of their self-portraits or 'Frames of Strength' drawings, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate them with their own voices, sharing affirmations or strategies they use to bounce forward and stay resilient.

Learners create a visual collage representing their personal strengths, calming strategies, and goals for Year 3, using photos, stickers, and text bubbles to make connections between their identity and how they interact with others.

plannerText: This unit engages Year 2 students in exploring self-awareness, strengths, and self-regulation strategies through activities like self-portraits, 'Calm Palm' exercises, personal goal setting, and peer reflection. Students investigate what helps them get calm, how to identify their needs, and how their choices and strengths influence their learning and social connections.

ci: Methods of communication have different features which impact their effectiveness
lo: The forms of verbal, non-verbal and visual communication; How the correct form enhances the effectiveness of a message; How people interpret messages and bring their own meaning to interactions

s.d: Students select a historical or modern communication device (e.g., Morse code, emoji, smartphone, hieroglyphics), draw or photograph it, and use ChatterPix Kids to animate the object explaining its features and how it impacts communication effectiveness. This brings non-verbal and visual forms of communication to life in a playful, memorable way.

Students design a digital timeline poster comparing different communication methods across history (e.g., hieroglyphics, letters, telephones, texting, emojis). They use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to create collages and annotate with icons, arrows, and text to highlight how features have changed and why some methods are more effective than others.

Students collaboratively create an interactive digital book showcasing various forms of communication (verbal, non-verbal, visual, digital). Each page features student-created examples, audio recordings, and reflections on how the features of each method affect its effectiveness for different audiences and purposes.

Small groups script and record short podcasts debating which communication method is most effective for a specific scenario (e.g., giving directions, expressing emotions, persuading someone). They edit in sound effects or music to reinforce the tone, then publish and share with the class for peer feedback.

Students design and run a Kahoot quiz for their peers, with questions focused on identifying features, strengths, and weaknesses of different communication methods. This reinforces understanding of the central idea and encourages critical thinking about effectiveness in real-world contexts.

plannerText: This unit explores the different forms and features of communication?verbal, non-verbal, and visual?and how these impact effectiveness. Students investigate historical and modern communication methods, experiment with signs, symbols, music, and body language, and reflect on how people interpret messages. Activities include silent movies, composing music, and exploring digital and non-digital communication platforms.

ci: Ethical decision making supports online choices.
lo: How choice of action and reaction affects others online; How informed choices can make a positive difference; Our right to feel safe online and the responsibility to make ethical decisions

s.d: Students create interactive 3D 'ethical dilemma cubes' using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app on iPads. Each face of the cube presents a different online scenario, and students record short video reflections or solutions using the iPad camera, then link these to the cube via QR codes. This tactile, AR-powered workflow makes abstract digital citizenship concepts visible and hands-on.

Students document their own digital media use at home and school by creating a multimedia journal in Seesaw. They reflect on ethical choices, annotate screenshots, and record voiceovers explaining their decision-making process in real-life online situations.

Students collaboratively design a digital 'Online Essential Agreement' poster using Canva, incorporating their own illustrations, icons, and slogans. They use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to add photos of themselves acting out safe and unsafe online behaviours.

Students collaborate in Explain Everything to create interactive presentations that explore ethical decision making online. They record their own scenarios demonstrating how choices affect others, annotate digital media examples, and co-construct an online essential agreement that highlights responsible and safe online behaviours.

Students co-create a Kahoot quiz on ethical online choices, using real scenarios from their own digital lives. They then run the quiz for another class, sparking discussion and peer teaching about responsible online behaviour.

plannerText: This unit focuses on ethical decision making to support safe and responsible online choices. Students investigate their own digital media use, co-create an online essential agreement, and engage in cyber safety projects and reflections, with strong emphasis on student agency and real-world application.

ci: Materials behave and interact in certain ways, which poses challenges and solutions
lo: The three states of matter and their properties; How matter changes states depending on particles, temperature and energy; Responsible and ethical use of materials like glass and plastic

s.d: Students use Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app to create interactive 3D models demonstrating the molecular structure of solids, liquids, and gases. They record short video explanations using the iPad camera, showing how particles behave and change state, then share these AR experiences with peers for feedback.

Students design an infographic in Canva that visually explains the process of changing states of matter (e.g., melting, freezing, evaporation). They use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to add photos of their own science experiments, making the infographic both personal and scientifically accurate.

Students document their scientific experiments on materials and states of matter by creating a digital science journal in Book Creator. They embed photos, voice recordings, and diagrams to explain their observations and conclusions, then publish their journals for the class to review.

Students use Explain Everything to create animated videos that model how materials change state. They draw particle diagrams, record their voices explaining each stage, and use the animation tools to visually show the movement and interaction of particles during changes like melting or condensation.

s.t: Minecraft: Filtering Water On The Iss
s.d: Students explore the 'Filtering Water On The ISS' Minecraft Education lesson to investigate how materials can be used to solve real-world challenges. They experiment with building and testing water filtration systems, connecting directly to the unit's focus on how materials behave and interact in different contexts.

plannerText: This unit explores the different states of matter, how and why matter changes, and the responsible use of materials. Students conduct hands-on experiments, document their observations, and investigate real-world applications such as water filtration and material adaptation. The unit emphasizes scientific inquiry, ethical use of materials, and student agency in designing and testing solutions.

ci: People can respond to natural disasters with resilience
lo: The causes and types of natural disasters (tectonic events, weather events); How natural disasters impact the environment and people; How humans respond with resilience and connection

s.d: Students create interactive 3D models of different types of natural disasters using Merge Cubes, then use the iPad camera to record their own voiceover narratives explaining the causes, impacts, and resilient responses to these disasters. These AR experiences can be shared with peers, allowing students to 'hold' a disaster in their hand and explore how communities respond and rebuild.

Students design digital infographics or animated posters in Canva that illustrate the steps communities take to prepare for, respond to, and recover from natural disasters, highlighting real-world examples of resilience and community action.

Students collaboratively author a digital class book documenting famous natural disasters, including survivor stories, scientific explanations, and resilience strategies, integrating photos, drawings, and audio reflections.

Students design and build simple 3D models of disaster-resilient homes or community shelters using Tinkercad, applying their understanding of natural disaster impacts and resilient features. They experiment with shapes and structures that could withstand floods, earthquakes, or bushfires, then share their designs digitally to explain how their creations help communities respond with resilience.

Students create short, impactful video PSAs in Adobe Express, educating their community about disaster preparedness and the importance of resilience, using a mix of student voice, animation, and real footage.

plannerText: This unit immerses students in understanding natural disasters, their causes, and impacts, with a strong focus on how people and communities respond with resilience. Students research different disaster types, analyze real-world case studies (such as Japan's earthquake preparedness), and engage in hands-on design challenges to create disaster-resistant solutions, culminating in creative projects that showcase their learning and action.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Many factors shape the development of cities.
lo: The essential features of a city; The function of a city; The development of cities through time

s.d: Students work in teams to redesign Melbourne for the future, considering how demographic, density, and social factors shape city development. They will build innovative city features, plan infrastructure, and reflect on how their designs address real-world challenges such as natural disasters, transport, and community needs. This immersive experience directly connects to the unit's focus on how cities adapt and change over time.

Students create interactive city planning infographics or posters that showcase the essential features and amenities of a functional city. They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to add themselves into city scenes, visually demonstrating their placement of key infrastructure and reflecting on their design choices.

Students collaboratively author a digital class book documenting their research on cities around the world, including maps, photos (using the iPad camera), and audio reflections. Each student can contribute a page about a city, highlighting unique features and how cities respond to challenges like natural disasters.

Students design and narrate animated city maps, demonstrating how transport systems, green spaces, and infrastructure interact. They can record their voices explaining the rationale for city layouts, and use drawing tools to show changes over time or responses to disasters.

Students build a collaborative digital wall to collect and compare city features, disaster responses, and architectural innovations from around the world. They can post images, videos, and reflections, then sort and group ideas to inform their own city designs.

plannerText: Students explore how cities develop and adapt over time, focusing on factors like demographics, density, transport, infrastructure, and disaster resilience. Activities include researching global cities, designing city layouts, building models, and reflecting on the needs and functions of urban environments.

ci: Health and wellbeing are impacted by the choices we make
lo: How our body systems connect and function; How our choices affect physical, emotional and mental wellbeing; Strategies to measure and maintain the health of the human body

s.d: Students create an interactive 3D model of a body system (e.g., digestive, circulatory) using Merge Cube, then app smash by embedding their own narrated explanations, fitness tips, and wellbeing reflections into a Book Creator digital book. This allows students to virtually 'hold' and explore their own health-focused content, making abstract systems tangible and personal.

Design a personalised 'Wellbeing Infographic' in Canva, where students visually map out their daily choices (food, exercise, sleep, screen time) and their impact on different aspects of health and wellbeing. Encourage students to use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to add photos of themselves making healthy choices.

Run a 'Wellbeing Vlog Challenge' where students document a week of health-related choices (meals, exercise, mindfulness, digital balance) using the iPad camera and Seesaw's annotation tools. They reflect on how their choices affect their mind and body, sharing insights with peers for feedback.

Students program Micro:bit devices to create interactive models that simulate body system functions, such as using sensors to track heart rate or steps, linking these measurements to choices affecting physical wellbeing. Learners collaborate to design simple wearable tech that displays data, helping them explore how their choices impact health and fitness in real time, making abstract concepts tangible and personal.

Create a 'Mind-Body Connection' multimedia poster in Adobe Express, combining student voice recordings, photos, and graphics to illustrate how exercise, nutrition, and friendships influence their wellbeing. Posters can be shared digitally with families to promote home-school connection.

plannerText: This unit explores how health and wellbeing are impacted by the choices students make, focusing on body systems, fitness, cyber safety, and personal responsibility. Students research and report on body systems, set and track fitness goals, and reflect on strategies for maintaining balanced wellbeing through collaborative and individual activities.

ci: The creative process involves understanding and applying artistic elements to communicate ideas and emotions across disciplines
lo: How colour, movement and music communicate character and mood; The creative process of making a multi-disciplinary presentation; The effort and commitment required to produce a live performance

s.d: Students design and create interactive 3D Beijing Opera masks or character artefacts using Merge Cube and the Merge Explorer app, then use the iPad camera to record themselves performing a short vignette in character. The 3D artefact becomes a digital prop, allowing students to blend physical movement, digital art, and AR storytelling in a single workflow.

Students create a digital portfolio showcasing their character mask designs, script excerpts, and reflections on how colour and artistic elements communicate emotion and personality, using Canva's visual storytelling tools and 'Remove Background' feature for mask photos.

Students collaboratively author a multimedia eBook documenting the creative process behind their Beijing Opera-inspired performances, including audio recordings, video snippets, and annotated images of their masks and poses.

Students compose original soundtracks for their character vignettes, experimenting with tempo, dynamics, and instrument selection to evoke mood and support the narrative, then sync their music to performance videos.

The teacher creates a Wise Discussion Chatbot using the Character Interview scenario and sets the bot's role as Ava, a community celebration organiser explaining why particular traditions matter to different families. The topic is ?How We Express Ourselves: The creative process involves understanding and applying artistic elements to communicate ideas and emotions across disciplines?. Students chat with the bot to explore a named perspective through guided questioning rather than general research; for example, they ask ?How does this celebration show what your community values?? and ?What might outsiders misunderstand about this tradition??. After the chat, students produce a question bank plus a short perspective-and-evidence summary that compares the chatbot responses with unit sources linked explicitly to How We Express Ourselves.

plannerText: This unit immerses students in the creative process across drama, music, visual arts, and Chinese, focusing on how artistic elements like colour, movement, and sound communicate ideas and emotions. Students design and perform original Beijing Opera-inspired vignettes, create expressive masks, compose music, and reflect on their creative choices through digital portfolios and collaborative projects.

ci: Not-for-profit organisations exist to respond to a variety of needs within our community
lo: The function and purpose of not-for-profit organisations; How not-for-profit organisations respond to community needs; Our responsibility to take action and make a difference

s.d: Students create interactive 3D models of not-for-profit organisations using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app. Each group designs a virtual 'cube exhibit' that visually explains the mission, impact, and structure of their chosen organisation, then shares these cubes with peers for an immersive, hands-on learning experience.

Launch a collaborative digital 'Action Wall' on Padlet where students post and comment on real-world examples of not-for-profit actions, fundraising ideas, and community needs. This living wall can include embedded videos, links, and student-created flyers, fostering ongoing discussion and idea sharing throughout the unit.

Students design professional-quality digital flyers and infographics in Canva to promote their chosen not-for-profit actions. They use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to create eye-catching visuals and then distribute these digitally within the school community to raise awareness and encourage participation.

Document the entire inquiry process on Seesaw by having students record video reflections, upload research snapshots, and share progress on their action projects. Families and peers can comment, providing authentic feedback and celebrating student agency in real time.

Students explore 'The Hall of Inventions' in Minecraft Education, then collaboratively design and build a virtual exhibit for a not-for-profit innovation that addresses a community need. They justify their invention's impact and connect it to real-world organisations. https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/challenges/the-hall-of-inventions

plannerText: Students investigate not-for-profit organisations, research their functions and community impact, and collaborate to design and promote action projects such as fundraising drives and awareness campaigns. They create digital flyers, posters, and presentations to inform and inspire the school community, culminating in student-led initiatives that support local and national causes.

ci: Engineers design and build structures using specific features and materials to make them strong, stable and fit for purpose
lo: Different types of bridges and their design features; How materials and engineering principles contribute to structural strength and stability; Testing and refining designs through experimentation

s.d: Students use Minecraft Education to collaboratively design and build a lunar base, applying engineering principles of strength, stability, and material selection. They must justify their design choices based on the unique challenges of building structures in space, such as gravity, available materials, and environmental hazards. This pushes students to think beyond Earth-based engineering and consider how engineers adapt to extreme environments.

Students digitally prototype their own bridge or building component using Tinkercad, experimenting with different shapes and materials. They can simulate stress tests within the software to see which designs are most stable and why, then share their models with peers for feedback.

Students program Sphero BOLT robots to navigate across model bridges they have constructed. They collect data on which bridge designs best support the robot's weight and movement, linking digital coding with physical engineering and iterative testing.

Students create a podcast series interviewing real-world engineers (or role-play as engineers) about the challenges of designing strong, stable structures. They discuss the importance of material choice, environmental factors, and innovative engineering solutions, then publish episodes to share with the school community.

Students use Explain Everything to create animated explanations of how different bridge types (beam, truss, suspension) distribute forces and why certain designs are chosen for specific environments. They can annotate, draw, and record voiceovers to demonstrate their understanding.

plannerText: Students investigate different types of bridges and structures, research engineering features that contribute to strength and stability, and work in teams to design, build, and test their own bridges using various materials. The unit emphasizes inquiry, hands-on prototyping, iterative improvement, and reflection on real-world engineering challenges.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students design a 3D interactive model of their bridge using Tinkercad, then export and visualize it on Merge Cubes using the Merge Explorer app on iPads. This allows students to 'hold' and inspect their bridge designs in augmented reality, testing stability and features virtually before building physically.","url":""},{"t":"Seesaw","d":"Students document their bridge design process by capturing photos and videos at each stage using the iPad camera, then create a digital portfolio in Seesaw. For presentations, they use Canva to create visually engaging posters explaining the engineering choices behind their bridge's strength and stability.","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students use Minecraft Education's 'Area and Volume' lesson to design and solve real-world area and volume problems related to bridge building, experimenting with different shapes and materials to understand how these factors impact structural integrity.","url":"https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/area-and-volume-2"},{"t":"Sphero BOLT","d":"Students program Sphero BOLT robots to simulate 'stress tests' on their bridge prototypes. By driving the Sphero across different bridge models, students collect data on which designs withstand movement and weight, linking digital coding with hands-on engineering.","url":""},{"t":"Book Creator","d":"Students create a digital book chronicling the history of famous bridges, the science behind their construction, and their own design journey. They document each iteration, collect data on load-bearing capacity, and present their final design with a focus on how engineering choices made their structure strong, stable, and fit for purpose.","url":""}]

ci: First Nations people have a deep connection to Country, language and culture
lo: The diversity of language and culture across First Nations communities in Australia; The significance of connection to Country as custodial responsibility; Listening to and learning from Indigenous perspectives

s.d: Students create interactive 3D artefacts using Merge Cubes and the iPad camera, overlaying their own First Nations-inspired symbols and stories. They record oral storytelling in their own voices, then use the Merge Explorer app to bring their artefacts to life, allowing peers to 'hold' and explore each other's stories in augmented reality.

Students design digital posters or infographics that compare and contrast different First Nations countries, languages, and cultural practices across Australia, using Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to integrate their own artwork or photos of their paintings.

Students produce short documentaries or creative retellings of Dreamtime stories, combining footage from their Melbourne Museum excursion, voiceovers, and digital artwork to reflect on the importance of Country and cultural diversity.

Students collaboratively author a digital book that weaves together personal reflections, research, and original artwork about First Nations perspectives on Country, language, and sustainability, embedding audio explanations and music soundscapes they have created.

Students build a collaborative digital map on Padlet, pinning locations of different First Nations countries, adding student-created videos, soundscapes, and artwork to each region to showcase the diversity and connection to Country.

plannerText: This unit explores First Nations Australians' deep connection to Country, language, and culture. Students engage in activities such as creating soundscapes, researching cultural diversity, visiting the Melbourne Museum, and producing artworks and digital reflections that highlight Indigenous perspectives, environmental responsibility, and the importance of tradition and identity.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students create interactive 3D artefacts using Merge Cubes and the iPad camera, overlaying their own First Nations-inspired symbols and stories. They record oral storytelling in their own voices, then use the Merge Explorer app to bring their artefacts to life, allowing peers to 'hold' and explore each other's stories in augmented reality.","url":""},{"t":"Canva","d":"Students design digital posters or infographics that compare and contrast different First Nations countries, languages, and cultural practices across Australia, using Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to integrate their own artwork or photos of their paintings.","url":""},{"t":"iMovie","d":"Students produce short documentaries or creative retellings of Dreamtime stories, combining footage from their Melbourne Museum excursion, voiceovers, and digital artwork to reflect on the importance of Country and cultural diversity.","url":""},{"t":"Book Creator","d":"Students collaboratively author a digital book that weaves together personal reflections, research, and original artwork about First Nations perspectives on Country, language, and sustainability, embedding audio explanations and music soundscapes they have created.","url":""},{"t":"Padlet","d":"Students build a collaborative digital map on Padlet, pinning locations of different First Nations countries, adding student-created videos, soundscapes, and artwork to each region to showcase the diversity and connection to Country.","url":""},{"t":"Lego Spike Prime","d":"Using the Design Cycle, students prototype and build a 'sustainable gathering tool' inspired by First Nations ingenuity, then document their process and test their designs for environmental impact and efficiency, reflecting on how traditional knowledge can inform modern STEM solutions.","url":""}]

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration and discovery have led to changes that shape the world we live in
lo: The age of exploration and the reasons explorers set out on their journeys; How Australia changed through European arrival; Different perspectives on the consequences of exploration

s.d: Students create interactive 3D explorer journals using Merge Cubes and the iPad camera. They film themselves as 'time-traveling explorers', overlaying their video narrations onto 3D models of ships, maps, or artifacts they design in Tinkercad, then use the Merge Cube to present their discoveries in augmented reality to the class.

Students document their explorer research by creating multimedia portfolios, combining voice recordings, annotated maps, and digital drawings to show the journeys, discoveries, and impacts of different explorers on Australia and the world.

Students design visually engaging digital timelines or infographics that compare pre- and post-European arrival in Australia, highlighting key explorers, their routes, and the resulting changes in landscape, demographics, and identity.

Students explore the museum of key innovations from history, then build their own 'explorer-inspired' inventions or landmarks in Minecraft, reflecting on how exploration and discovery have shaped the modern world.

Students create short documentary-style videos or animated explainers about a chosen explorer, using Adobe Express to combine historical images, maps, and their own voiceovers to communicate the positive and negative consequences of exploration.

plannerText: Students investigate the concept of exploration and discovery, focusing on the Age of Exploration and Australian history. They research explorers, map their journeys, present findings, and reflect on the impact of European arrival from multiple perspectives, using a range of inquiry-based and creative activities.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students create interactive 3D explorer journals using Merge Cubes and the iPad camera. They film themselves as 'time-traveling explorers', overlaying their video narrations onto 3D models of ships, maps, or artifacts they design in Tinkercad, then use the Merge Cube to present their discoveries in augmented reality to the class.","url":""},{"t":"Seesaw","d":"Students document their explorer research by creating multimedia portfolios, combining voice recordings, annotated maps, and digital drawings to show the journeys, discoveries, and impacts of different explorers on Australia and the world.","url":""},{"t":"Canva","d":"Students design visually engaging digital timelines or infographics that compare pre- and post-European arrival in Australia, highlighting key explorers, their routes, and the resulting changes in landscape, demographics, and identity.","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students explore the museum of key innovations from history, then build their own 'explorer-inspired' inventions or landmarks in Minecraft, reflecting on how exploration and discovery have shaped the modern world.","url":"https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/challenges/the-hall-of-inventions"},{"t":"Adobe Express","d":"Students create short documentary-style videos or animated explainers about a chosen explorer, using Adobe Express to combine historical images, maps, and their own voiceovers to communicate the positive and negative consequences of exploration.","url":""},{"t":"Lego Spike Prime","d":"Using the Design Cycle, students prototype and test a 'navigation robot' inspired by historical exploration tools. They design, build, and program a Lego Spike Prime robot to follow a mapped route representing an explorer's journey, iterating on their design to overcome obstacles and simulate real-world exploration challenges.","url":""}]

ci: Leaders can help a group to function effectively
lo: Roles and responsibilities of effective leadership; Purpose of leadership; The strengths and qualities of a leader

s.d: Students design and script interactive chatbots that simulate leadership scenarios, such as resolving team conflicts or making group decisions. Using Wise Discussion Chatbots, students can program branching dialogues that challenge peers to make choices and reflect on the qualities of effective leaders.

Students create digital leadership portfolios using Canva, showcasing their understanding of leadership qualities, reflections on famous leaders, and their own leadership journey. They can include infographics, timelines, and visual reflections on how leaders help groups function effectively.

Students film and edit short documentaries or role-play scenarios demonstrating leadership in action, such as leading a team project or resolving a classroom issue. iMovie allows them to add voiceovers, captions, and music to enhance their storytelling and analysis of leadership qualities.

Students collaboratively build a digital wall of 'Leadership Moments' using Padlet, posting examples from history, school, or their own lives. They can comment on each other's posts, discuss different perspectives, and identify patterns in effective leadership.

Students author interactive digital books that explore the attributes of effective leaders, including interviews, reflections, and case studies. Book Creator enables them to embed audio, video, and images, making their exploration of leadership engaging and multimodal.

plannerText: This unit explores the qualities and responsibilities of effective leaders, with students brainstorming attributes, analyzing real-world examples, and reflecting on their own leadership potential. Activities include collaborative discussions, leadership application tasks, and multimedia presentations, all aimed at understanding how leaders help groups function effectively.

ci: Social norms and stereotypes can influence the way people express themselves
lo: How stereotypes and social norms form and persist in society; The impact of discrimination, prejudice and assumptions on individuals and communities; How people overcome stereotypes to express themselves and inspire change

s.d: Students design 3D 'expression cubes' on Merge Cube, representing how social norms and stereotypes influence self-expression, then use Delightex to animate scenarios and challenge biases. This app smash allows learners to visualise and critique the impact of stereotypes, fostering empathy and creative problem-solving as they explore identity and self-expression. The interactive artefacts become conversation starters for class debates and personal reflection.

Students research a famous figure who overcame stereotypes and script a short podcast episode sharing that person?s story and impact. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), they record and edit their narration, adding sound effects or music to enhance their message about overcoming social norms and inspiring change.

Students collaboratively author a digital book that explores different forms of self-expression and the influence of social norms, including personal stories, artwork, and research on stereotypes. Each student contributes a page, using multimedia elements to enhance engagement.

Students engage in a Socratic Tutor scenario using a teacher-created Wise Discussion Chatbot playing 'Ava Patel, a social psychologist specialising in stereotypes'. Students ask questions such as 'How do stereotypes form in society?' or 'What strategies can help challenge assumptions?' After the chat, students summarise their key insights in a reflection table, highlighting how their thinking about self-expression has changed.

Guide students to create dynamic infographics or short video stories that visually unpack a stereotype or social norm, using Adobe Express's animation and design features to communicate powerful messages about overcoming prejudice.

plannerText: This unit engages Year 5 students in understanding how social norms and stereotypes influence self-expression. Through activities such as gallery walks, scenario analysis, research on famous figures, and multimedia presentations, students critically examine and creatively respond to issues of prejudice and identity, culminating in both digital and physical expressions of learning.

ci: Goods and services are exchanged within a marketplace
lo: The production of goods from raw materials through manufacturing to consumer; The exchange of currency for goods and services in a functioning economy; Ethics and responsible decision-making in the marketplace

s.d: Transform your classroom into a virtual marketplace using Merge Cubes and iPads. Students design 3D models of their own products or services, then use Merge Explorer to create interactive, augmented reality 'stalls' where peers can explore, compare, and 'purchase' goods/services in a simulated digital market. This brings the concept of exchange and value to life in a futuristic, hands-on way.

Students create and distribute digital market surveys using Microsoft Forms to collect real-time data on classmates' consumer preferences, then analyse the results to inform their business decisions and pricing strategies in the class marketplace simulation.

Students create and manage their own digital budgets and sales records using Microsoft Excel, entering data on goods produced, prices, and sales to track their business performance. They apply formulas to calculate profits and expenses, gaining hands-on experience with currency exchange and responsible financial decision-making within their simulated marketplace.

Students design visually engaging digital posters and infographics in Canva to promote their goods or services, focusing on persuasive marketing techniques and ethical advertising within the classroom economy.

Students explore 'The Hall of Inventions' in Minecraft Education, then design and build their own innovative marketplace inventions in the virtual world, reflecting on how goods and services have evolved and are exchanged over time.

plannerText: Students investigate how goods and services are exchanged within a marketplace by simulating a class economy, creating their own businesses, applying for jobs, managing budgets with spreadsheets, and engaging in trading sessions. They explore concepts such as supply and demand, ethics, and consumer rights through collaborative projects, role play, and digital tools, culminating in a hands-on, student-driven marketplace experience.
